> - **Topic:** Introduction to Data Warehousing: Overview of Data Warehousing, Need, Definition, Characteristics, Benefits, Operational vs. Analytical Systems, Applications in Industry. Architecture of Data Warehousing, Data Sources, Staging Area, Data Marts, ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) Processes, Components of a Data Warehouse (Data Models, OLAP, Metadata)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Data Warehouse (KTU Syllabus Standard)

According to **Bill Inmon (the "Father of Data Warehousing")**, a Data Warehouse is formally defined as:

> *"A Data Warehouse is a **subject-oriented, integrated, time-variant, and non-volatile** collection of data in support of management's decision-making process."*

This is the canonical KTU definition and is a **guaranteed 5-mark question** in every KTU End Semester Examination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Memorize the exact Inmon definition word-for-word. Partial credit is awarded for each of the four keywords: *Subject-Oriented, Integrated, Time-Variant, Non-Volatile*.

## 1.2 The Four Defining Characteristics (Inmon's Pillars)

| # | Characteristic | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Subject-Oriented** | Organized around major business subjects (e.g., Sales, Finance, Inventory) rather than operational applications. |
| 2 | **Integrated** | Consolidates data from multiple heterogeneous sources with consistent naming, units, and encoding. |
| 3 | **Time-Variant** | Data is historical (5–10 years), time-stamped, and never updated in real-time. |
| 4 | **Non-Volatile** | Once loaded, data is **never deleted or modified** — only appended or refreshed. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library vs. Newsroom" Model

Imagine a **newspaper office (Operational System)** versus a **national library archive (Data Warehouse)**:

- **Newspaper Office (OLTP)**: Deals with hundreds of small, daily transactions — *"Who bought today's paper?"* Fast inserts, updates, and deletes. Data changes every second.
- **National Library (Data Warehouse)**: Stores decades of newspapers, encyclopedias, and journals. You can **read old data**, but you cannot erase history. Queries are heavy and analytical: *"How did India's GDP trend over 30 years?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** A Data Warehouse is essentially a **read-only historical archive** built for analysis, while operational systems are **write-heavy transactional engines** built for day-to-day operations.

## 1.4 Why Do We Need a Data Warehouse? (The Need)

Operational databases (OLTP) **cannot** answer strategic business questions because:

1. **Data Silos**: Sales, HR, and Finance use different systems — no unified view.
2. **Query Performance**: Complex analytical queries on OLTP slow down transactions.
3. **Historical Absence**: OLTP typically retains only 30–90 days of data.
4. **Inconsistent Data**: Different departments define "Customer" differently.
5. **Decision Support Gap**: Executives need trends, forecasts, and KPIs — not row-level transactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A Data Warehouse solves the **"Information Gap"** between operational data stores and strategic decision-making needs.

## 1.5 Operational vs. Analytical Systems (OLTP vs. OLAP)

| Parameter | Operational (OLTP) | Analytical (OLAP/Data Warehouse) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Day-to-day operations | Strategic decision support |
| **Data Type** | Current, detailed | Historical, summarized |
| **Queries** | Simple, frequent | Complex, infrequent |
| **Operations** | INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE | Mostly SELECT (read-only) |
| **Users** | Clerks, front-line staff | Managers, analysts, executives |
| **Response Time** | Milliseconds | Seconds to minutes |
| **Size** | MB to GB | GB to TB to PB |
| **Schema** | Normalized (3NF) | Denormalized (Star/Snowflake) |
| **Metric** | Transaction throughput | Query throughput |
| **Backup** | Critical, continuous | Archival, periodic |

## 1.6 Applications of Data Warehousing in Industry

| Industry | Real-World DW Application |
|---|---|
| **Retail (Amazon, Walmart)** | Customer purchase pattern analysis, recommendation engines |
| **Banking (HDFC, SBI)** | Credit risk analysis, fraud detection, customer segmentation |
| **Healthcare (Apollo)** | Patient outcome analysis, disease trend monitoring |
| **Telecom (Jio, Airtel)** | Churn prediction, network optimization |
| **E-Commerce (Flipkart)** | Market basket analysis, dynamic pricing |
| **Manufacturing (Tata Steel)** | Supply chain optimization, predictive maintenance |
| **Education (KTU itself)** | Student performance analytics, accreditation reporting |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Data Warehouse Architecture — The Layered Model

A modern Data Warehouse follows a **multi-tiered architecture**:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         TOP TIER: Front-End Tools             │
│   (OLAP, Reporting, BI, Data Mining, EIS)     │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│         MIDDLE TIER: OLAP Server             │
│   (ROLAP / MOLAP / HOLAP Engine)             │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│       CORE TIER: Data Warehouse DBMS         │
│   (Star/Snowflake Schema, Fact Tables)       │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│       BOTTOM TIER: ETL + Staging Area        │
│   (Extract → Transform → Load Process)       │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│         DATA SOURCES                         │
│   (OLTP DBs, Flat Files, ERP, CRM, APIs)     │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 2.2 The ETL Process — Step-by-Step

ETL is the **heart** of any Data Warehouse. It is the engine that populates the warehouse from raw sources.

### Step 1: EXTRACTION
- Data is pulled from heterogeneous source systems (RDBMS, ERP, CRM, flat files, APIs, web logs).
- **Challenge**: Different formats, time zones, encoding standards.
- **Technique**: *Full Extraction* vs. *Incremental Extraction* (using CDC — Change Data Capture).

### Step 2: TRANSFORMATION
Raw data is cleaned, standardized, and enriched. Common transformations include:

- **Data Cleansing**: Removing duplicates, handling NULLs.
- **Data Standardization**: Currency conversion (USD → INR), unit conversion.
- **Data Integration**: Reconciling schema mismatches (e.g., `Sex` vs. `Gender` vs. `M/F`).
- **Data Aggregation**: Pre-computing daily totals from transaction logs.
- **Deduplication**: Removing redundant records using surrogate keys.

### Step 3: LOADING
Transformed data is loaded into the Data Warehouse.

| Loading Type | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Initial Load** | One-time bulk population of entire warehouse | First-time deployment |
| **Incremental Load** | Only new/changed data appended | Periodic refresh (daily/hourly) |
| **Full Refresh** | Warehouse rebuilt from scratch | Monthly/yearly maintenance |
| **Delta Load** | Captures CDC deltas | Real-time/near-real-time DW |

## 2.3 Components of a Data Warehouse

### 2.3.1 Data Models
- **Conceptual Model**: High-level business entities (e.g., Entity-Relationship diagrams).
- **Logical Model**: Schema design in Star or Snowflake form.
- **Physical Model**: Actual DBMS implementation with indexes, partitions.

### 2.3.2 OLAP (Online Analytical Processing)
OLAP is the analytical engine on top of a Data Warehouse.

| OLAP Type | Full Form | Storage | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ROLAP** | Relational OLAP | Relational DB (star schema) | Large datasets, dynamic aggregation |
| **MOLAP** | Multidimensional OLAP | Proprietary multidimensional cubes | Fast slice-dice, small-to-medium data |
| **HOLAP** | Hybrid OLAP | Mix of relational + cube storage | Balanced performance |

**OLAP Operations:**
- **Roll-up**: Aggregate data (City → Country)
- **Drill-down**: Disaggregate data (Year → Quarter → Month)
- **Slice**: Fix one dimension (Sales for "2024" only)
- **Dice**: Fix multiple dimensions (Sales in "Kerala" during "Diwali")
- **Pivot**: Rotate the cube axes for alternate views

### 2.3.3 Metadata
Metadata is **"data about data"** — the self-describing intelligence layer of a Data Warehouse.

| Metadata Type | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Business Metadata** | KPIs, ownership, business rules |
| **Technical Metadata** | Table names, column types, ETL job logs |
| **Process Metadata** | Load timestamps, row counts, error logs |
| **Operational Metadata** | Query statistics, user access logs |

> [!NOTE]
> Without metadata, a Data Warehouse is just a "data jail" — data is stored but cannot be discovered or trusted.

## 2.4 Data Marts

A **Data Mart** is a *subset* of a Data Warehouse focused on a single subject area or department.

| Parameter | Data Warehouse | Data Mart |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Enterprise-wide | Department-specific |
| **Data Volume** | TB to PB | GB to TB |
| **Users** | Organization-wide | Departmental |
| **Source** | Multiple integrated sources | Single DW or subset |
| **Cost** | High | Low |
| **Implementation Time** | Months to years | Weeks to months |

**Three Types of Data Marts:**
1. **Dependent Data Mart**: Sourced from the central Data Warehouse.
2. **Independent Data Mart**: Sourced directly from operational systems (no central DW).
3. **Hybrid Data Mart**: Combines both approaches.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While Data Warehousing is largely conceptual, the following quantitative metrics are frequently tested:

| Metric | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Compression Ratio (CR)** | $CR = \frac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Query Response Time (QRT)** | $QRT = T_{\text{end}} - T_{\text{start}}$ | Seconds (s) |
| **ETL Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Records Loaded}}{\text{ETL Duration}}$ | Rows/second |
| **Storage Growth Rate** | $\text{GR} = \frac{\text{Current Size} - \text{Previous Size}}{\text{Previous Size}} \times 100$ | % per period |
| **Data Quality Score (DQS)** | $DQS = \frac{\text{Valid Records}}{\text{Total Records}} \times 100$ | Percentage (0–100) |
| **Star Schema Cardinality** | $\text{Rows} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert D_i \vert \text{ (for cube)}$ | Number of cells |
| **Dimensional Cardinality** | $\text{Total Cells} = \vert \text{Facts} \vert \times \prod_{j=1}^{m} \vert \text{Dim}_j \vert$ | Cube cells |

## 2.6 Benefits of Data Warehousing

1. **Enhanced Business Intelligence**: Single version of truth for KPIs.
2. **Improved Decision Making**: Historical trend analysis enables forecasting.
3. **Increased Query Performance**: Pre-aggregated, indexed data accelerates analytics.
4. **Data Quality & Consistency**: Centralized cleansing enforces standards.
5. **Historical Preservation**: Time-variant design retains 5–10+ years of data.
6. **ROI on Data Assets**: Transforms raw operational data into strategic insight.
7. **Regulatory Compliance**: Easier auditing (SOX, GDPR, RBI norms).
8. **Customer 360° View**: Unified view across touchpoints (CRM, web, support).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Process Derivations

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The ETL Data Flow Pipeline

Let us analytically derive the total time taken for a complete ETL cycle in a Data Warehouse.

**Let:**
- $N$ = Total number of records to be processed
- $E$ = Extraction rate (records/second)
- $T_r$ = Transformation rate (records/second)
- $L$ = Loading rate (records/second)

**Step 1: Time for Extraction**
The extraction phase processes all $N$ records at rate $E$:

$$T_{\text{extract}} = \frac{N}{E}$$

**Step 2: Time for Transformation**
Transformation operates on the extracted dataset:

$$T_{\text{transform}} = \frac{N}{T_r}$$

**Step 3: Time for Loading**
Loading writes to the target warehouse:

$$T_{\text{load}} = \frac{N}{L}$$

**Step 4: Total ETL Time (Sequential Pipeline)**
Assuming a strictly sequential pipeline:

$$T_{\text{ETL,total}} = T_{\text{extract}} + T_{\text{transform}} + T_{\text{load}}$$

Substituting:

$$T_{\text{ETL,total}} = \frac{N}{E} + \frac{N}{T_r} + \frac{N}{L}$$

Factoring out $N$:

$$T_{\text{ETL,total}} = N \left( \frac{1}{E} + \frac{1}{T_r} + \frac{1}{L} \right)$$

**Step 5: Bottleneck Identification**
The slowest stage (smallest rate) dominates. The bottleneck rate $R_{\min}$ is:

$$R_{\min} = \min(E,\ T_r,\ L)$$

Thus the theoretical minimum ETL time is:

$$T_{\min} = \frac{N}{R_{\min}}$$

**Step 6: Parallel Optimization Gain**
If we parallelize transformation across $k$ workers:

$$T_{\text{transform,parallel}} = \frac{N}{k \cdot T_r}$$

The percentage improvement is:

$$\text{Gain} (\%) = \left(1 - \frac{T_{\text{transform,parallel}}}{T_{\text{transform}}} \right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{k}\right) \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** Amazon's Redshift, Snowflake, and Google BigQuery all implement such parallelized ETL pipelines to process petabytes daily.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Star Schema vs. Snowflake Schema

| Criterion | Star Schema | Snowflake Schema |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Central fact table surrounded by denormalized dimension tables | Central fact table with normalized dimension tables (sub-dimensions) |
| **Normalization** | Denormalized (1NF only) | Normalized (3NF) |
| **Number of Tables** | Fewer (1 fact + N dimensions) | More (1 fact + N dimensions + sub-dimensions) |
| **Query Performance** | Faster (fewer joins) | Slower (more joins) |
| **Storage Space** | Higher (redundancy) | Lower (no redundancy) |
| **Maintenance** | Easier | Complex |
| **Data Integrity** | Lower | Higher (no update anomalies) |
| **Use Case** | OLAP cubes, BI dashboards | Complex hierarchies, large DWs |
| **KTU Preference** | **Most commonly asked** | Asked as contrast |

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Data Warehouse Storage Estimation

For a Star Schema with one fact table and $m$ dimension tables:

**Step 1: Fact Table Size**
If the fact table has $F$ rows and each row is $S_f$ bytes:

$$\text{Size}_{\text{fact}} = F \times S_f$$

**Step 2: Dimension Table Size**
For the $j$-th dimension with $D_j$ rows and $S_{d_j}$ bytes per row:

$$\text{Size}_{\text{dimension}_j} = D_j \times S_{d_j}$$

**Step 3: Total Storage**

$$\text{Size}_{\text{total}} = F \times S_f + \sum_{j=1}^{m} D_j \times S_{d_j}$$

**Step 4: Index Overhead (typically +20% to +40%)**

$$\text{Size}_{\text{with\_index}} = \text{Size}_{\text{total}} \times 1.30$$

**Step 5: 5-Year Retention Multiplier**
With annual growth rate $g$ compounded over $n$ years:

$$\text{Size}_{n\text{-year}} = \text{Size}_{\text{with\_index}} \times \frac{(1+g)^n - 1}{g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** This formula is used by DBAs at Infosys, TCS, and Wipro to forecast storage procurement budgets for enterprise Data Warehouses.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Process: Building a Data Warehouse

A KTU-favored 14-mark question asks for "Explain the steps to design a Data Warehouse." Here is the complete analytical model:

**Step 1: Requirements Gathering**
- Identify business processes (Sales, Inventory, HR).
- Define KPIs and analytical questions.
- Identify user personas (analysts, executives).

**Step 2: Source System Analysis**
- Catalog all operational systems (ERP, CRM, legacy DBs).
- Profile data: volume, velocity, variety, quality.
- Map data lineage.

**Step 3: Conceptual Modeling**
- Identify **grain** (lowest level of detail in fact table).
- Identify dimensions (Who, What, When, Where, Why).
- Identify measures (sales amount, quantity, profit).

**Step 4: Logical Modeling**
- Design Star or Snowflake schema.
- Define surrogate keys for dimensions.
- Apply Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD) strategy.

**Step 5: Physical Modeling**
- Choose DBMS (e.g., Snowflake, Redshift, Teradata).
- Define partitioning, indexing, compression.
- Estimate storage using the formula in Section 3.3.

**Step 6: ETL Development**
- Build extraction jobs (SQL, Python, Informatica).
- Implement transformation logic.
- Schedule incremental loads.

**Step 7: OLAP Cube Construction**
- Pre-aggregate measures along dimension hierarchies.
- Choose ROLAP/MOLAP/HOLAP.

**Step 8: Front-End & BI Tools**
- Connect Tableau, Power BI, Looker.
- Build dashboards and reports.

**Step 9: Testing & Validation**
- Data quality checks (completeness, accuracy, consistency).
- User acceptance testing (UAT).

**Step 10: Deployment & Maintenance**
- Go-live with monitoring.
- Periodic archival, purging, and tuning.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison: Three-Tier vs. Two-Tier Architecture

| Aspect | Three-Tier (with Staging) | Two-Tier (Direct Load) |
|---|---|---|
| **Layers** | Source → Staging → DW | Source → DW (direct) |
| **Performance** | Slower (extra hop) but safer | Faster but riskier |
| **Recovery** | Easy (replay from staging) | Hard (must re-extract) |
| **Data Quality** | High (staging validates) | Moderate |
| **Cost** | Higher storage | Lower storage |
| **KTU Preference** | **Recommended answer** | Less common in exams |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Data Warehouse. List any four characteristics. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A Data Warehouse is a **subject-oriented, integrated, time-variant, and non-volatile** collection of data organized to support management decision-making (W.H. Inmon, 1996).

**Four Characteristics (1 mark — 0.25 each):**
1. **Subject-Oriented** — organized by business subject (Sales, Finance).
2. **Integrated** — unified data from heterogeneous sources.
3. **Time-Variant** — preserves historical data (5–10 years).
4. **Non-Volatile** — data is stable; only loads and reads, no updates.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between OLTP and OLAP systems. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | OLTP | OLAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Transaction processing | Analytical processing |
| **Operations** | INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE | Mostly SELECT |
| **Data** | Current, detailed | Historical, summarized |
| **Schema** | Normalized (3NF) | Denormalized (Star) |
| **Users** | Operational staff | Analysts, managers |
| **Response Time** | Milliseconds | Seconds to minutes |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A: Explain the Three-Tier Architecture of a Data Warehouse in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of ETL, Staging Area, and Metadata. **[14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### Part (a) — Three-Tier Architecture [7 Marks]

**1. Bottom Tier — Data Sources & Staging (3 marks)**

- **Data Sources** include operational databases (OLTP), ERP systems (SAP), CRM platforms (Salesforce), flat files (CSV, XML), web logs, and external APIs.
- **Staging Area** is an intermediate, transient storage zone where raw data is temporarily parked after extraction and before transformation.
- **Purpose of Staging:**
  - Decouples source systems from the warehouse (source independence).
  - Allows data validation, profiling, and audit logging.
  - Provides recovery point in case of ETL failures (replay capability).
  - Reduces load on operational systems.

**2. Middle Tier — Data Warehouse DBMS & OLAP Server (2 marks)**

- Contains the **Data Warehouse database** (central repository) and the **OLAP Server** (ROLAP/MOLAP/HOLAP).
- Data is stored in **Star Schema** or **Snowflake Schema** with fact and dimension tables.
- The OLAP server handles complex multi-dimensional queries (slice, dice, drill-down, roll-up).

**3. Top Tier — Front-End BI Tools (2 marks)**

- Tools like **Tableau, Power BI, Looker, QlikView** connect to the warehouse.
- Provides **dashboards, ad-hoc query, EIS (Executive Information Systems), and data mining**.

---

#### Part (b) — ETL, Staging, and Metadata Roles [7 Marks]

**1. Extract (2 marks)**
- Pulls raw data from sources using **JDBC/ODBC connectors, APIs, flat-file readers**.
- Two modes: **Full Extraction** (entire source every load) and **Incremental Extraction** (only deltas, via CDC — Change Data Capture).

**2. Transform (2.5 marks)**
- **Cleansing:** NULL handling, deduplication, outlier removal.
- **Standardization:** Date format, currency conversion, unit normalization.
- **Enrichment:** Deriving new columns (e.g., `Age` from `DOB`).
- **Integration:** Reconciling schema differences (`Gender` vs `Sex`).
- **Aggregation:** Pre-computing totals (e.g., monthly sales from daily rows).

**3. Load (1.5 marks)**
- Inserts transformed data into warehouse using **Bulk Loaders, INSERT...SELECT, COPY commands**.
- Strategies: **Initial, Incremental, Full Refresh, Delta Load**.
- Post-load: indexes rebuilt, statistics updated, constraints validated.

**4. Metadata (1 mark)**
- The **"DNA of the Data Warehouse"** — descriptive information about data.
- Types: Business, Technical, Process, Operational.
- Stored in a **Metadata Repository** (e.g., Apache Atlas, AWS Glue Data Catalog).

---

### Question B (Alternative): Define Data Warehouse. Explain its characteristics, benefits, and components in detail. Compare Data Warehouse with Data Mart. **[14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

#### Part (a) — Definition, Characteristics & Benefits [7 Marks]

**1. Definition (1.5 marks)**
Per Inmon: A Data Warehouse is a **subject-oriented, integrated, time-variant, non-volatile** collection of data supporting management decisions.

**2. Characteristics (3 marks)**
- **Subject-Oriented**: Built around subjects (Customer, Product, Sales), not applications.
- **Integrated**: Resolves conflicts in naming, units, codes across sources.
- **Time-Variant**: Stores 5–10+ years of historical data with time-stamps.
- **Non-Volatile**: Read-only; data is appended or refreshed, never modified by users.
- (Bonus mention: Atomic + Summary data)

**3. Benefits (2.5 marks)**
- Strategic decision support via trend analysis.
- Single version of truth across the enterprise.
- High query performance via pre-aggregation.
- Data quality enforcement and consistency.
- Historical data preservation enables forecasting and what-if analysis.
- Regulatory compliance and audit trail.

---

#### Part (b) — Components & DW vs. Data Mart [7 Marks]

**1. Components of a Data Warehouse (4 marks)**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Data Sources** | OLTP, ERP, CRM, files, web, IoT |
| **Staging Area** | Temporary buffer for raw extracted data |
| **ETL Engine** | Extracts, transforms, and loads data |
| **Data Warehouse DB** | Central repository (Star/Snowflake schema) |
| **Data Marts** | Departmental subsets (Sales, HR, Finance) |
| **OLAP Server** | ROLAP/MOLAP/HOLAP analytical engine |
| **Metadata Repository** | Data about data (technical + business) |
| **Front-End Tools** | BI, reporting, dashboards, data mining |

**2. Data Warehouse vs. Data Mart (3 marks)**

| Parameter | Data Warehouse | Data Mart |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Enterprise-wide | Single department |
| **Size** | TB to PB | GB to TB |
| **Data Source** | Multiple integrated sources | Subset of DW (or independent) |
| **Implementation Time** | 6–24 months | 1–6 months |
| **Cost** | Very High (crores) | Low (lakhs) |
| **Users** | Entire organization | Departmental users |
| **Subject** | Multiple subjects | Single subject area |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse "Data Warehouse" with "Database." A DW is **analytical + historical + read-mostly**; a DB is **transactional + current + read-write**.
> - **Do NOT** define ETL in wrong order. The correct order is **Extract → Transform → Load** (not "Load Transform Extract"). Reversing the order costs 2 marks.
> - **Do NOT** write vague answers like *"Data Warehouse stores data."* Always include the **four keywords** of Inmon's definition.
> - **Do NOT** forget units/numbers in storage estimation problems — examiners deduct 1 mark for missing "GB/TB" units.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorize Points for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1**

- **Inmon Definition:** Subject-Oriented, Integrated, Time-Variant, Non-Volatile. *(4 keywords = 4 marks guaranteed.)*
- **OLTP vs. OLAP:** OLTP = transactional, OLAP = analytical.
- **ETL Order:** Extract → Transform → Load. Always in this sequence.
- **Data Warehouse = Enterprise-wide, Data Mart = Departmental.**
- **Star Schema = Denormalized (faster queries), Snowflake = Normalized (less redundancy).**
- **OLAP Types:** ROLAP, MOLAP, HOLAP. Know one-line definitions.
- **OLAP Operations:** Roll-up, Drill-down, Slice, Dice, Pivot. *(Expect a 7-mark question asking to explain any 4.)*
- **Metadata = Data about Data.** Four types: Business, Technical, Process, Operational.
- **Three-Tier Architecture:** Sources/Staging → DW/OLAP → Front-End BI.
- **Data Mart Types:** Dependent, Independent, Hybrid.
- **Applications:** Retail, Banking, Healthcare, Telecom, E-Commerce, Manufacturing.
- **Benefits:** Strategic decisions, data quality, historical preservation, ROI, compliance.
- **Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD):** Type 1 (overwrite), Type 2 (new row), Type 3 (new column). *(Often asked as bonus.)*
- **Storage Estimation Formula:** $\text{Size}_{\text{total}} = F \times S_f + \sum D_j \times S_{d_j}$.
- **ETL Bottleneck:** Slowest stage dominates; use parallelization with gain $\left(1 - \frac{1}{k}\right) \times 100$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Warehouse Models: Enterprise Data Warehouse, Virtual Warehouse, Data Mart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT412 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Warehouse Models: Enterprise Data Warehouse, Virtual Warehouse, Data Mart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Data Warehouse Models — Formal Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** (PEAIT412 — Data Warehousing, Module 1), *Data Warehouse Models* refers to the **architectural blueprints** that determine **how data is organized, integrated, scoped, and delivered** to end users across an organization. A *model* (or *architecture*) is the logical and physical plan that defines the **scope, structure, users, and technology footprint** of the warehouse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Adopted from Bill Inmon & Ralph Kimball Frameworks)**
> A *Data Warehouse Model* is a **centralized or distributed repository architecture** that supports **analytical reporting, structured and/or ad-hoc queries, and strategic decision-making**, by integrating data from heterogeneous operational sources.

The three canonical models prescribed in the KTU Module 1 syllabus are:

1. **Enterprise Data Warehouse (EDW)** — Organization-wide, centralized.
2. **Data Mart (DM)** — Department/subject-area scoped, decentralized.
3. **Virtual Warehouse (VW)** — A *logical view* over distributed data sources with **no persistent physical storage**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Library Analogy (to make it unforgettable)**
> 
> Imagine a **massive Central National Library** (Enterprise Data Warehouse) that stores every book across every subject in one giant building. It takes years to build, costs a fortune, and serves scholars nationwide.
> 
> Now imagine **small departmental libraries inside a University** (Data Marts) — the Engineering Department has its own library with only Mechanical books, the Medical Department has only medical journals. They are fast, cheap, and focused.
> 
> Finally, the **Virtual Library** is like a **Google Search portal** — it does **not own a single book**. It simply gives you a *virtual shelf* by pulling titles, in real-time, from many different libraries across the world. The moment you close the portal, the "library" disappears.

This analogy maps directly onto the KTU syllabus triad.

---

## 1.3 Enterprise Data Warehouse (EDW) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Enterprise Data Warehouse (EDW)**
> An **organization-wide, centralized, subject-oriented, integrated, time-variant, and non-volatile** collection of data (the classic Inmon definition) that supports **enterprise-wide decision-making** across all departments, business units, and geographies.

**Core Properties:**

| Property | Meaning in EDW Context |
|----------|------------------------|
| **Scope** | Entire enterprise (pan-departmental) |
| **Data Volume** | **Tens to hundreds of Terabytes** (often **Petascale** in modern systems) |
| **Users** | **Hundreds to thousands** of concurrent analysts |
| **Latency** | Typically **batch-loaded** (daily / weekly / monthly) |
| **Source Coverage** | **All operational systems** — ERP, CRM, SCM, HR, Finance |
| **Standardization** | A single **corporate-wide data model** |
| **Implementation Time** | **18 months to 3+ years** |
| **Cost** | Very High — typically **multi-million dollar** investments |

---

## 1.4 Data Mart (DM) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Mart**
> A **subset of the data warehouse** that is **scoped to a single subject area, department, or business function** (e.g., Sales, Marketing, Finance, Inventory). It is **subject-oriented, smaller, faster to deploy, and typically owned by a single business unit**.

**Core Properties:**

| Property | Meaning in Data Mart Context |
|----------|------------------------------|
| **Scope** | **Single department** or business process |
| **Data Volume** | **Gigabytes to a few Terabytes** |
| **Users** | **Tens to a few hundred** analysts |
| **Latency** | Often **near-real-time** or daily refresh |
| **Source Coverage** | A **subset** of operational data + (optionally) EDW |
| **Standardization** | Department-specific schema |
| **Implementation Time** | **Weeks to a few months** |
| **Cost** | Low to moderate |

**Two Architectural Variants of Data Marts (KTU Mandatory):**

- **Dependent Data Mart** — Sourced **directly from the EDW** (top-down approach of Inmon).
- **Independent Data Mart** — Sourced **directly from operational systems**, *without* an EDW (bottom-up approach of Kimball).

---

## 1.5 Virtual Warehouse (VW) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Virtual Warehouse**
> A **logical, on-demand, read-only view** created over **distributed, heterogeneous, and physically separate data sources**. It contains **no persistent physical data** of its own — queries are translated and pushed down to the underlying source systems at execution time.

**Core Properties:**

| Property | Meaning in Virtual Warehouse Context |
|----------|----------------------------------------|
| **Scope** | Variable — depends on the view definition |
| **Data Volume** | **Zero persistent storage** |
| **Users** | Ad-hoc analysts and executives |
| **Latency** | **Real-time** (queries hit live sources) |
| **Source Coverage** | Federated — multiple heterogeneous DBs |
| **Standardization** | Logical schema only (mediated via middleware) |
| **Implementation Time** | **Days to weeks** |
| **Cost** | Lowest (no ETL infrastructure) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** A Virtual Warehouse is sometimes also called a **"Data Warehouse without a Warehouse"** because it is *just a query layer* — it has **no physical tables, no ETL jobs, and no data movement**.

---

## 1.6 High-Yield Quick-Reference Matrix (Introductory Snapshot)

| Parameter | EDW | Data Mart | Virtual Warehouse |
|-----------|-----|-----------|-------------------|
| **Architecture Style** | Centralized | Decentralized | Federated / Logical |
| **Storage** | Physical, large | Physical, small | **None (logical only)** |
| **Subject Coverage** | Enterprise-wide | Single subject | Cross-subject (logical) |
| **Latency** | Batch (high) | Near-real-time | Real-time |
| **Implementation Cost** | Very High | Low–Medium | **Lowest** |
| **Implementation Time** | Years | Months | Weeks |
| **Data Movement** | Heavy ETL | Light ETL | **No movement** |
| **Best For** | Strategic, cross-functional BI | Tactical, departmental BI | **Ad-hoc / exploratory** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind the Three Models — KTU Analytical Reasoning

KTU examiners expect students to articulate **why** three models exist rather than one. The underlying drivers are:

### 2.1.1 The Enterprise Data Warehouse (EDW) — Top-Down Philosophy

**Bill Inmon's Argument (Father of DW):**
> *"The data warehouse is a subject-oriented, integrated, time-variant, non-volatile collection of data in support of management's decision-making process."*

Operational Logic Steps:

1. **Start with the Enterprise.** Capture *every* business process across *every* department.
2. **Normalize the Data Model.** Use **3NF (Third Normal Form)** to eliminate redundancy and ensure atomicity.
3. **Build Conformance.** A single **Corporate Information Factory (CIF)** becomes the trusted source.
4. **Derive Subject-Area Data Marts.** Once EDW is stable, dependent data marts pull slices from it.
5. **Serve Strategic Reporting.** Cross-functional OLAP, executive dashboards, regulatory reporting.

**The "Why":**
- Guarantees **one version of truth** across the enterprise.
- Eliminates **data silos** and conflicting KPIs.
- Provides **historical depth** (10+ years) for trend analysis.

**The "How" — Inmon's CIF (Corporate Information Factory) Architecture:**

```
Operational Systems  →  Staging Area  →  EDW (3NF)
                                          ↓
                              Dependent Data Marts
                                          ↓
                                  OLAP / Reports / DSS
```

---

### 2.1.2 The Data Mart — Bottom-Up Philosophy

**Ralph Kimball's Argument:**
> *"A data warehouse is nothing more than the union of its data marts."* — Kimball et al., *The Data Warehouse Toolkit* (paraphrased canonical stance).

Operational Logic Steps:

1. **Identify a high-value business process** (e.g., Sales).
2. **Build a Star / Snowflake schema** (dimensional modelling) directly.
3. **Pull data from operational sources** (independent) **or from EDW** (dependent).
4. **Deliver rapid ROI** to the business unit.
5. **Repeat** for the next subject area (Marketing, HR, …).

**The "Why":**
- **Time-to-value is weeks**, not years.
- **Departmental ownership** ensures business buy-in.
- **Dimensional models** (Star schema) are intuitive for analysts.

**The "How" — Kimball's Dimensional Bus Architecture:**

```
Source 1 ─┐
Source 2 ─┼── Staging ──→  Data Mart (Star Schema: Fact + Dimensions)
Source 3 ─┘                       ↓
                             Reports / BI Tools
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight — Inmon vs. Kimball**
> | Aspect | Inmon (EDW-first) | Kimball (Data-Mart-first) |
> |--------|-------------------|---------------------------|
> | Approach | **Top-Down** | **Bottom-Up** |
> | Data Model | **3NF (Normalized)** | **Star / Snowflake (Dimensional)** |
> | First Deliverable | **EDW** | **Data Mart** |
> | Enterprise Consistency | **High** | Medium (achieved via *conformed dimensions*) |
> | Time-to-Value | **Slow** | **Fast** |
> | KTU Marking Weight | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

---

### 2.1.3 The Virtual Warehouse — The Federation Philosophy

Operational Logic Steps:

1. **Identify distributed source databases** that are physically separate.
2. **Define a global schema** (often an **integrated/mediated schema** using **GAV, LAV, or GLAV** approaches).
3. **Install a middleware / mediator layer** that rewrites high-level queries into sub-queries.
4. **Push queries down to sources** at execution time.
5. **Aggregate / join results** virtually for the user.

**The "Why":**
- **Zero data duplication** → no storage cost, no synchronization issues.
- **Real-time freshness** because the source IS the data.
- **Best for volatile, exploratory, or rarely-queried use cases.**

**The "How" — Mediator / Wrapper Architecture:**

```
User Query → Mediator (Query Rewriter)
                ├── Wrapper 1 → Source DB 1
                ├── Wrapper 2 → Source DB 2
                └── Wrapper 3 → Source DB 3
                ← Results integrated and returned
```

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet Legend:** Use these formulas and ratios to justify answers quantitatively in **14-mark KTU questions**.

### 2.2.1 Storage & Volume Estimation

$$
V_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_{i} \times (1 + r_{\text{overhead}})
$$

where $V_i$ is the volume of each data domain and $r_{\text{overhead}}$ is the index/metadata overhead (typically $\mathbf{20\% \text{ to } 40\%}$).

### 2.2.2 Data Mart Size as a Fraction of EDW

$$
V_{\text{DM}} = \alpha \cdot V_{\text{EDW}}, \quad \text{where } \alpha \in [0.05, \, 0.20]
$$

For KTU numericals, assume **$\alpha = 0.10$** (a Data Mart is typically **~10%** of the EDW size).

### 2.2.3 Query Response Time (ETL vs Virtual)

$$
T_{\text{virtual}} = T_{\text{network}} + \sum_{j=1}^{m} T_{\text{source},j} + T_{\text{integration}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{ETL}} = T_{\text{load}} + T_{\text{query}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Generally $T_{\text{ETL,query}} \ll T_{\text{virtual}}$ because data is pre-staged, but $T_{\text{ETL,load}}$ is incurred upfront.

### 2.2.4 Cost Justification Rule (KTU 14-Mark Favourite)

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{EDW}} \approx 5 \text{ to } 10 \times \text{Cost}_{\text{Data Mart}}
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{Virtual}} \approx 0.1 \times \text{Cost}_{\text{Data Mart}}
$$

### 2.2.5 Implementation Time (Rule of Thumb)

$$
T_{\text{impl,EDW}} \approx 6 \times T_{\text{impl,DM}} \approx 24 \times T_{\text{impl,VW}}
$$

| Model | Implementation Time | Cost Multiplier | Data Volume |
|-------|---------------------|------------------|-------------|
| **EDW** | $\mathbf{18-36 \text{ months}}$ | $\mathbf{10\times}$ | $\mathbf{>10 \text{ TB}}$ |
| **Data Mart** | $\mathbf{3-6 \text{ months}}$ | $\mathbf{2\times}$ | $\mathbf{100 \text{ GB} - 2 \text{ TB}}$ |
| **Virtual** | $\mathbf{2-6 \text{ weeks}}$ | $\mathbf{0.5\times}$ | $\mathbf{0 \text{ (logical)}}$ |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Typical Model Choice | Rationale |
|------------------|----------------------|-----------|
| **Banking (Retail)** | **EDW** for risk + Data Marts for product lines | Regulatory compliance demands single truth. |
| **E-Commerce** | **Data Marts** (Sales, Inventory, Marketing) | Rapid iteration, departmental ownership. |
| **Healthcare Research** | **Virtual Warehouse** across hospital systems | Privacy + no central copy of patient data. |
| **Government Census** | **EDW** | National-scale historical analytics. |
| **Telecom (CDR Analysis)** | **Data Marts** feeding an EDW | Subject areas are clearly bounded. |

---

## 2.4 Section Summary Table

| Architecture | Philosophy | Designer | Storage | Granularity | KTU Bloom Level |
|--------------|-----------|----------|---------|-------------|------------------|
| **EDW** | Top-Down | **Inmon** | Physical, large | Enterprise-wide | **Analyze** (L4) |
| **Data Mart** | Bottom-Up | **Kimball** | Physical, small | Departmental | **Apply** (L3) |
| **Virtual** | Federation | **Mediator-based** | **None** | Cross-domain, real-time | **Understand** (L2) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides **exhaustive, board-style derivations** for the **quantitative comparisons** KTU examiners love. Every step is written out; **no shortcut is taken**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sizing a Data Mart from an EDW

**Problem Statement (KTU 14-Mark Favourite Style):**
> *"A bank's enterprise data warehouse occupies **8 TB** of physical storage. The Marketing department wants to spin up a **dependent Data Mart** for campaign analytics. Assuming the Data Mart will hold **10% of EDW volume** with an additional **25% index/metadata overhead**, compute the Data Mart's effective storage footprint."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the base Data Mart volume (logical data, before overhead).

$$
V_{\text{DM,base}} = \alpha \cdot V_{\text{EDW}} = 0.10 \times 8 \text{ TB} = 0.8 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the overhead factor $r_{\text{overhead}} = 25\% = 0.25$.

$$
V_{\text{DM,eff}} = V_{\text{DM,base}} \times (1 + r_{\text{overhead}})
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$
V_{\text{DM,eff}} = 0.8 \text{ TB} \times (1 + 0.25) = 0.8 \text{ TB} \times 1.25
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to GB for clarity.

$$
0.8 \text{ TB} = 800 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 5:** Final calculation.

$$
V_{\text{DM,eff}} = 800 \text{ GB} \times 1.25 = 1000 \text{ GB} = 1 \text{ TB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** The Marketing Data Mart will require **1 TB** of effective physical storage, including the **25% overhead** for indexes, aggregations, and metadata.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost-of-Ownership Comparison

**Problem Statement:**
> *"An EDW implementation costs **₹5 Crore** (≈ \$600K) and takes **24 months**. A Data Mart costs **₹80 Lakh** and takes **5 months**. A Virtual Warehouse costs **₹15 Lakh** and takes **6 weeks**. Compare the cost-per-month-of-delivery and recommend the best approach for a startup that needs insights in 2 months with a budget of ₹50 Lakh."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute cost-per-month for each model.

$$
C_{\text{EDW,per-month}} = \frac{\text{Cost}_{\text{EDW}}}{T_{\text{EDW}}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}00{,}000}{24} \approx \text{₹}20{,}83{,}333 \text{ per month}
$$

**Step 2:**

$$
C_{\text{DM,per-month}} = \frac{80{,}00{,}000}{5} = \text{₹}16{,}00{,}000 \text{ per month}
$$

**Step 3:**

$$
C_{\text{VW,per-month}} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{1.5} = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000 \text{ per month}
$$

**Step 4:** Check constraints.

$$
T_{\text{needed}} = 2 \text{ months}, \quad \text{Budget} = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate feasibility.

| Model | Time ≤ 2 months? | Cost ≤ ₹50 Lakh? | Verdict |
|-------|------------------|-------------------|----------|
| **EDW** | ❌ (needs 24 months) | ✅ | **Rejected** — time violation |
| **Data Mart** | ❌ (needs 5 months) | ❌ (₹80L) | **Rejected** — both violated |
| **Virtual** | ✅ (1.5 months) | ✅ (₹15L) | **Accepted** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Recommendation:** The **Virtual Warehouse** is the only model that fits the startup's constraints. It delivers within 1.5 months and consumes only 30% of the allocated budget, leaving ₹35 Lakh for downstream Data Mart construction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Query Performance: ETL-based vs Virtual

**Problem Statement:**
> *"In an EDW, a typical query takes **4 seconds** over pre-staged data. In a Virtual Warehouse, the same query incurs a network latency of **800 ms** and requires joining results from **3 source DBs**, each taking **1.2 seconds**. The integration step takes **600 ms**. Compute and compare the total response times."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** EDW response time (direct, no integration overhead at query time).

$$
T_{\text{EDW}} = 4 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2:** Virtual Warehouse response time using the formula from Section 2.

$$
T_{\text{VW}} = T_{\text{network}} + \sum_{j=1}^{3} T_{\text{source},j} + T_{\text{integration}}
$$

**Step 3:** Plug in values.

$$
T_{\text{VW}} = 0.8 + (1.2 + 1.2 + 1.2) + 0.6
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the sum of source query times.

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{3} T_{\text{source},j} = 3 \times 1.2 = 3.6 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5:** Add the latency and integration terms.

$$
T_{\text{VW}} = 0.8 + 3.6 + 0.6 = 5.0 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the slowdown ratio.

$$
\text{Slowdown} = \frac{T_{\text{VW}}}{T_{\text{EDW}}} = \frac{5.0}{4.0} = 1.25\times
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The Virtual Warehouse is **25% slower** in this scenario, but offers **zero storage cost and real-time freshness**. The KTU examiner will award full marks only if you mention **this trade-off explicitly**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What?

| Decision Criterion | Choose **EDW** if… | Choose **Data Mart** if… | Choose **Virtual** if… |
|--------------------|---------------------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| **Budget** | > ₹3 Crore available | ₹50L – ₹1 Crore | < ₹25 Lakh |
| **Time-to-Market** | 18+ months acceptable | 3–6 months needed | < 2 months needed |
| **User Base** | > 500 concurrent users | 50–500 users | < 50 users |
| **Subject Area** | Multiple cross-functional domains | Single department | Cross-source exploration |
| **Data Freshness** | Daily/weekly acceptable | Hourly/daily | **Real-time mandatory** |
| **Data Volume** | > 5 TB | 100 GB – 2 TB | Any (no copy) |
| **Governance** | Strict (one version of truth) | Departmental | Loose / experimental |
| **Industry Norm** | Banking, Govt, Insurance | E-commerce, Retail | Research, Healthcare, POCs |

---

## 3.5 Hybrid Architecture — The KTU "Trick" Question

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 14-mark questions often ask: *"Can these three models coexist?"* — **YES**, in a **Hub-and-Spoke Hybrid**.

**Logical Layout:**

$$
\text{Operational Sources} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{EDW (Hub)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Data Marts (Spokes)}
$$
$$
\text{Operational Sources} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Virtual Warehouse (Overlay Layer)}
$$

The **EDW** is the *trusted source*. **Data Marts** are derived from it. The **Virtual Warehouse** is an *additional layer* for ad-hoc, real-time queries that bypass ETL. This is the architecture most modern enterprises (e.g., **Walmart, Amazon, ICICI Bank**) actually deploy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below use **purely alphanumeric Mermaid node IDs** (no reserved keywords) and **raw uppercase text** inside quoted labels (no markdown formatting inside labels).

---

## 4.1 Enterprise Data Warehouse (EDW) — Top-Down Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[OPERATIONAL SYSTEM 1 ERP] --> B1[STAGING AREA ETL LANDING]
    A2[OPERATIONAL SYSTEM 2 CRM] --> B1
    A3[OPERATIONAL SYSTEM 3 SCM] --> B1
    A4[OPERATIONAL SYSTEM 4 HR] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[DATA CLEANSING TRANSFORMATION CONFORMING]
    C1 --> D1[ENTERPRISE DATA WAREHOUSE 3NF CENTRAL]
    D1 --> E1[DEPENDENT DATA MART SALES]
    D1 --> E2[DEPENDENT DATA MART FINANCE]
    D1 --> E3[DEPENDENT DATA MART HR]
    E1 --> F1[OLAP CUBES DASHBOARDS REPORTS]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[ENTERPRISE USERS STRATEGIC DECISION MAKING]
```

---

## 4.2 Data Mart — Bottom-Up Dimensional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[OPERATIONAL DB 1] --> ST1[STAGING NORMALIZE]
    S2[OPERATIONAL DB 2] --> ST1
    ST1 --> DM1[INDEPENDENT DATA MART SALES STAR SCHEMA]
    ST1 --> DM2[INDEPENDENT DATA MART MARKETING STAR SCHEMA]
    ST1 --> DM3[INDEPENDENT DATA MART INVENTORY STAR SCHEMA]
    DM1 --> B1[POWER BI TABLEAU AD HOC QUERIES]
    DM2 --> B1
    DM3 --> B1
    DM1 -.-> CONFD[CONFORMED DIMENSIONS DATE PRODUCT CUSTOMER]
    DM2 -.-> CONFD
    DM3 -.-> CONFD
```

---

## 4.3 Virtual Warehouse — Mediator-Based Federation

```mermaid
graph TD
    UQ[USER AD HOC QUERY] --> MED[MEDIATOR MIDDLEWARE QUERY REWRITER]
    MED --> W1[WRAPPER 1 TRANSLATOR]
    MED --> W2[WRAPPER 2 TRANSLATOR]
    MED --> W3[WRAPPER 3 TRANSLATOR]
    W1 --> SD1[SOURCE DB 1 HETEROGENEOUS]
    W2 --> SD2[SOURCE DB 2 HETEROGENEOUS]
    W3 --> SD3[SOURCE DB 3 HETEROGENEOUS]
    SD1 --> RES[RESULT INTEGRATION]
    SD2 --> RES
    SD3 --> RES
    RES --> MED
    MED --> UQ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** In the Virtual Warehouse diagram, the user perceives one logical warehouse, but **no physical data ever moves**. The mediator is the *only* persistent component.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — All Three Models Side by Side

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph EDW_BLOCK[ENTERPRISE DATA WAREHOUSE]
        E1[ALL SOURCES] --> E2[SINGLE STAGING]
        E2 --> E3[CENTRAL EDW 3NF]
        E3 --> E4[DEPENDENT DATA MARTS]
        E4 --> E5[ENTERPRISE BI]
    end
    subgraph DM_BLOCK[DATA MART]
        D1[FEW SOURCES] --> D2[LIGHT STAGING]
        D2 --> D3[STAR SCHEMA DM]
        D3 --> D4[DEPARTMENTAL BI]
    end
    subgraph VW_BLOCK[VIRTUAL WAREHOUSE]
        V1[USER QUERY] --> V2[MEDIATOR]
        V2 --> V3[NO PHYSICAL STORAGE]
        V3 --> V4[REAL TIME RESULTS]
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Hybrid Architecture — Hub-and-Spoke with Virtual Overlay

```mermaid
graph TB
    OS[OPERATIONAL SOURCES] --> STG[STAGING AREA]
    STG --> EDW[HUB ENTERPRISE DATA WAREHOUSE 3NF]
    EDW --> DM1[SPOKE DATA MART FINANCE]
    EDW --> DM2[SPOKE DATA MART SALES]
    EDW --> DM3[SPOKE DATA MART MARKETING]
    OS -.-> VW[OVERLAY VIRTUAL WAREHOUSE]
    EDW -.-> VW
    DM1 --> BI[BI TOOLS]
    DM2 --> BI
    DM3 --> BI
    VW --> BI
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This hybrid is the **most commonly asked diagram in KTU Module 1**. The dashed lines indicate **logical/optional flows** (the Virtual Warehouse does not extract data into itself).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** 
**Define a Data Mart. Differentiate between a Dependent Data Mart and an Independent Data Mart.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Board Standard):**

A **Data Mart** is a **subset of a data warehouse, scoped to a single subject area or department**, designed to serve the analytical needs of a specific business function.

| Aspect | Dependent Data Mart | Independent Data Mart |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Source** | **Pulled from the EDW** | **Pulled directly from operational systems** |
| **Approach** | **Top-Down (Inmon)** | **Bottom-Up (Kimball)** |
| **Consistency** | High (inherits EDW standards) | Lower (risk of silos) |
| **Cost** | Higher (EDW must exist first) | Lower (no EDW needed) |
| **Time** | Slower | Faster |

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark definition + 2 marks comparison.

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** 
**What is a Virtual Data Warehouse? List two advantages and one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Virtual Data Warehouse** is a **logical view over physically distributed, heterogeneous data sources** that contains **no persistent data of its own**. Queries are rewritten by a mediator and executed at the source systems in real time.

**Advantages:**
1. **Zero storage cost** — no data duplication.
2. **Real-time data freshness** — sources are queried live.

**Limitation:**
1. **Poor query performance** for complex joins across many sources due to high network and integration overhead.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark definition + 1 mark each for advantages (2) + 1 mark for limitation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **ONE full question** out of two. Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Q3 (A). `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** (14 Marks)

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Enterprise Data Warehouse (EDW)** and the **Data Mart** models with respect to **scope, data model, time-to-implementation, cost, and users**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A retail chain's EDW occupies **12 TB**. The **HR department** is building a **dependent Data Mart** estimated at **8% of EDW volume** with a **30% index overhead**. Compute the effective storage and justify whether the organization should opt for an **EDW-first** or **Data-Mart-first** strategy. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

| Parameter | **Enterprise Data Warehouse (EDW)** | **Data Mart (DM)** |
|-----------|------------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Scope** | Enterprise-wide; all subjects | Single subject / department |
| **Designer** | Bill **Inmon** | Ralph **Kimball** |
| **Architecture** | **Top-Down (CIF)** | **Bottom-Up (Dimensional)** |
| **Data Model** | **3NF (Normalized)** | **Star / Snowflake (Dimensional)** |
| **Users** | **Hundreds to thousands** | **Tens to hundreds** |
| **Time-to-Implement** | **18–36 months** | **3–6 months** |
| **Cost** | **Very High** (multi-crore) | **Low–Medium** |
| **Data Consistency** | **One version of truth** | Possible silos (Kimball mitigates via *conformed dimensions*) |
| **Subject Orientation** | All subjects | One subject |

> **Marking Scheme:** Tabular comparison (5 marks) + brief justification (2 marks).

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute base Data Mart volume.

$$
V_{\text{DM,base}} = \alpha \cdot V_{\text{EDW}} = 0.08 \times 12 \text{ TB} = 0.96 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert to GB.

$$
V_{\text{DM,base}} = 960 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply 30% overhead.

$$
V_{\text{DM,eff}} = 960 \text{ GB} \times (1 + 0.30) = 960 \times 1.3 = 1248 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert back to TB.

$$
V_{\text{DM,eff}} = 1.248 \text{ TB} \approx 1.25 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 5:** Strategic Justification.

- Since the organization **already has an EDW** (12 TB), the **EDW-first** approach is the **correct historical choice** — building Data Marts from it ensures **data consistency**.
- The HR Data Mart is **~10% of EDW**, well within the **practical scope** of a single department.
- Building **independent Data Marts from scratch** would be **redundant and costly** because the EDW is already the source of truth.

> **Final Answer:** Effective storage = **1.25 TB**; recommended strategy = **EDW-first (Inmon) with dependent Data Marts**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - ❌ Students often confuse **"dependent"** with **"low-quality"** — the EDW is the *strength*, not the weakness.
> - ❌ Forget to convert units (TB → GB) — full marks are lost.
> - ❌ Give a **generic answer** for strategic justification — KTU wants the **EDW-first stance** explicitly defended.

---

### **Q3 (B). `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the **Virtual Data Warehouse** architecture with a neat block diagram. Discuss **two scenarios** where it is the preferred choice over an EDW. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pharmaceutical company has **4 geographically distributed research databases** (each ~500 GB) containing clinical trial data. The IT team is debating between building an **EDW** (₹4 Crore, 18 months) versus a **Virtual Warehouse** (₹25 Lakh, 2 months) for a **cross-site drug-interaction study** lasting 6 months. Recommend the appropriate model with quantitative justification. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The **Virtual Data Warehouse (V-warehouse)** is a **federated architecture** where a *mediator* layer provides a unified logical view over physically distributed, heterogeneous data sources. **No data is physically copied**; queries are decomposed and pushed to the source databases, and results are integrated at query time.

**Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4.3 above):**

```
User Query → Mediator (Query Rewriter)
                ├── Wrapper 1 → Source DB 1
                ├── Wrapper 2 → Source DB 2
                └── Wrapper 3 → Source DB 3
                ← Integrated results returned
```

**Scenarios Where V-Warehouse is Preferred:**

1. **Regulatory / Privacy Constraints (e.g., Healthcare):**
   - Centralizing patient data into an EDW is **legally prohibited** (HIPAA, GDPR, India's DPDP Act). A Virtual Warehouse allows analytics **without moving sensitive data**.

2. **Short-Term / Exploratory Projects (e.g., Research Pilot):**
   - For a **6-month cross-site study**, the 18-month EDW timeline is **3× longer than the project itself**. A Virtual Warehouse delivers in 2 months.

> **Marking Scheme:** 4 marks for explanation + diagram, 1.5 marks each for scenarios.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Identify the total source data volume.

$$
V_{\text{total,sources}} = 4 \times 500 \text{ GB} = 2000 \text{ GB} = 2 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare timelines.

$$
T_{\text{EDW}} = 18 \text{ months} \quad \text{vs} \quad T_{\text{VW}} = 2 \text{ months}
$$

The project lasts **6 months**. The EDW timeline (18 months) **exceeds** the project duration — the project would **end before the EDW is ready**.

**Step 3:** Compare costs.

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{EDW}} = \text{₹}4{,}00{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{vs} \quad \text{Cost}_{\text{VW}} = \text{₹}25{,}00{,}000
$$

The Virtual Warehouse is **16× cheaper**.

**Step 4:** Cost-per-month ratio.

$$
C_{\text{EDW,per-month}} = \frac{4 \text{ Cr}}{18} \approx \text{₹}22.2 \text{ Lakh/month}
$$

$$
C_{\text{VW,per-month}} = \frac{25 \text{ Lakh}}{2} = \text{₹}12.5 \text{ Lakh/month}
$$

**Step 5:** Decision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommendation: Virtual Warehouse.**
> 
> - **Timeline Constraint:** EDW (18 months) > Project (6 months) → ❌ infeasible.
> - **Cost Constraint:** VW is 16× cheaper → ✅ favourable.
> - **Data Sensitivity:** Clinical trial data is **highly regulated**; centralizing into an EDW raises **compliance risks**; the Virtual Warehouse keeps data at source.
> - **Use Case Fit:** A cross-site **ad-hoc** drug-interaction study is a **classic V-warehouse use case** — exploratory, short-term, federated.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q3-B):**
> - ❌ Students often recommend the EDW simply because "it's more powerful" — **ignoring the project timeline constraint** is the most common mark-loser.
> - ❌ Failing to mention **regulatory/privacy** justification loses 1.5 marks.
> - ❌ Skipping the **cost-per-month calculation** (a quantitative anchor) costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Master Pitfall List (Module 1 Specific)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Virtual Warehouse with Data Mart** — Remember, VW has *no physical data*; DM does.
> 2. **Mixing up Inmon and Kimball** — Inmon = EDW-first, 3NF, Top-Down; Kimball = Data-Mart-first, Star Schema, Bottom-Up.
> 3. **Forgetting the four properties of EDW** — Subject-oriented, Integrated, Time-variant, Non-volatile (Inmon's classic).
> 4. **Omitting diagrams** — Every 14-mark question **demands** a block diagram; skipping it costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Not converting units in numericals** — TB ↔ GB conversions are tested; show the conversion step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ✅ A **Data Warehouse Model** is an **architectural blueprint**, not a product.
- ✅ **Three canonical models**: **EDW (centralized)**, **Data Mart (departmental)**, **Virtual Warehouse (logical/federated)**.
- ✅ **EDW** is associated with **Bill Inmon**, **3NF**, **Top-Down**, **CIF architecture**, **enterprise-wide scope**.
- ✅ **Data Mart** is associated with **Ralph Kimball**, **Star/Snowflake schema**, **Bottom-Up**, **dimensional modelling**, **conformed dimensions** for cross-mart consistency.
- ✅ **Dependent Data Mart** is sourced from the **EDW**; **Independent Data Mart** is sourced from **operational systems**.
- ✅ **Virtual Warehouse** has **zero physical storage**; it is a **mediator + wrapper** layer that translates user queries into source-specific sub-queries.
- ✅ **Cost order:** $\text{Cost}_{\text{VW}} < \text{Cost}_{\text{DM}} \ll \text{Cost}_{\text{EDW}}$.
- ✅ **Time order:** $T_{\text{EDW}} \gg T_{\text{DM}} > T_{\text{VW}}$.
- ✅ **Data Volume order:** $V_{\text{EDW}} \gg V_{\text{DM}}$; $V_{\text{VW}} = 0$ logically.
- ✅ **Inmon's CIF (Corporate Information Factory)** = EDW + dependent Data Marts + OLAP tools.
- ✅ **Hybrid Architecture** (EDW as hub + Data Marts as spokes + Virtual Warehouse as overlay) is the **de-facto industry standard** — KTU expects you to **know and recommend** it.
- ✅ **Virtual Warehouse** is the **only model offering real-time freshness** by default.
- ✅ KTU numericals frequently use the **$\alpha = 0.10$ (10%) rule** for Data Mart sizing from EDW.
- ✅ Always **draw a block diagram** in 14-mark answers — it is worth **2–3 marks by itself**.
- ✅ Always **state the assumptions** (e.g., overhead = 25%, $\alpha$ = 8–10%) before plugging into formulas.
- ✅ **Real-time use case** → Virtual Warehouse; **Single department, fast ROI** → Data Mart; **Enterprise-wide, regulated, long-term** → EDW.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensional Modeling & Design: Dimensional Modeling Basics, Facts, Dimensions, Star Schema, Snowflake Schema
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT412 - DATA WAREHOUSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Dimensional Modeling & Design: Dimensional Modeling Basics, Facts, Dimensions, Star Schema, Snowflake Schema # MODULE 2 — DIMENSIONAL MODELING & DESIGN ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Dimensional Modeling — Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Dimensional Modeling (DM)** is a logical design technique used in data warehousing to structure data into **fact tables** (containing measurable, quantitative business events) and **dimension tables** (containing descriptive, textual attributes that provide context to the facts). It optimizes the data warehouse for **query performance, ease of use, and business intelligibility** rather than for transaction processing (OLTP normalization). The technique was popularized by **Ralph Kimball** (mid-1990s) as the de-facto standard for analytical data warehouse design. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, dimensional modeling is the bridge between the *conceptual* warehouse design and the *physical* schema implementation. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Catalog" Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a giant public library. The **catalogue card index** describes *what* each book is about (title, author, genre, publisher, year). The **circulation log** records *how many times* each book was borrowed and *when*. You don't store both on the same card. Instead, the log refers to the book by a unique ID. This is exactly how dimensional modeling works: > - **Dimensions** = the catalogue (descriptive context: *who, what, where, when, why*) > - **Facts** = the circulation log (the measurable event: *how many, how much, how often*) ### 1.3 Why Dimensional Modeling? — The Business Case | Aspect | OLTP (Normalized) | Dimensional (Star/Snowflake) | |---|---|---| | Primary Goal | Transaction integrity | Analytical query speed | | Read Pattern | Single-row lookups | Bulk aggregations, scans | | Schema Style | Highly normalized (3NF) | Denormalized / partially denormalized | | User Friendliness | Low (many joins) | **High (intuitive)** | | KTU Exam Focus | Background only | **Primary focus of Module 2** | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Dimensional modeling sacrifices some storage efficiency (denormalization causes redundancy) in exchange for **fewer joins, simpler SQL, and faster business queries** — a deliberate trade-off because analytics is read-heavy and write-light. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Core Building Blocks Dimensional modeling rests on three primitives. Every star/snowflake schema is built from these. ### 2.1.1 Fact Table A fact table stores the **business measurements** (metrics) of a business process. Each row represents a business event at a measurable grain. **The Four-Part Anatomy of a Fact Table Row:** 1. **Foreign Keys** — link to dimension tables (one per dimension). 2. **Additive / Semi-additive / Non-additive Measures** — the numeric facts. 3. **Optional Degenerate Dimensions** — operational keys (e.g., *Invoice Number*). 4. **Audit Timestamps** — load date, source system ID. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Grain Statement (Kimball Rule #1):** A fact table must be declared at its **atomic grain** — the lowest level of detail captured. Examples: *one row per line item on a sales receipt* or *one row per ATM withdrawal transaction*. **Classification of Facts (Numeric Measures):** | Type of Measure | Definition | Example | Aggregation Allowed? | |---|---|---|---| | **Additive** | Can be summed across *all* dimensions | Sales Amount (₹), Quantity Sold | ✅ Yes — across all dims | | **Semi-additive** | Summable across *some* dimensions only | Account Balance (sum across products, NOT across time) | ⚠️ Partial | | **Non-additive** | Cannot be summed; must be averaged/derived | Unit Price, Profit Margin %, Ratios | ❌ No — use averages | ### 2.1.2 Dimension Table A dimension table provides the **descriptive context** surrounding a fact. Columns are *attributes* (text, hierarchies, classifications). **Conformed Dimensions:** Dimensions shared across multiple fact tables in an enterprise data warehouse. Example: a single `dim_date` used by both *Sales* facts and *Inventory* facts. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight — Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD):** Dimensions change over time. The SCD strategy chosen (Type 1, Type 2, Type 3) determines how history is preserved. Most KTU questions test **SCD Type 2** — adding a new row with effective dates when an attribute changes. ### 2.1.3 Surrogate Key vs Natural Key | Key Type | Definition | Why Used in DW? | |---|---|---| | **Natural Key** | Operational key from source system (e.g., `cust_ssn`, `product_sku`) | Reused for lookup only | | **Surrogate Key** | System-generated integer (e.g., `customer_key = 1001`) | **Insulates DW from source-system changes**; enables SCD Type 2 history tracking | ## 2.2 Star Schema > [!IMPORTANT] > **Star Schema** is the canonical dimensional model where a **central fact table** is directly connected to multiple **denormalized dimension tables**. The visual layout resembles a **star** — fact at the center, dimensions as the radiating points. **Characteristics:** - One fact table + N dimension tables. - Dimensions are **denormalized** (all hierarchies flattened into one table). - Foreign keys in the fact table point to **single surrogate keys** in each dimension. - Query path: 1 fact table join + N dimension joins (typical SQL has 3–6 joins). ## 2.3 Snowflake Schema > [!IMPORTANT] > **Snowflake Schema** is a **normalized extension** of the star schema where one or more dimension tables are split into **multiple related sub-tables** along their natural hierarchy. The resulting visual layout resembles a **snowflake**. **Characteristics:** - Dimensions are **normalized** into multiple tables (e.g., `dim_product` → `dim_category` → `dim_department`). - Saves storage by eliminating redundancy. - Increases **number of joins** required for a query → potentially slower. - Often used in **large, slowly-changing reference data** (geography, product hierarchies). ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Concept | One-Line Definition | KTU Exam Tip | |---|---|---| | Fact Table | Stores numeric measurements at a defined grain | Always state the **grain** first | | Dimension Table | Stores descriptive context (who, what, where, when) | Surrogate keys are mandatory | | Star Schema | Denormalized fact + denormalized dimensions | Default choice for DW | | Snowflake Schema | Normalized dimension hierarchies | Save storage, cost more joins | | Fact Constellation (Galaxy) | Multiple fact tables sharing dimensions | Used in enterprise DW | | Surrogate Key | System-generated integer primary key | Never use natural key as PK | | SCD Type 2 | Adds a new row to preserve history | Most commonly tested SCD | | Grain | Atomic level of detail in a fact row | Define before designing schema | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Performance Metrics in DW (for reference):** > - **Query response time target:** 3–5 seconds for typical analytical queries. > - **Index strategy on fact table:** Bitmap indexes on low-cardinality foreign keys; B-tree on high-cardinality date keys. > - **Partitioning grain:** Usually monthly or weekly partitions on the date dimension key. # 3. Step-by-Step Design Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — Retail Sales Data Warehouse We will design schemas for the following business process: > **Business Requirement:** *A retail company wants to analyze daily sales by store, product, customer, and promotion.* ### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Identify the Business Process The business process is **Sales Transactions** (point-of-sale events). ### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Declare the Grain The atomic grain is: **One row per product line item on a sales transaction receipt per day per store per customer per promotion.** > [!NOTE] > The grain statement fixes: > - One fact row per **line item** (not per transaction, because a receipt may have multiple products). > - Date, store, product, customer, and promotion are the **dimensions** of analysis. ### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Identify the Dimensions From the grain, the dimensions are: 1. `dim_date` — when the sale happened. 2. `dim_store` — where the sale happened. 3. `dim_product` — what was sold. 4. `dim_customer` — who bought. 5. `dim_promotion` — under what offer. ### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Identify the Facts (Numeric Measures) | Measure | Type | Why | |---|---|---| | `quantity_sold` | Additive | Sum across all dims | | `sales_amount` | Additive | Sum across all dims | | `discount_amount` | Additive | Sum across all dims | | `unit_cost` | Non-additive | Cannot be summed; use weighted average | | `profit_margin_pct` | Non-additive | Ratio, not a measure | ### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Schema Construction (Star Schema) The resulting star schema design is: - **Center:** `fact_sales` table with foreign keys to all 5 dimensions + the 5 measures. - **Rays:** 5 denormalized dimension tables, each with a `*_key` surrogate primary key. ### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Schema Construction (Snowflake Schema) For the snowflake variant, we normalize the `dim_product` table: $$ \text{dim\_product} \rightarrow \text{dim\_category} \rightarrow \text{dim\_department} $$ And similarly for `dim_store`: $$ \text{dim\_store} \rightarrow \text{dim\_city} \rightarrow \text{dim\_state} \rightarrow \text{dim\_country} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **fact table** in both star and snowflake variants is *identical* — only the **dimension tables** are normalized in the snowflake variant. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Star vs Snowflake Comparison The decision metric is governed by an analytical trade-off. Define: $$ \text{Query Complexity} = J_{\text{star}} + \sum_{d=1}^{N} (\text{NormalizationDepth}_d - 1) $$ Where: - $J_{\text{star}}$ = number of joins in the equivalent star schema. - $N$ = number of dimensions. - $\text{NormalizationDepth}_d$ = number of sub-tables for dimension $d$ (1 for star, $\geq 2$ for snowflake). **Derivation Logic — Why the Snowflake Has More Joins:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Let } D_d &= \text{depth of dimension } d \text{ in tables} \\ \text{Total dimension joins per query} &= \sum_{d=1}^{N} D_d \\ \text{For star schema: } D_d &= 1 \; \forall d \\ \text{For snowflake schema: } D_d &\geq 2 \text{ for normalized dims} \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical Comparison for the Retail Example:** | Dimension | Star Depth $D_d$ | Snowflake Depth $D_d$ | |---|---|---| | dim_date | 1 | 1 | | dim_product | 1 | 3 (product → category → department) | | dim_store | 1 | 4 (store → city → state → country) | | dim_customer | 1 | 1 | | dim_promotion | 1 | 1 | | **Total dimension joins** | **5** | **9** | Plus 1 join to the fact table itself in both cases. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Star joins} &= 1 + 5 = 6 \\ \text{Snowflake joins} &= 1 + 9 = 10 \\ \text{Extra joins in snowflake} &= 10 - 6 = 4 \end{aligned} $$ This 67% increase in joins is the **analytical cost** of the snowflake's storage savings. ## 3.3 Storage Cost Derivations Let: - $R_d$ = number of rows in the original (star) dimension $d$. - $A_d$ = average number of child rows per parent (e.g., avg products per category). **Storage Comparison:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Storage}_{\text{star}} &= \sum_{d=1}^{N} R_d \\ \text{Storage}_{\text{snowflake}} &= \sum_{d=1}^{N} \frac{R_d}{A_d} + \sum_{d=1}^{N} (R_d \cdot A_d) \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Dimensional modeling is the design foundation of every modern cloud data warehouse — **Snowflake, Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery, Azure Synapse**. The same star/snowflake principles apply, even though these platforms physically store data in columnar, distributed formats. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Star Schema — Mermaid Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TD factA["FACT_SALES
PK: sales_key
FKs: date_key, store_key, product_key, customer_key, promotion_key
Measures: quantity_sold, sales_amount, discount_amount, unit_cost, profit_margin_pct"] dimA["DIM_DATE
PK: date_key
Attrs: day, month, quarter, year, weekday, is_holiday, fiscal_year"] dimB["DIM_STORE
PK: store_key
Attrs: store_name, city, state, country, region, store_type, open_date"] dimC["DIM_PRODUCT
PK: product_key
Attrs: sku, product_name, brand, category, subcategory, package_size"] dimD["DIM_CUSTOMER
PK: customer_key
Attrs: customer_name, gender, age_band, income_band, city, signup_date, loyalty_tier"] dimE["DIM_PROMOTION
PK: promotion_key
Attrs: promo_name, discount_type, ad_type, start_date, end_date"] factA --- dimA factA --- dimB factA --- dimC factA --- dimD factA --- dimE ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagram above shows a classic **5-ray star schema** for retail sales analytics. Note the denormalized dimensions — e.g., `dim_product` directly contains `category` and `subcategory` in the same table, no separate join needed. ## 4.2 Snowflake Schema — Mermaid Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TD factB["FACT_SALES
sales_key, FKs to all dims, Measures"] dimF["DIM_DATE
date_key, day, month, year"] dimG["DIM_STORE
store_key, store_name, city_key"] dimH["DIM_CITY
city_key, city_name, state_key"] dimI["DIM_STATE
state_key, state_name, country_key"] dimJ["DIM_COUNTRY
country_key, country_name, region"] dimK["DIM_PRODUCT
product_key, product_name, brand, category_key"] dimL["DIM_CATEGORY
category_key, category_name, department_key"] dimM["DIM_DEPARTMENT
department_key, department_name"] dimN["DIM_CUSTOMER
customer_key, name, attributes"] dimO["DIM_PROMOTION
promotion_key, promo_attributes"] factB --- dimF factB --- dimG factB --- dimK factB --- dimN factB --- dimO dimG --- dimH dimH --- dimI dimI --- dimJ dimK --- dimL dimL --- dimM ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > Observe the snowflake pattern: the `dim_store → dim_city → dim_state → dim_country` chain and the `dim_product → dim_category → dim_department` chain. Each child table holds the surrogate key of its parent. This is what gives the snowflake its fractal-like appearance. ## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Query Execution Path ```mermaid graph LR userA["BI User / Analyst"] --> toolA["Reporting Tool
Tableau / Power BI"] toolA --> queryA["SQL Query Generator"] queryA --> optA["Query Optimizer"] optA --> joinA["Join Engine
Fact table + N Dimensions"] joinA --> aggA["Aggregation Engine
SUM, AVG, COUNT"] aggA --> resultA["Result Set returned to BI Tool"] resultA --> userA ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** In star schemas, the query optimizer can apply **star-join optimization** — a specialized algorithm that recognizes a fact table radiating to multiple dimension lookups and uses bitmap indexes for high-speed filtering. Snowflake schemas often defeat this optimization because the optimizer sees a complex normalized graph instead of a clean star. ## 4.4 SCD Type 2 — Historical Tracking Flow ```mermaid graph TD srcA["Source System
Customer moves from Kochi to Chennai"] --> etlA["ETL Process Detects Change"] etlA --> insA["Insert NEW row in dim_customer
with new surrogate key
and effective_date"] insA --> expA["Expire OLD row
Set end_date = today
Set is_current = 0"] expA --> factRef["Existing Fact Rows
continue to point to OLD key
preserving historical accuracy"] insA --> factRef ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1: Define Dimensional Modeling. Mention any four advantages of Star Schema over Snowflake Schema. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** Dimensional modeling is a data warehouse design technique that organizes data into **fact tables** (quantitative business measurements) and **dimension tables** (descriptive context). It is optimized for analytical querying rather than transaction processing. **Four advantages of Star Schema over Snowflake Schema:** 1. **Simpler SQL Queries** — fewer joins required, easier to write and read. 2. **Better Query Performance** — exploits star-join optimization in modern RDBMS engines. 3. **Easier Business Understanding** — denormalized structure is intuitive for business users. 4. **Faster Aggregation** — fewer table scans during GROUP BY operations on dimensions. *(3 marks — definition 1 mark, advantages 2 marks)* --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between Fact Table and Dimension Table with an example. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Fact Table | Dimension Table | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Stores measurable business events | Stores descriptive context | | **Data Type** | Mostly numeric (quantitative) | Mostly textual (qualitative) | | **Size** | Very large (millions/billions of rows) | Relatively smaller | | **Example** | `fact_sales` with `sales_amount`, `quantity` | `dim_product` with `product_name`, `brand`, `category` | | **Primary Key** | Composite (all FKs) | Single surrogate key | *Example:* In a sales DW, a row in `fact_sales` records *“Customer 1041 bought 2 units of Product P-78 for ₹450 on 15-Jul-2024 at Store S-12 under Promotion PR-09”*. The fact table holds `quantity=2, sales_amount=450`; the dimension tables hold the descriptions of the customer, product, date, store, and promotion. *(3 marks — differences 2 marks, example 1 mark)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Design a Star Schema for a Hospital Management Data Warehouse to analyze monthly patient admissions by doctor, department, hospital branch, diagnosis, and insurance provider. State the grain, identify 3 facts and 5 dimensions, draw the schema, and write 2 sample analytical queries.** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Design the Star Schema **Step 1 — Identify the Business Process:** *Patient Admissions and Treatments.* **Step 2 — Declare the Grain:** *One row per patient admission event per diagnosis per doctor per day per hospital branch.* **Step 3 — Identify Facts (Numeric Measures):** | Fact Measure | Type | Why | |---|---|---| | `admission_count` | Additive | Count of admissions | | `treatment_cost` | Additive | Sum across all dimensions | | `length_of_stay_days` | Semi-additive | Sum across dims, NOT across time | | `insurance_claim_amount` | Additive | Sum across all dimensions | **Step 4 — Identify Dimensions:** 1. `dim_date` — admission date (day, month, quarter, year, weekday, is_weekend) 2. `dim_doctor` — doctor info (name, specialization, qualification, years_experience) 3. `dim_department` — department (name, type, head_of_department) 4. `dim_branch` — hospital branch (name, city, state, pincode) 5. `dim_diagnosis` — diagnosis (icd_code, diagnosis_name, severity, category) 6. `dim_insurance` — insurance provider (company_name, policy_type, coverage_pct) **Step 5 — Star Schema Diagram (Mermaid):** ```mermaid graph TD factH["FACT_ADMISSION
PK: admission_key
FKs: date_key, doctor_key, dept_key, branch_key, diag_key, ins_key
Measures: admission_count, treatment_cost, length_of_stay, claim_amount"] d1["DIM_DATE
date_key, day, month, year, is_weekend"] d2["DIM_DOCTOR
doctor_key, name, specialization, qualification"] d3["DIM_DEPARTMENT
dept_key, dept_name, type, hod_name"] d4["DIM_BRANCH
branch_key, branch_name, city, state"] d5["DIM_DIAGNOSIS
diag_key, icd_code, diag_name, severity"] d6["DIM_INSURANCE
