ins_key, company_name, policy_type, coverage_pct"] factH --- d1 factH --- d2 factH --- d3 factH --- d4 factH --- d5 factH --- d6 ``` **Sample Analytical Queries:** **Query 1: Total treatment cost by department per quarter.** ```sql SELECT d.dept_name, dt.quarter, dt.year, SUM(f.treatment_cost) AS total_cost FROM fact_admission f JOIN dim_department d ON f.dept_key = d.dept_key JOIN dim_date dt ON f.date_key = dt.date_key GROUP BY d.dept_name, dt.quarter, dt.year ORDER BY dt.year, dt.quarter, total_cost DESC; ``` **Query 2: Average length of stay by diagnosis and branch.** ```sql SELECT dx.diag_name, b.branch_name, AVG(f.length_of_stay_days) AS avg_los FROM fact_admission f JOIN dim_diagnosis dx ON f.diag_key = dx.diag_key JOIN dim_branch b ON f.branch_key = b.branch_key GROUP BY dx.diag_name, b.branch_name ORDER BY avg_los DESC; ``` *(7 marks — grain 1, facts 1, dimensions 2, schema 2, queries 1)* --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Convert to Snowflake Schema and Compare **Snowflake Normalization:** The `dim_branch` table is normalized into a 3-level hierarchy: `dim_branch → dim_city → dim_state → dim_country` The `dim_doctor` table is normalized: `dim_doctor → dim_specialization → dim_department` **Snowflake Schema Diagram:** ```mermaid graph TD factS["FACT_ADMISSION
Same as star"] ds["DIM_DATE
date_key, day, month, year"] dbranch["DIM_BRANCH
branch_key, branch_name, city_key"] dcity["DIM_CITY
city_key, city_name, state_key"] dstate["DIM_STATE
state_key, state_name, country_key"] dcountry["DIM_COUNTRY
country_key, country_name"] ddoc["DIM_DOCTOR
doctor_key, name, spec_key"] dspec["DIM_SPECIALIZATION
spec_key, spec_name, dept_key"] ddept["DIM_DEPARTMENT
dept_key, dept_name"] ddx["DIM_DIAGNOSIS
diag_key, icd_code, diag_name"] dins["DIM_INSURANCE
ins_key, company_name"] factS --- ds factS --- dbranch factS --- ddoc factS --- ddx factS --- dins dbranch --- dcity dcity --- dstate dstate --- dcountry ddoc --- dspec dspec --- ddept ``` **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Star Schema | Snowflake Schema | |---|---|---| | Dimension structure | Denormalized (flat) | Normalized (hierarchical) | | Number of joins | Fewer | More (extra joins per hierarchy level) | | Storage | Higher (redundancy) | Lower (no redundancy) | | Query complexity | Simple SQL | Complex SQL | | Query performance | **Faster** | Slower | | Maintainability | Harder (changes touch denormalized table) | Easier (changes localized) | | Business readability | High | Lower | *(7 marks — normalization steps 2, schema 2, comparison 3)* --- ### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Explain the concepts of Factless Fact Tables and Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD). Discuss SCD Type 1, Type 2, and Type 3 with suitable examples.** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Factless Fact Tables and SCD Introduction **Factless Fact Table:** > [!NOTE] > A factless fact table is a fact table that **contains no numeric measures** — only foreign keys to dimensions. It records the **occurrence of an event** or the **coverage/absence of a relationship**. **Two Use Cases:** 1. **Event Tracking** — e.g., `fact_student_attendance` records which student attended which class on which date. No metric, just the event. 2. **Coverage/Eligibility** — e.g., `fact_promotion_coverage` records which products were promoted in which stores on which dates, used to analyze products that were promoted but did NOT sell (anti-join with `fact_sales`). **Example Schema:** ```mermaid graph TD factAtt["FACT_STUDENT_ATTENDANCE
PK: date_key + student_key + class_key
No measures - just FKs"] dimDt["DIM_DATE
date_key, day, month, year"] dimSt["DIM_STUDENT
student_key, name, class, section"] dimCl["DIM_CLASS
class_key, subject, teacher_key"] dimTc["DIM_TEACHER
teacher_key, name"] factAtt --- dimDt factAtt --- dimSt factAtt --- dimCl dimCl --- dimTc ``` **SCD — Why Dimensions Change:** Dimensions store descriptive attributes. Over time, these attributes change — e.g., a customer's city, a product's category, an employee's department. The SCD strategy chosen determines **how the warehouse preserves history**. *(7 marks — factless fact 3, SCD intro 2, use cases 2)* --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: SCD Type 1, 2, 3 Comparison **SCD Type 1 — Overwrite (No History Preserved):** | Customer_Key (PK) | Customer_Name | City | Effective_Date | |---|---|---|---| | 101 | Rahul | Kochi | 2020-01-15 | | 102 | Priya | Chennai | 2020-03-20 | When Rahul moves to Delhi: | Customer_Key (PK) | Customer_Name | City | Effective_Date | |---|---|---|---| | 101 | Rahul | **Delhi** ← overwritten | 2020-01-15 | | 102 | Priya | Chennai | 2020-03-20 | **No history preserved.** Simple, fast, but loses the fact that Rahul was once in Kochi. --- **SCD Type 2 — Add New Row (Full History Preserved):** | Customer_Key (PK) | Customer_Name | City | Start_Date | End_Date | Is_Current | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 101 | Rahul | Kochi | 2020-01-15 | 2024-08-01 | 0 | | 102 | Priya | Chennai | 2020-03-20 | 9999-12-31 | 1 | | 103 | Rahul | **Delhi** | 2024-08-01 | 9999-12-31 | 1 | **Full history preserved.** Existing fact rows pointing to key 101 still correctly report Kochi sales; new fact rows will point to key 103 for Delhi sales. **Most recommended for data warehouses.** --- **SCD Type 3 — Add New Column (Limited History Preserved):** | Customer_Key (PK) | Customer_Name | Current_City | Previous_City | Effective_Date | |---|---|---|---|---| | 101 | Rahul | Delhi | Kochi | 2024-08-01 | | 102 | Priya | Chennai | NULL | 2020-03-20 | **Only the previous value is kept** in a separate column. Cannot track changes older than the most recent one. Used when only "current vs. previous" comparison is needed. --- **Comparison Summary Table:** | Aspect | SCD Type 1 | SCD Type 2 | SCD Type 3 | |---|---|---|---| | History preserved? | ❌ No | ✅ Full history | ⚠️ Limited (current + previous) | | Storage growth | None | High (new row per change) | Low (extra column) | | Implementation | UPDATE | INSERT + UPDATE old row | UPDATE + extra column | | Query complexity | Simplest | Moderate (filter by is_current) | Simplest | | Best for | Correctable errors, no audit need | Auditing, trend analysis | Comparing current vs previous only | | KTU frequency | Low | **Very High** | Medium | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:** > 1. **Forgetting to declare the GRAIN** before designing the schema — costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Mixing star and snowflake concepts** — confusing denormalized and normalized dimensions in the same diagram. > 3. **Using natural keys instead of surrogate keys** in the dimension table PKs. > 4. **Omitting foreign key columns** in the fact table when drawing the schema. > 5. **Not stating whether a measure is additive/semi-additive/non-additive** — losing a clear concept mark. > 6. **SCD answers missing the trigger event** — must explain WHEN Type 2 is applied (e.g., "when ETL detects `OLD.city ≠ NEW.city`"). *(7 marks — three types explained 5, comparison table 2)* --- ## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Bullet Summary — Module 2 Dimensional Modeling:** - ✅ **Dimensional Modeling** = Fact (numbers) + Dimension (descriptions); introduced by Ralph Kimball. - ✅ **Fact Table** = numeric measures at a declared atomic **grain**; has only FKs + measures. - ✅ **Dimension Table** = descriptive context (who, what, where, when, why); wide and denormalized. - ✅ **Additive measures** can be summed across all dimensions; **Semi-additive** across some; **Non-additive** must be averaged. - ✅ **Surrogate Keys** are mandatory in DW — system-generated integers, not natural source keys. - ✅ **Star Schema** = denormalized dimensions radiating from a central fact → faster queries, more storage. - ✅ **Snowflake Schema** = normalized dimension hierarchies → less storage, more joins, slower queries. - ✅ **Fact Constellation (Galaxy)** = multiple fact tables sharing conformed dimensions — used in enterprise DW. - ✅ **Factless Fact Table** = no measures, only FKs; used for event tracking and coverage analysis. - ✅ **SCD Type 1** = overwrite (no history). - ✅ **SCD Type 2** = insert new row with effective dates + is_current flag (full history) — most common. - ✅ **SCD Type 3** = add previous-value column (limited history). - ✅ **Grain declaration** is the **first step** in every DW design — non-negotiable. - ✅ **Conformed Dimensions** = shared across multiple fact tables (e.g., `dim_date`). - ✅ **Star-join optimization** is a query-optimizer technique that exploits the star topology for fast joins.

topics.title: Steps in Dimensional Modeling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT412 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Steps in Dimensional Modeling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
**Dimensional Modeling (DM)** is a logical design technique used in data warehousing that structures data into intuitive, business-facing models optimized for query performance and analytical processing. The **Steps in Dimensional Modeling** refer to the **Kimball Four-Step Design Process** — a disciplined, sequence-driven methodology pioneered by Ralph Kimball for designing star-schema-based data marts from business requirements.

The four canonical steps are:
1. **Select the Business Process**
2. **Declare the Grain**
3. **Identify the Dimensions**
4. **Identify the Facts**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** These four steps form the foundational framework for designing any dimensional schema. In KTU University examinations, at least one 14-mark question is frequently asked from the combined application of all four steps on a case study.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are running a **large supermarket chain** and want to analyze daily sales. The store generates thousands of transactions, each with items, prices, cashiers, and timestamps. 

If you simply dump these into a single massive spreadsheet, finding answers like *"Which product sold the most on rainy Saturdays in Kerala?"* becomes nearly impossible.

**Dimensional Modeling** is like organizing your warehouse into two simple zones:
- A central **fact table** (the cash register tape — just the numbers: quantity, revenue).
- Surrounding **dimension tables** (the descriptive context: *Who* bought, *What* product, *When* it was sold, *Where*, *Why* via promotion).

The four-step process is the **blueprint** you follow before laying down even a single brick of that warehouse.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** Dimensional modeling prioritizes *user understandability* and *query speed* over *storage normalization* — a deliberate trade-off for analytical workloads.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

#### Step 1: Select the Business Process
A **business process** is a low-level operational activity captured by an organization's transactional systems. It is the *event* you want to measure.

- **Goal:** Identify the *what* of the data mart.
- **Examples:** Order processing, shipments, inventory movements, insurance claims, ATM transactions, student enrollment.
- **Selection Criteria:**
  - Must be a discrete, measurable activity.
  - Must produce or consume **metrics** (numeric values).
  - Must be supported by operational source systems.
  - Should be expressible as a *verb* phrase (e.g., *"process sales"*).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The business process is NOT a department (e.g., "Marketing"). It is the *operational activity* of that department.

#### Step 2: Declare the Grain
**Grain** is the atomic level of detail represented by a single row in the fact table. It is the *most critical and irreversible* decision in the four-step process.

- **Rule of Thumb:** Declare the **finest grain available** from the source data.
- **Example Grain Statements:**
  - *One row per sales transaction line item.*
  - *One row per ATM withdrawal event.*
  - *One row per student-course enrollment semester.*
- **Why It Matters:** Every dimension and fact must be **consistent with the declared grain**. Mixing grains creates the dreaded *"double-counting"* error.

> [!WARNING]
> Once grain is declared, it cannot be relaxed without rebuilding the model. Always choose the **lowest atomic grain**.

#### Step 3: Identify the Dimensions
Dimensions provide the **descriptive context** (the *who, what, when, where, why, how*) surrounding the numeric measurements.

- **Classic Dimension Categories:**
  - **Who:** Customer, Employee, Supplier, Student.
  - **What:** Product, Service, Course.
  - **When:** Date (Day/Month/Quarter/Year).
  - **Where:** Store, Warehouse, Region, Branch.
  - **Why:** Promotion, Campaign, Reason Code.
  - **How:** Channel, Payment Mode, Device.

- **Dimension Characteristics:**
  - Contain textual, categorical, and slowly changing attributes.
  - Served as **conformed dimensions** across multiple data marts.
  - Implemented as separate dimension tables connected to the fact via **foreign keys**.

#### Step 4: Identify the Facts
**Facts** are the numeric measurements of the business process, captured at the declared grain.

- **Classification of Facts:**
  - **Additive Facts:** Can be summed across *all* dimensions (e.g., `sales_amount`, `quantity_sold`).
  - **Semi-Additive Facts:** Can be summed across *some* dimensions but not others (e.g., `account_balance` cannot be summed over time).
  - **Non-Additive Facts:** Cannot be summed meaningfully (e.g., `unit_price`, `temperature`, ratios).

### KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Term | Symbol / Keyword | Definition / Rule | KTU Exam Focus |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Business Process | $BP$ | Operational activity producing metrics | Definition + Example |
| Grain | $G$ | Atomic level of one fact row | "Lowest possible detail" |
| Dimension | $D_i$ | Descriptive context table ($i = 1, 2, \dots, n$) | Who/What/When/Where/Why |
| Fact | $F_j$ | Numeric measurement ($j = 1, 2, \dots, m$) | Additive $\vert$ Semi $\vert$ Non |
| Star Schema | $S = (F, \{D_i\})$ | Central fact + radiating dimensions | Diagram-based question |
| Additivity Rule | $\Sigma F \rightarrow \text{valid}$ | Numeric aggregation across all dims | Classification problems |
| Cardinality | $1 : N$ | One dimension row to many fact rows | Schema design choices |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These four steps are applied by Data Engineers, BI Developers, and Data Architects in industries like e-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart), banking (HDFC, SBI), telecom (Jio), and healthcare for building OLAP cubes, executive dashboards, and predictive analytics platforms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Formal Representation of the Four-Step Process

Let us mathematically formalize the four-step methodology so that the relationships between business process, grain, dimensions, and facts become rigorously defined for analytical reasoning.

#### Step 1: Business Process Selection
Let the universe of all operational processes be defined as:

$$
P = \{p_1, p_2, p_3, \dots, p_k\}
$$

The selected business process $p_s$ must satisfy:

$$
p_s \in P \quad \text{such that} \quad \exists \text{ metrics } M = \{m_1, m_2, \dots, m_n\} \text{ produced by } p_s
$$

**Logic:** Every chosen process must emit at least one **measurable metric**; otherwise it yields no facts.

#### Step 2: Grain Declaration
The grain $G$ defines the cardinality of the fact table $F$. Let $R$ represent a single fact row:

$$
G : 1 \text{ row } R \equiv 1 \text{ atomic event } e
$$

The total row count of the fact table is:

$$
\mid F \mid = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mid e_i \mid
$$

where $n$ is the number of atomic events captured. Choosing a finer grain increases $\mid F \mid$, granting higher analytical flexibility.

#### Step 3: Dimensions Identification
Let the set of dimensions be:

$$
D = \{D_1, D_2, D_3, \dots, D_n\}
$$

Each dimension $D_i$ is in the third normal form (3NF) and relates to the fact table via a foreign key relationship:

$$
\text{FK}_i : F \rightarrow D_i \quad \text{with cardinality} \quad 1 : N
$$

This means one dimension row (e.g., a single product) joins to many fact rows (e.g., many sales of that product).

#### Step 4: Facts Identification
Let the fact table schema be:

$$
F(\text{PK}_F, \text{FK}_1, \text{FK}_2, \dots, \text{FK}_n, m_1, m_2, \dots, m_n)
$$

The **additivity property** of a metric $m_j$ across dimensions $D = \{D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n\}$ is:

$$
\text{Additive}(m_j) = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } \Sigma_{D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n} \, m_j \text{ is meaningful} \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

For **semi-additive** facts, additivity holds across a subset $D^{\prime} \subset D$:

$$
\Sigma_{D^{\prime}} \, m_j \text{ is meaningful}, \quad \Sigma_{D \setminus D^{\prime}} \, m_j \text{ is NOT meaningful}
$$

**Worked Example (Case Study):** A retail chain wants to build a **Sales Data Mart**.

| Step | Decision Output | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. Business Process | "Retail Sales Transactions" | Verifiable from POS source system |
| 2. Grain | One row per product per transaction line item | Lowest atomic capture |
| 3. Dimensions | Date, Product, Customer, Store, Promotion | Covers Who/What/When/Where/Why |
| 4. Facts | quantity\_sold, sales\_amount, discount\_amount, profit | Numeric and mostly additive |

**Additivity Check:**

$$
\Sigma_{\text{Date, Product, Customer, Store, Promotion}} \, \text{sales\_amount} = \text{valid total revenue}
$$

This confirms `sales_amount` is a fully additive fact.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: The Four-Step Dimensional Modeling Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["STEP 1: SELECT BUSINESS PROCESS"] --> node2["Identify operational activity to model"]
    node2 --> node3["Confirm measurable metrics exist"]
    node3 --> node4{"Source data available?"}
    node4 -->|YES| node5["STEP 2: DECLARE THE GRAIN"]
    node4 -->|NO| nodeA["STOP: Reconsider scope"]
    node5 --> node6["Define atomic level of one fact row"]
    node6 --> node7["Choose LOWEST possible detail"]
    node7 --> node8["STEP 3: IDENTIFY DIMENSIONS"]
    node8 --> node9["Who What When Where Why How"]
    node9 --> node10["Build descriptive context tables"]
    node10 --> node11["STEP 4: IDENTIFY FACTS"]
    node11 --> node12["Select numeric measurements"]
    node12 --> node13["Classify as Additive Semi-Additive Non-Additive"]
    node13 --> node14["FINAL: STAR SCHEMA MODEL READY"]
    node14 --> node15["Implement Fact Table + Dimension Tables"]
    node15 --> node16["Validate Additivity and Grain Consistency"]
```

### Diagram 2: Resulting Star Schema Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FT["FACT TABLE\nSALES_FACT\nPK: TransactionID\nFKs: DateKey ProductKey CustomerKey StoreKey PromoKey\nMetrics: SalesAmt QtySold Profit"] --- DK["DIM DATE\nDateKey Day Month Quarter Year IsHoliday"]
    FT --- PK["DIM PRODUCT\nProductKey ProductName Category Brand UnitPrice"]
    FT --- CK["DIM CUSTOMER\nCustomerKey CustName Gender City State Segment"]
    FT --- SK["DIM STORE\nStoreKey StoreName City Region StoreType"]
    FT --- PRK["DIM PROMOTION\nPromoKey PromoName DiscountPct MediaType Duration"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The central node `FT` represents the fact table. The five radiating nodes (`DK`, `PK`, `CK`, `SK`, `PRK`) represent the five dimensions identified in Step 3. Each metric listed inside the fact table corresponds to one fact identified in Step 4.

### Diagram 3: Grain Declaration Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1["Candidate Grain Options"] --> G2["Daily Sales Summary"]
    G1 --> G3["Per Transaction Line Item"]
    G1 --> G4["Per Hour Per Store Summary"]
    G3 --> G5["LOWEST ATOMIC LEVEL"]
    G2 --> G6["Coarser Grain"]
    G4 --> G6
    G5 --> G7["SELECTED: MAXIMUM FLEXIBILITY"]
    G6 --> G8["Avoid: Limits drill-down capability"]
    G7 --> G9["Proceed to Dimension Identification"]
    G8 --> G9
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Grain" in dimensional modeling. Why is declaring grain considered the most critical step?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Grain** is the atomic level of detail represented by a single row in the fact table. It defines *what* each row signifies (e.g., one row per transaction line item). Declaring grain is the most critical step because (1) it irreversibly fixes the structure of the fact table, (2) all dimensions and facts must conform to this grain, and (3) a wrong grain leads to double-counting or missing analytical capability.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Differentiate between Additive, Semi-Additive, and Non-Additive facts with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Additive Fact:** Can be summed across all dimensions. Example: `sales_amount`, `quantity_sold`.
- **Semi-Additive Fact:** Can be summed across some dimensions but not all. Example: `account_balance` (cannot be summed over time).
- **Non-Additive Fact:** Cannot be summed meaningfully. Example: `unit_price`, `profit_margin_percent`.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

*(a) [7 Marks]* Explain the **four-step dimensional modeling process** proposed by Ralph Kimball. For each step, clearly state its purpose and the key decision made.

*(b) [7 Marks]* Consider a **university examination system** that wants to build a data mart. Apply the four-step process and design the star schema. Identify the business process, declare the grain, list at least four dimensions, and identify at least four facts.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — The Four-Step Process:**

1. **Step 1 — Select the Business Process:** Identify the operational activity (e.g., sales, exams) to model. The process must produce measurable metrics and be supported by source systems. *(1.5 Marks)*

2. **Step 2 — Declare the Grain:** State the atomic detail of one fact row. The rule is to use the *finest atomic grain* available. *(1.5 Marks)*

3. **Step 3 — Identify the Dimensions:** Determine the descriptive context — *who, what, when, where, why, how* — that accompanies the numeric measures. *(2 Marks)*

4. **Step 4 — Identify the Facts:** Choose the numeric measurements and classify them as additive, semi-additive, or non-additive. *(2 Marks)*

**Part (b) — University Examination Data Mart Design:**

| Step | Output |
| :--- | :--- |
| Business Process | Conducting end-semester examinations and publishing results |
| Grain | One row per student per subject per examination attempt |
| Dimensions | Dim\_Date, Dim\_Student, Dim\_Subject, Dim\_Faculty, Dim\_Department |
| Facts | marks\_scored, total\_marks, attendance\_percent, grade\_points |

**Sample Star Schema Representation:**

- **Fact Table:** `EXAM_RESULT_FACT (ResultID, DateKey, StudentKey, SubjectKey, FacultyKey, DepartmentKey, marks_scored, total_marks, attendance_percent, grade_points)`
- **Sample Dimension Attributes:**
  - `Dim_Student`: StudentKey, StudentName, Gender, Programme, Semester, BatchYear.
  - `Dim_Subject`: SubjectKey, SubjectName, CourseCode, Credits, Type (Theory/Lab).

*(7 Marks breakdown: Process Identification 1.5M, Grain 1.5M, Dimensions 2M, Facts 2M)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Set]**

*(a) [7 Marks]* A **hospital chain** wants to analyze **patient treatment outcomes** across its 25 branches. Apply the four-step dimensional modeling process to design the schema. Clearly list the business process, grain, dimensions (at least 5), and facts (at least 4).

*(b) [7 Marks]* Explain the **classification of facts** in dimensional modeling with suitable examples. State why additivity matters when designing a fact table.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Hospital Treatment Outcomes Data Mart:**

| Step | Decision |
| :--- | :--- |
| Business Process | Patient treatment episodes (admission to discharge) |
| Grain | One row per patient per treatment per day |
| Dimensions | Dim\_Patient, Dim\_Doctor, Dim\_Branch, Dim\_Date, Dim\_Diagnosis, Dim\_Treatment |
| Facts | treatment\_duration, treatment\_cost, recovery\_score, readmission\_flag |

**Additivity Check:**

- `treatment_cost` is **additive** across patient, doctor, branch, date.
- `recovery_score` is **non-additive** (averaging required).
- `treatment_duration` is **semi-additive** (can be summed across patient/branch but not date).

**Part (b) — Classification of Facts:**

| Type | Meaning | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Additive | Summed across all dimensions | Revenue, Quantity Sold |
| Semi-Additive | Summed across some dimensions only | Account Balance, Inventory Level |
| Non-Additive | Cannot be summed; use average/ratio | Unit Price, Profit Margin % |

**Why Additivity Matters:** Additivity determines whether a metric can be aggregated across user-driven dimensional cuts (e.g., drill-down from Year $\rightarrow$ Quarter $\rightarrow$ Month $\rightarrow$ Day). Non-additive facts require pre-computed aggregations stored separately to avoid misleading results.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Business Process with Business Department:** Students often write "Marketing" or "Sales Department" instead of *"process sales orders"*. Always phrase it as an operational *verb-based activity*.
> 2. **Choosing Coarse Grain:** Declaring "monthly summary" grain kills the analytical flexibility. Always default to the **lowest atomic level**.
> 3. **Mixing Grain Within a Fact Table:** Storing both per-transaction and daily summary rows in the same fact table leads to inflated aggregation. Strict grain consistency is mandatory.
> 4. **Treating All Numbers as Additive:** Ratios, percentages, and balances are NOT additive. Misclassification costs up to **2 marks** in Part B.
> 5. **Omitting Foreign Keys:** In the star schema diagram, missing dimension foreign keys inside the fact table is a **frequently penalized** diagrammatic error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **four-step process** (Business Process $\rightarrow$ Grain $\rightarrow$ Dimensions $\rightarrow$ Facts) is the canonical Kimball methodology.
- **Step 1 (Business Process)** must be a measurable, verb-based operational activity.
- **Step 2 (Grain)** is the atomic level — always choose the *lowest* possible.
- **Step 3 (Dimensions)** cover Who, What, When, Where, Why, How and serve descriptive context.
- **Step 4 (Facts)** are numeric measures classified as Additive, Semi-Additive, or Non-Additive.
- **Star Schema** = 1 Fact Table + N Dimension Tables radiating outward.
- **Foreign keys** in the fact table link to **primary keys** of dimension tables.
- **Conformed dimensions** can be reused across multiple data marts.
- **Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD Types 1, 2, 3)** are an advanced extension beyond Step 3.
- **Additivity** must be validated BEFORE the schema is finalized to avoid misleading OLAP results.
- **Order of execution** is strict; do NOT skip ahead — each step constrains the next.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Process $\rightarrow$ Grain $\rightarrow$ Dimensions $\rightarrow$ Facts."* Memorize this sequence. In KTU 14-mark questions, this exact ordering is worth a minimum of **2 marks** for procedural correctness alone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fact Tables and Dimension Tables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT412 - DATA WAREHOUSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fact Tables and Dimension Tables # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the **Dimensional Modelling** paradigm proposed by **Ralph Kimball**, a data warehouse is logically divided into two structurally and semantically distinct table types that together form the **Star Schema** (and its variants). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fact Table:** A *fact table* is the **central, large, growing table** in a star schema that stores the **quantitative business measurements (facts/measures)** of a business process, along with the **foreign keys** that link it to the surrounding dimension tables. Every row corresponds to a **business event** measured at a specific intersection of dimensions, known as the **grain** of the fact table. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dimension Table:** A *dimension table* is a **descriptive, relatively static, wide table** that stores the **textual/qualitative attributes** (who, what, when, where, why, how) of the business. Each dimension row is uniquely identified by a **surrogate primary key**, which appears as a foreign key inside the fact table. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you run a chain of **supermarkets in Kerala**. At the end of every day, you want to know: - **How much revenue** did each **store** generate? - From which **product category**? - On which **date**? - Promoted through which **promotion**? - Sold to which **customer segment**? | Real-world element | Data warehouse element | |---|---| | Each individual **bill/receipt** | One **row in the fact table** | | Amount, quantity, profit on that bill | **Measures (facts)** in the row | | The **product** sold | One **row in the Product dimension** | | The **date** of sale | One **row in the Date dimension** | | The **store** where sold | One **row in the Store dimension** | | The **promotion** applied | One **row in the Promotion dimension** | > [!NOTE] > **Intuition summary:** Think of a **fact table** as the *spreadsheet of numbers* (a giant Excel of revenue, qty, profit), and a **dimension table** as the *dictionary* that gives meaning to each column heading. The fact tells you **"how much"** — the dimension tells you **"of what, when, where, and to whom."** --- ## 1.3 Granularity — The Most Critical Design Decision > [!IMPORTANT] > **Grain (Granularity):** The *grain* of a fact table is the **atomic level** at which data is captured — i.e., the precise *business event* each row represents. Examples: > - One row = one line item on a POS receipt *(transaction grain)* > - One row = total sales per store per day *(periodic snapshot grain)* > - One row = one order lifecycle from placement to delivery *(accumulating snapshot grain)* Once grain is declared, **all candidate measures and dimensions must conform** to it. Violating grain is the #1 modelling mistake — a fact called *“monthly sales by store”* cannot legally contain daily transactions. --- ## 1.4 Characteristics at a Glance | Property | Fact Table | Dimension Table | |---|---|---| | Row count | **Very large (millions/billions)** | Relatively small (thousands/millions) | | Number of columns | **Wide but mostly numeric** | Wide and mostly textual | | Purpose | Stores **measurements** | Stores **descriptive context** | | Keys | Composed of **FKs to dimensions** (+ degenerate keys) | Single **surrogate PK** | | Growth | **Additive** — grows with events | **Slowly changing** — SCD handling required | | Indexing | Heavy **bitmap & join indexes** | Heavy **b-tree** on PK | | Cardinality | **High** | **Low** | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Fact Table A fact table row is essentially a **tuple of foreign keys + numeric measures**, with each foreign key referencing exactly one row in its parent dimension. ``` ┌────────────┐ │ DIM_DATE │ PK: date_key └─────┬──────┘ │ FK ┌────────────┐ ┌──▼─────────────┐ ┌────────────┐ │ DIM_STORE │──│ FACT_SALES │──│ DIM_PROD │ └────────────┘ │ date_key (FK) │ └────────────┘ │ store_key(FK) │ │ prod_key (FK) │ │ promo_key(FK) │ │ ─ measures ─ │ │ revenue │ │ units_sold │ │ discount_amt │ │ profit │ └────────────────┘ ``` ### 2.1.1 The Four Pillars Inside a Fact Table 1. **Foreign Keys (FKs):** One FK per conforming dimension. Their combination **uniquely identifies** the row (composite key). 2. **Numeric Measures (Facts):** The business KPIs. Three sub-classes: - **Additive:** Sum-able across *all* dimensions (e.g., `revenue`, `units_sold`). - **Semi-additive:** Sum-able across *some* but not all dimensions (e.g., `account_balance` — cannot sum across time). - **Non-additive:** Cannot be summed (e.g., `unit_price`, `profit_margin_%`). Often stored as pre-computed ratios or decomposed into additive components. 3. **Degenerate Dimensions (DD):** Operational identifiers (e.g., *invoice_number*, *order_id*) that live in the fact table itself because they have no associated descriptive dimension. 4. **Audit / Timestamp Columns:** `load_date`, `etl_batch_id`, `source_system` — for lineage. --- ## 2.2 The Three Canonical Types of Fact Tables > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists three fact table families. Memorising their grain and date-handling is **high-yield** for 14-mark questions. | Type | Grain (one row represents…) | Date Column | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Transaction** | A single business event at a point in time | `transaction_date` (event time) | One POS line item | | **Periodic Snapshot** | The **state** of a process at regular intervals (e.g., daily) | `snapshot_date` | Daily bank-account balance | | **Accumulating Snapshot** | The **lifecycle** of a process with multiple milestones | **Multiple date columns** (one per milestone) | Order: order_date, ship_date, delivery_date | ### Key behavioural differences - **Transaction fact** → rows inserted continuously; measures are fully **additive**. - **Periodic Snapshot** → rows are densely *re-loaded* for every period; **semi-additive measures** are common. - **Accumulating Snapshot** → row is **updated** as the process moves through milestones; the row count stays constant once the pipeline stabilises. --- ## 2.3 Anatomy of a Dimension Table A dimension table provides the **“who, what, when, where, why, how”** context to numeric facts. It is **denormalised** (flat) in a star schema — deliberately sacrificing 3NF to maximise query performance. ### 2.3.1 Structural Components | Component | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Surrogate Primary Key** | System-generated integer, **independent of source-system keys**; enables SCD handling | `customer_key = 1042` | | **Natural Key** | Original business identifier (often retained as an attribute, not the PK) | `customer_id = "CUST-IND-77821"` | | **Outrigger Attributes** | Foreign keys to *other* dimensions (used in snowflake variants) | `region_key → DIM_REGION` | | **Hierarchy Attributes** | Roll-up levels stored in a single flat row | `country, state, city, pincode` | | **Slowly Changing Attributes** | Fields whose values change *slowly* over time | `customer_marital_status` | ### 2.3.2 The Surrogate Key — Why It Is Mandatory > [!NOTE] > KTU frequently tests *“Why use a surrogate key instead of natural keys in a DW?”* — memorise the four reasons: > 1. **Performance:** Integer joins are **4–10× faster** than string/compound natural keys. > 2. **SCD handling:** Natural keys *change* with SCD Type 2; surrogate keys stay **stable & unique**. > 3. **Integration:** Different source systems may use the *same* natural key with *different meanings* — surrogate keys are warehouse-unique. > 4. **Insulation from operational changes:** Renumbering in OLTP does not break DW joins. --- ## 2.4 Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD) — The Six Official Types | SCD Type | Behaviour | New Row? | History Preserved? | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Type 0** | Attribute is *never* changed | No | N/A | Date of birth | | **Type 1** | **Overwrite** old value | No | No | Typo correction | | **Type 2** | **Insert new row**, track with `effective_date`, `end_date`, `is_current` flag | **Yes** | Yes | Address change | | **Type 3** | Add a **new column** for previous value | No | One prior version | Previous employer | | **Type 4** | Store history in a **separate “history” mini-dimension** | No | Yes (in mini-dim) | Fast-changing attrs | | **Type 6** | Hybrid 1 + 2 + 3 (current value + prior value column + history rows) | Yes | Yes (full) | Comprehensive tracking | > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU **most-frequently** asks SCD **Type 1 vs Type 2 comparison** (table form, 7 marks) and the **mechanics of SCD Type 2** (algorithm, 7 marks). --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > *Dimensional modelling is largely qualitative, so this "formula sheet" lists the canonical design equations, sizing rules, and ratios examiners love to test.* | # | Rule / Equation | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{Grain} = \arg\max_{\text{atoms}} \lbrace \text{event atomicity} \rbrace$ | Choose the **lowest atomic grain** the business needs | | 2 | $\text{PrimaryKey}_{\text{FACT}} = \bigotimes_{i=1}^{n} \text{FK}_{i}$ | Fact PK is the **concatenation** of all dimension FKs | | 3 | $\text{Additivity}_{\text{measure}} : \sum_{d \in D} m_d = m_{\text{rollup}}$ | An additive measure can be summed across *every* dimension | | 4 | $\text{FactRows} \approx \lvert \text{EventStream} \rvert \times \text{Period}$ | Transaction fact rows ≈ events × time | | 5 | $\text{SnowflakeDepth} = \lvert \text{OutriggerLevels} \rvert$ | Number of normalised hierarchy hops | | 6 | $\text{Type2Rows}_{\text{new}} = \text{history}_{\text{old}} \cup \{\text{new version}\}$ | Old row expires, new row is inserted | | 7 | $\text{SurrogateKey} : \mathbb{N} \to \text{Dim}$ | Integer monotonic generator; not derived from natural key | | 8 | $\lvert \text{Fact} \rvert / \lvert \text{Dim} \rvert \;\gg\; 1$ | Fact cardinality should be **orders of magnitude larger** than dimension | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Banking & Retail** — Daily snapshot fact of account balances joined to customer, branch, and product dimensions for risk analytics. - **Telecommunications** — Accumulating-snapshot fact of subscriber lifecycle (activation → plan change → deactivation). - **Healthcare & IoT** — Transaction fact of sensor readings joined to patient/device/time dimensions for anomaly detection. - **E-commerce (Flipkart/Amazon)** — Periodic snapshot of inventory joined to warehouse/SKU/date dimensions for supply-chain dashboards. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — Building a Sales Fact from Raw POS Data This is the canonical KTU 14-mark *“design a fact and dimension model”* problem. We will derive the structure **from the ground up** using the four-step Kimball methodology. ### Step 1 — Choose the Business Process The process we are modelling: **Retail Point-of-Sale (POS) sales transactions** at a supermarket chain. $$ \text{Process} = \text{POS Sales} $$ ### Step 2 — Declare the Grain > [!NOTE] > The **most atomic grain** that is useful to the business and that the source system can support is: > *“One row per individual product line on a customer’s POS receipt, scanned at a particular store on a particular date and time, under (optionally) one promotion.”* $$ \text{Grain}_{\text{SALES}} = \text{POS\_receipt} \times \text{product line} \times \text{store} \times \text{date/time} \times \text{promotion} $$ This choice is **non-negotiable** — once declared, every fact and dimension must conform to it. ### Step 3 — Identify the Dimensions (Conforming) Each orthogonal “who/what/where/when/why” context that qualifies the chosen grain becomes a dimension: $$ D = \{ \text{DIM\_DATE}, \text{DIM\_STORE}, \text{DIM\_PRODUCT}, \text{DIM\_PROMOTION}, \text{DIM\_CUSTOMER}, \text{DIM\_CASHIER} \} $$ Each of these will become its **own dimension table** with a surrogate `*_key` primary key. ### Step 4 — Identify the Facts (Numeric Measures) From the POS line-item we can extract: | Measure | Source Column | Additivity | |---|---|---| | `units_sold` | qty scanned | Fully additive | | `gross_revenue` | qty × list_price | Fully additive | | `discount_amount` | coupon/promo deduction | Fully additive | | `net_revenue` | gross − discount | Fully additive | | `cost_of_goods` | qty × unit_cost | Fully additive | | `gross_profit` | net_revenue − cost | Fully additive | | `tax_amount` | GST computed | Fully additive | | `unit_price` | list price | **Non-additive** (store as-is) | ### Step 5 — Build the Final Schema (Analytical Matrix) The resulting fact table, expressed as a set notation of attributes: $$ \text{FACT\_SALES} = \{ \text{date\_key}, \text{store\_key}, \text{product\_key}, \text{promo\_key}, \text{customer\_key}, \text{cashier\_key}, \text{receipt\_no}, \text{line\_no}, \text{units\_sold}, \text{gross\_revenue}, \text{discount\_amount}, \text{net\_revenue}, \text{cost\_of\_goods}, \text{gross\_profit}, \text{tax\_amount} \} $$ Where the **composite primary key** is: $$ \text{PK}_{\text{FACT\_SALES}} = \text{date\_key} \otimes \text{store\_key} \otimes \text{product\_key} \otimes \text{promo\_key} \otimes \text{customer\_key} \otimes \text{cashier\_key} \otimes \text{receipt\_no} \otimes \text{line\_no} $$ The pair `(receipt_no, line_no)` is a **degenerate dimension** because it carries no separate descriptive context. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Fact vs Dimension Side-by-Side | Analytical Lens | Fact Table | Dimension Table | |---|---|---| | **Data role** | Stores the *quantitative verb* (sold, paid, shipped) | Stores the *qualitative noun* (store, product, date) | | **Cardinality** | Equal to number of business events | Equal to number of unique business entities | | **Time behaviour** | Append-only / slowly overwritten (snapshot) | Slowly changing across versions | | **Storage** | **90 %** of total DW volume (typical) | **10 %** of total DW volume (typical) | | **Index strategy** | Bitmap indexes on low-cardinality FKs; partitioning on date | B-tree on PK; bitmap on filter attrs | | **Update frequency** | Inserts dominate | Inserts + updates (SCD) | | **Optimised for** | `SUM`, `COUNT`, `AVG` aggregations | `SELECT … WHERE` filtering & label retrieval | | **NULL policy** | Foreign keys **never null**; measures **null only if truly unknown** | Attributes are usually **NOT NULL** with default value | | **ETL dependency** | Loads **after** dimensions | Loads **before** facts (dimensions must exist first) | | **Naming convention** | Prefix `FACT_` (e.g., `FACT_SALES`) | Prefix `DIM_` (e.g., `DIM_PRODUCT`) | --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example — SCD Type 2 Mechanics > [!IMPORTANT] > SCD Type 2 is the most-asked KTU algorithm. We will solve it end-to-end so it is fully reproducible in the exam hall. **Scenario:** A customer `CUST-001` lives in *Kochi* in 2020 and later moves to *Trivandrum* in 2024. The DW must preserve both addresses historically. ### Step 1 — Examine the *incoming* row from the source system $$ \text{Source row} = \{ \text{cust\_id}=\text{CUST-001}, \; \text{name}=\text{Anand}, \; \text{city}=\text{Trivandrum}, \; \text{state}=\text{Kerala} \} $$ ### Step 2 — Look up the current dimension row in DW $$ \text{Existing DW row} = \{ \text{customer\_key}=101, \; \text{cust\_id}=\text{CUST-001}, \; \text{city}=\text{Kochi}, \; \text{effective\_date}=2020\text{-}01\text{-}01, \; \text{end\_date}=9999\text{-}12\text{-}31, \; \text{is\_current}=Y \} $$ ### Step 3 — Compare *tracked* attributes (city changed) $$ \Delta = \{ \text{city}: \text{Kochi} \rightarrow \text{Trivandrum} \} \neq \emptyset $$ A change is detected → SCD Type 2 path activates. ### Step 4 — **Expire** the old row Update the existing current row: $$ \text{customer\_key}=101 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{end\_date}=2024\text{-}06\text{-}15, \; \text{is\_current}=N $$ ### Step 5 — **Insert** the new version Generate a new surrogate key and insert a new row: $$ \text{customer\_key}=102, \; \text{cust\_id}=\text{CUST-001}, \; \text{city}=\text{Trivandrum}, \; \text{effective\_date}=2024\text{-}06\text{-}16, \; \text{end\_date}=9999\text{-}12\text{-}31, \; \text{is\_current}=Y $$ ### Step 6 — Final DW state (analytical view) | customer_key | cust_id | city | effective_date | end_date | is_current | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 101 | CUST-001 | Kochi | 2020-01-01 | 2024-06-15 | N | | 102 | CUST-001 | Trivandrum | 2024-06-16 | 9999-12-31 | Y | > [!NOTE] > **Historical reporting** uses *both* rows. **Current-state reporting** filters `WHERE is_current = 'Y'`. The fact table FK stores `customer_key` at the time of sale, so historical facts correctly link to the address that was valid at the time of the event. --- ## 3.4 Additivity Classification — Worked Example | Measure | Across Date | Across Store | Across Product | Conclusion | |---|---|---|---|---| | `units_sold` | Sum ✓ | Sum ✓ | Sum ✓ | **Fully additive** | | `discount_amount` | Sum ✓ | Sum ✓ | Sum ✓ | **Fully additive** | | `account_balance` | **Last-value ✗** | Sum ✓ | Sum ✓ | **Semi-additive** (time) | | `unit_price` | Avg only ✗ | Avg only ✗ | Avg only ✗ | **Non-additive** | | `profit_margin_pct` | Ratio ✗ | Ratio ✗ | Ratio ✗ | **Non-additive** | > [!WARNING] > A common student error is to declare *all* numeric measures as additive. If the measure is a **ratio/percentage/average/snapshot value**, mark it **non-additive or semi-additive**. --- ## 3.5 Star Schema vs Snowflake Schema — Analytical Comparison | Parameter | Star Schema | Snowflake Schema | |---|---|---| | **Dimension normalisation** | Denormalised (flat) | Normalised (hierarchy split) | | **Number of tables** | 1 fact + N dimensions | 1 fact + N + outrigger tables | | **Join complexity** | Few joins (1–3) | More joins (4–8) | | **Query speed** | **Faster** (fewer joins) | Slower (more joins) | | **Storage** | Slightly higher redundancy | Lower redundancy | | **Maintenance** | Easier (SCD in one place) | Harder (cascading updates) | | **Kimball recommendation** | **Preferred for DW** | Acceptable when space critical | | **ETL complexity** | Lower | Higher | > [!NOTE] > KTU may ask: *“Justify why Kimball recommends star schema in spite of violating 3NF.”* Answer template: > 1. **Query performance dominates** in DW (read-heavy). > 2. Storage cost is **negligible** compared to query latency cost. > 3. Pre-joined hierarchies simplify BI tools (Tableau, Power BI, Cognos). > 4. Surrogate keys + SCD are easier to manage in a single denormalised row. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Star Schema for Retail Sales ```mermaid graph TD dimDate["DIM_DATE
date_key PK
full_date
day, month, quarter, year
is_holiday, fiscal_year"] dimStore["DIM_STORE
store_key PK
store_name
city, state, region
store_type, open_date"] dimProd["DIM_PRODUCT
product_key PK
sku, product_name
category, subcategory
brand, unit_price"] dimPromo["DIM_PROMOTION
promo_key PK
promo_name
discount_pct
start_date, end_date"] dimCust["DIM_CUSTOMER
customer_key PK
cust_id
name, gender, age
city, segment, join_date"] dimCash["DIM_CASHIER
cashier_key PK
cashier_id, name
shift, store_key FK"] factSales["FACT_SALES
date_key FK
store_key FK
product_key FK
promo_key FK
customer_key FK
cashier_key FK
receipt_no DD
line_no DD
--- MEASURES ---
units_sold
gross_revenue
discount_amount
net_revenue
cost_of_goods
gross_profit
tax_amount"] dimDate --> factSales dimStore --> factSales dimProd --> factSales dimPromo --> factSales dimCust --> factSales dimCash --> factSales ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Snowflake Schema (Same Sales Domain) ```mermaid graph TD dimDate["DIM_DATE"] dimStore["DIM_STORE
store_key PK"] dimCity["DIM_CITY
city_key PK"] dimState["DIM_STATE
state_key PK"] dimRegion["DIM_REGION
region_key PK"] dimProd["DIM_PRODUCT
product_key PK"] dimCat["DIM_CATEGORY
category_key PK"] dimDept["DIM_DEPARTMENT
dept_key PK"] dimPromo["DIM_PROMOTION"] dimCust["DIM_CUSTOMER"] factSales["FACT_SALES
date_key, store_key
product_key, promo_key
customer_key
units_sold, net_revenue"] dimDate --> factSales dimStore --> factSales dimStore --> dimCity dimCity --> dimState dimState --> dimRegion dimProd --> factSales dimProd --> dimCat dimCat --> dimDept dimPromo --> factSales dimCust --> factSales ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Three Fact-Table Type Comparison ```mermaid graph LR subgraph TXN["TRANSACTION FACT"] T1["Row 1: 10:00 Sale A"] T2["Row 2: 10:05 Sale B"] T3["Row 3: 10:12 Sale C"] end subgraph SNAP["PERIODIC SNAPSHOT FACT"] S1["Jan 1: Store Balances"] S2["Jan 2: Store Balances"] S3["Jan 3: Store Balances"] end subgraph ACC["ACCUMULATING SNAPSHOT FACT"] A1["Order ORD-1: placed"] A2["Order ORD-1: shipped"] A3["Order ORD-1: delivered"] end TXN -.->|"Inserts only, fully additive measures"| TXNnote["Single event_date column
Row grows continuously"] SNAP -.->|"Re-loaded per period, semi-additive measures"| SNAPnote["snapshot_date column
Same row-key each period"] ACC -.->|"Row updated as milestones hit, multiple date columns"| ACCnote["Multiple date columns
Row count stabilises"] ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — SCD Type 2 Lifecycle (Customer Example) ```mermaid graph TD S0["SOURCE: customer CUST-001
city = Kochi, year 2020"] S1["SOURCE: customer CUST-001
city = Trivandrum, year 2024"] D0["DW Row customer_key = 101
city = Kochi
effective_date = 2020-01-01
end_date = 9999-12-31
is_current = Y"] D1Expire["UPDATE old row 101
end_date = 2024-06-15
is_current = N"] D1Insert["INSERT new row 102
city = Trivandrum
effective_date = 2024-06-16
end_date = 9999-12-31
is_current = Y"] Final["DW Final State
Row 101: Kochi (history)
Row 102: Trivandrum (current)"] S0 --> D0 D0 --> S1 S1 --> D1Expire S1 --> D1Insert D1Expire --> Final D1Insert --> Final ``` --- ## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — ETL Load Order (Dimensions Before Facts) ```mermaid graph TD SRC["Source Systems
(OLTP, Files, APIs)"] STG["Staging Area
raw copies"] DIM["Dimension Tables
(loaded FIRST)"] FACT["Fact Tables
(loaded AFTER dims)"] DM["Data Mart / BI
Tableau, Power BI"] SRC -->|"extract"| STG STG -->|"cleanse, conform, surrogate-key"| DIM STG -->|"lookup FKs from dims"| FACT DIM --> FACT FACT --> DM DIM --> DM ``` > [!NOTE] > This sequence is **mandatory**: a fact row that loads before its dimension is created will be **orphaned** (FK violation) and silently dropped — a classic ETL failure mode examiners love to highlight. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between fact table and dimension table in a data warehouse. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Fact Table | Dimension Table | |---|---|---| | **Content** | Stores quantitative business measures (numerical) | Stores descriptive textual attributes | | **Keys** | Foreign keys to dimensions + degenerate keys | Single surrogate primary key | | **Cardinality** | Very high (millions/billions of rows) | Low (thousands/millions) | | **Example** | `FACT_SALES` storing `revenue`, `units_sold` | `DIM_PRODUCT` storing `product_name`, `category`, `brand` | > **Example fact row:** `(date=12-Jun, store=TVM, product=Milk, revenue=₹60, qty=2)` > **Example dimension row:** `(product_key=11, sku=MLK-500, name=Amul Toned Milk 500ml, category=Dairy, brand=Amul)` *(Examiner’s note: 1 mark per cell-pair, 1 mark for the example pair.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **What is a surrogate key? Why is it preferred over natural keys in a data warehouse?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **surrogate key** is a *system-generated, warehouse-unique, integer* identifier assigned to every dimension row. It has *no business meaning* and is independent of any operational source-system key. **Reasons surrogate keys are preferred (any 3 of 4):** 1. **Performance:** Integer joins are 4–10× faster than string natural keys. 2. **SCD compatibility:** Natural keys change with SCD Type 2; surrogate keys are stable. 3. **Cross-system integration:** Two OLTP systems may reuse the same natural key — surrogate keys guarantee warehouse uniqueness. 4. **Insulation:** Renumbering in source OLTP does not break warehouse joins or fact history. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three types of fact tables** in a data warehouse. For each, give the grain, the date-column pattern, and one real-world example. **(b) [7 Marks]** A retail company wants to track **monthly inventory levels per store per product**. Design the appropriate **fact table schema** with all relevant dimensions, measures, and a **dimension table** for `DIM_PRODUCT`. Justify your choice of fact-table type. --- ### ✅ Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Three Types of Fact Tables | # | Type | Grain (one row =) | Date Column Pattern | Real-world Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Transaction** | A single business event | `event_date_time` (single column) | One POS line item, one ATM withdrawal | | 2 | **Periodic Snapshot** | State of a process at regular intervals (e.g., daily/monthly) | `snapshot_date` (single column, dense) | Daily inventory level, monthly account balance | | 3 | **Accumulating Snapshot** | Lifecycle of a process with multiple milestones | **Multiple date columns**, one per milestone (e.g., `order_date`, `ship_date`, `delivery_date`) | Order fulfilment, insurance claim, patient hospitalisation | **Key distinguishing traits (must mention for full marks):** - **Transaction:** inserts only; **fully additive** measures; row count grows unboundedly. - **Periodic Snapshot:** re-loaded each period; **semi-additive** measures common (e.g., balance); row count = entities × periods. - **Accumulating Snapshot:** **row is updated** as the process advances; row count stabilises after pipeline saturates; multiple date FKs. *(Marking: 1 mark per row in table, 1 mark for key traits explanation.)* --- #### Part (b) — Monthly Inventory Fact Schema **Step 1 — Choose the grain:** > One row = **month-end inventory snapshot** for a given `(store, product)` combination. $$ \text{Grain} = (\text{store} \times \text{product} \times \text{month-end date}) $$ **Step 2 — Choose fact-table type:** This is a **Periodic Snapshot Fact** because: - Inventory is a **state** measured at a **regular interval** (month-end). - The measure `quantity_on_hand` is **semi-additive** — it cannot be summed across time, only across store × product. **Step 3 — Fact Table Schema:** ``` FACT_INVENTORY_MONTHLY ------------------------- date_key (FK → DIM_DATE) -- month-end date store_key (FK → DIM_STORE) product_key (FK → DIM_PRODUCT) warehouse_key (FK → DIM_WAREHOUSE) quantity_on_hand (semi-additive) quantity_received (additive) quantity_shipped (additive) quantity_adjusted (additive, +/-) unit_cost (non-additive) inventory_value (semi-additive) ``` **Step 4 — DIM_PRODUCT Schema:** ``` DIM_PRODUCT ------------- product_key (PK, surrogate) sku (natural key from OLTP) product_name brand category subcategory unit_of_measure unit_list_price unit_cost effective_date end_date is_current (for SCD Type 2) ``` **Step 5 — Justification (must appear in answer):** - Periodic snapshot chosen because grain is *state at regular interval*, not a single event. - `quantity_on_hand` declared **semi-additive** — students must state it cannot be summed across time. - Foreign keys chosen for **all** orthogonal dimensions; the **composite PK** = `date_key ⊗ store_key ⊗ product_key ⊗ warehouse_key`. - `DIM_PRODUCT` uses a **surrogate key** to support SCD Type 2 on `unit_list_price` and `category`. *(Marking: 1 mark grain, 1 mark fact-type justification, 3 marks schema, 1 mark DIM_PRODUCT, 1 mark SCD/surrogate justification.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — **[Alternative ESE Choice]** **(a) [7 Marks]** What are **Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD)**? Explain **SCD Type 1, Type 2, and Type 3** with neat diagrams and state one business scenario suitable for each. **(b) [7 Marks]** A hospital wants to track every **patient visit** including diagnosis, doctor, department, and charges. The patient may have changed their address or phone number between visits. Design a **star schema** with appropriate fact and dimension tables. Show how the patient dimension handles address change using **SCD Type 2**. --- ### ✅ Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — SCD Overview + Types 1, 2, 3 **Definition:** SCD are **dimension attributes** that change *slowly and unpredictably* over time, requiring deliberate handling to preserve (or not preserve) historical accuracy. Ralph Kimball classified them into 6 types; the three most important are: **SCD Type 1 — Overwrite (no history):** ``` BEFORE: customer_key=101 city=Kochi UPDATE: customer_key=101 city=Trivandrum (old value LOST) ``` - **Scenario:** Correcting a data-entry typo (e.g., `emial` → `email`). - **Pros:** Simple, no extra storage. **Cons:** History is permanently lost. **SCD Type 2 — Add New Row (full history):** ``` BEFORE: 101 | CUST-001 | Kochi | 2020-01-01 | 9999-12-31 | Y CHANGE detected → EXPIRE old, INSERT new AFTER: 101 | CUST-001 | Kochi | 2020-01-01 | 2024-06-15 | N 102 | CUST-001 | Trivandrum | 2024-06-16 | 9999-12-31 | Y ``` - **Scenario:** Patient address change between hospital visits — must link past visits to old address and future visits to new address. - **Pros:** Complete history preserved; fact-FK can reference the correct version at the time of the event. **Cons:** More rows, more storage, query must filter `is_current = Y` for current state. **SCD Type 3 — Add New Column (limited history):** ``` BEFORE: customer_key=101 current_city=Kochi previous_city=NULL UPDATE: customer_key=101 current_city=Trivandrum previous_city=Kochi ``` - **Scenario:** Tracking *one* prior employer or *one* prior address for a customer. - **Pros:** No row multiplication. **Cons:** Only **one** level of history kept; not scalable. **Comparison Table (include for full marks):** | Aspect | Type 1 | Type 2 | Type 3 | |---|---|---|---| | History kept? | No | Yes (full) | Yes (one version) | | New row? | No | Yes | No | | New column? | No | No | Yes (`previous_*`) | | Storage growth | None | Linear with changes | Constant | | Query complexity | Lowest | Medium | Low | *(Marking: 1 mark definition, 2 marks each type with diagram/scenario = 6, 1 mark for comparison.)* --- #### Part (b) — Hospital Visit Star Schema with SCD Type 2 **Step 1 — Declare the grain:** > One row = **one line item on one patient-visit bill** (e.g., one consultation charge, one lab test, one procedure). **Step 2 — Identify dimensions:** $$ D = \{ \text{DIM\_PATIENT}, \text{DIM\_DOCTOR}, \text{DIM\_DEPARTMENT}, \text{DIM\_DIAGNOSIS}, \text{DIM\_DATE}, \text{DIM\_VISIT\_TYPE} \} $$ **Step 3 — Fact table schema `FACT_VISIT`:** ``` FACT_VISIT ----------- visit_date_key FK -> DIM_DATE patient_key FK -> DIM_PATIENT (SCD Type 2 enabled) doctor_key FK -> DIM_DOCTOR department_key FK -> DIM_DEPARTMENT diagnosis_key FK -> DIM_DIAGNOSIS visit_type_key FK -> DIM_VISIT_TYPE visit_id DEGENERATE DIMENSION line_no DEGENERATE DIMENSION ---------------------------- charge_amount (additive) discount_amount (additive) paid_amount (additive) quantity (additive) ``` **Step 4 — DIM_PATIENT with SCD Type 2 columns:** ``` DIM_PATIENT ------------- patient_key PK (surrogate, e.g. 2001, 2002, ...) patient_natural_id natural key from HIS patient_name gender date_of_birth address_line1 city state pincode phone ---------------------------- SCD Type 2 metadata effective_date end_date is_current (Y/N) ``` **Step 5 — Show SCD Type 2 mechanics on address change:** > Patient P-100 moves from Kochi → Trivandrum in 2024. | patient_key | natural_id | city | effective_date | end_date | is_current | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 2001 | P-100 | Kochi | 2018-03-01 | 2024-04-30 | **N** | | 2002 | P-100 | Trivandrum | 2024-05-01 | 9999-12-31 | **Y** | - Old row **2001** is *expired* (end_date set, is_current flipped to N). - New row **2002** is *inserted* with a new surrogate key. - All **prior fact rows** continue to reference `patient_key = 2001` (Kochi era), so historical reporting on Kochi visits remains accurate. - All **future fact rows** will reference `patient_key = 2002` (Trivandrum era). *(Marking: 1 mark grain, 2 marks fact schema, 2 marks DIM_PATIENT schema, 2 marks SCD Type 2 mechanics.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing star with snowflake** in schema diagrams — remember *one* level of outrigger makes it a snowflake. > 2. **Not declaring grain** before listing measures — examiners deduct 2–3 marks if grain is missing. > 3. **Treating all measures as additive** — `unit_price`, `profit_margin_%`, `balance` are *non/semi-additive*. > 4. **Using natural keys as PK in dimension** — always use a *surrogate* integer key in DW. > 5. **Drawing a generic ER diagram** instead of a *star/snowflake* — Dimensional Modelling diagrams are mandatory. > 6. **Omitting the `is_current` flag and `end_date`** when describing SCD Type 2 — these are the *defining* metadata columns. > 7. **Loading fact before dimensions** in ETL sequence answers — wrong load order loses 2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ A **fact table** stores *quantitative measures* at a *declared grain*; a **dimension table** stores *descriptive context* for those facts. - ✅ Always **declare the grain first** — grain dictates which measures and dimensions are legal. - ✅ The **primary key of a fact table is the concatenation of all its foreign keys** to dimensions, plus any degenerate dimension components. - ✅ **Three fact-table types:** Transaction (event), Periodic Snapshot (state-at-interval), Accumulating Snapshot (lifecycle with multiple date columns). - ✅ **Three additivity classes:** Fully additive, semi-additive (e.g., balances across time), non-additive (ratios, prices). - ✅ **Dimension tables are denormalised** in a star schema — Kimball accepts the 3NF violation for query speed. - ✅ Always use a **surrogate integer key** in dimensions; never use natural keys as the dimension PK. - ✅ **SCD Type 1** = overwrite, no history. **SCD Type 2** = insert new row, full history, uses `effective_date`, `end_date`, `is_current`. **SCD Type 3** = add a `previous_*` column, one prior value only. - ✅ **Degenerate dimensions** (e.g., invoice number) live in the fact table because they have no separate descriptive dimension. - ✅ **Star schema =** 1 fact + N denormalised dimensions. **Snowflake schema =** 1 fact + N dimensions + outrigger hierarchy tables. - ✅ **ETL load order:** Source → Staging → **Dimensions first** → **Facts second** → Data Marts / BI tools. - ✅ **Kimball’s four-step design:** Choose process → declare grain → identify dimensions → identify measures. Memorise this sequence — it is the canonical answer skeleton for 14-mark design questions. - ✅ **90/10 volume rule:** Fact tables hold ~90 % of DW rows; dimensions hold ~10 %. - ✅ Foreign keys in fact tables are **never NULL**; measures may be NULL only when the measure is genuinely unknown. - ✅ Bitmap indexes on low-cardinality dimension FKs in the fact table; B-tree on dimension surrogate PKs.

topics.title: Data Warehouse Design: Top-Down & Bottom-Up, Fact Table Granularity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT412 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Warehouse Design: Top-Down & Bottom-Up, Fact Table Granularity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Data Warehouse Design — The Strategic Question

Before a single byte of data is stored, the architect must decide **HOW** the warehouse will be conceived. Two opposing yet complementary philosophies dominate the industry: **Bill Inmon's Top-Down** approach and **Ralph Kimball's Bottom-Up** approach. Coupled with this architectural decision is the most critical physical design choice — **Fact Table Granularity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Data Warehouse Design** is the disciplined process of translating business information requirements into a logical and physical data storage architecture that supports OLAP, reporting, and analytical decision-making. It is broadly classified into **Top-Down Design** (Enterprise-driven, normalized, Inmon-style) and **Bottom-Up Design** (Data Mart-driven, dimensional, Kimball-style).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Building a City

Imagine you are commissioned to design a **city** (the Data Warehouse).

| Approach | Real-World Analogy | City Builder's Mindset |
|----------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Top-Down** | Architect the masterplan first (roads, zoning, utilities), then build districts. | "Plan the entire enterprise data model first, then build subject-area data marts." |
| **Bottom-Up** | Identify a thriving neighborhood (a data mart), replicate its success, then connect them with highways. | "Deliver a quick-win Data Mart for Sales, then expand to Finance, HR, etc." |
| **Granularity** | Deciding if a camera records every second or every hour. | "How detailed should each transactional row in the fact table be?" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** The two approaches are not enemies — modern hybrid architectures often begin Bottom-Up (for speed) and consolidate Top-Down (for governance). KTU examiners love this nuance.

## 1.3 What is Fact Table Granularity?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Fact Table Granularity** is the **atomic (lowest) level of detail** at which facts (measurements) are stored in a fact table. It defines *what a single row represents*. Choosing granularity is a **once-and-for-all architectural decision** because changing it later requires reloading the entire warehouse.

**Plain English Intuition:** Granularity answers the question — *"What does ONE ROW in my fact table count?"*
- If one row = one transaction line item → **Transaction Granularity** (most atomic)
- If one row = one daily sales total per store → **Daily Snapshot Granularity**
- If one row = one month's total per store → **Periodic Snapshot** (coarsest)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing granularity with the *number of rows*. Granularity is about the **semantic meaning of a row**, not the row count. A billion-row table can be coarse-grained if each row represents a month.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

## 2.1 The Top-Down Design Approach (Inmon's School)

Bill Inmon, the "Father of Data Warehousing," advocates an **Enterprise-Wide** perspective.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)
1. **Build the Enterprise Data Model First** — Analyze all business processes, entities, and relationships across the entire organization.
2. **Create the Normalized Central Warehouse** — A subject-oriented, integrated, time-variant, non-volatile (the famous 4 traits) **3NF/ER-modeled** repository.
3. **Derive Departmental Data Marts** — Smaller, denormalized slices are then provisioned for HR, Finance, Sales, etc.
4. **Iterate and Refine** — Data marts continuously pull from the central warehouse.

### Why and How
- **Why?** Eliminates data inconsistency, ensures a single version of truth.
- **How?** Centralized ETL enforces enterprise standards before any reporting.

## 2.2 The Bottom-Up Design Approach (Kimball's School)

Ralph Kimball champions a **pragmatic, dimensional** methodology.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)
1. **Identify a High-Value Business Process** (e.g., Sales, Orders).
2. **Build a Conformed Dimensional Data Mart** — Using the **Star Schema** (one fact + many dimensions).
3. **Validate with End-Users** — Quick ROI from the first data mart.
4. **Expand by Adding More Data Marts** — Use *conformed dimensions* (shared across marts) to stitch them into a "bus architecture."

### Why and How
- **Why?** Faster delivery, iterative feedback, business user buy-in.
- **How?** Dimensional modeling trades normalization for query performance.

## 2.3 Comparative Cheat Sheet (KTU Favorite)

| Parameter | Top-Down (Inmon) | Bottom-Up (Kimball) |
|----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Starting Point** | Enterprise Data Model | Single Business Process |
| **Central Artifact** | Normalized Warehouse (3NF) | Dimensional Data Mart (Star) |
| **Modeling Technique** | ER Modeling, 3NF | Dimensional (Star/Snowflake) |
| **Time to First Deliverable** | High (months) | Low (weeks) |
| **Data Consistency** | Excellent (centralized) | Depends on Conformed Dimensions |
| **Cost & Risk** | High upfront, low long-term | Low upfront, risk of redundancy |
| **Best Suited For** | Large enterprises, regulated industries | SMBs, agile analytics teams |
| **KTU Term to Remember** | "Enterprise-Driven" | "Data-Mart-Driven" |

## 2.4 Fact Table Granularity — Deep Dive

There are **four canonical granularities** every KTU student must memorize:

> [!NOTE]
> **The 4 Levels of Fact Granularity**
> 1. **Transaction Granularity** — One row per business event (e.g., one sales line). Most flexible, largest in size.
> 2. **Periodic Snapshot Granularity** — One row per time period (e.g., daily inventory level). Used for cumulative measures.
> 3. **Accumulating Snapshot Granularity** — One row per *lifecycle*, with date columns updated as the entity passes milestones (e.g., order → ship → deliver).
> 4. **Factless Fact Table Granularity** — One row per event occurrence (e.g., student attended class) — no numeric measures, just keys.

## 2.5 The Four-Step Grain Declaration Process (Kimball)

A grain declaration is the **single most important line in your design document**. KTU examiners test this.

1. **Declare the grain** in business terms (e.g., "One row per sales ticket line item at a store on a given day").
2. **Conform dimensions** to the grain.
3. **Identify numeric facts** consistent with the grain.
4. **Verify** that all proposed facts are valid at that grain (a "discount amount" is valid at a line item, but a "store's total monthly rent" is NOT).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Banking:** Top-Down ensures regulatory compliance (Basel III reporting consistency).
- **E-Commerce:** Bottom-Up delivers rapid recommendation-engine data marts.
- **Manufacturing:** Transaction grain supports root-cause defect analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Granularity Resolution

## 3.1 Analytical Framework: When to Choose Which Approach

Below is the **decision matrix** that KTU answers expect for a 14-mark design question.

Let:
- $T$ = Time-to-delivery pressure (in weeks)
- $N$ = Number of business processes (subject areas)
- $R$ = Regulatory/audit risk level (1 to 5)
- $C$ = Conformed-dimension maturity (0 to 1)

**Heuristic Score Function:**

$$
S_{TD} = (R \times 2) + (N \times 1.5) - (T \times 0.1)
$$

$$
S_{BU} = (T \times 0.3) + (C \times 5) - (R \times 0.5)
$$

Choose **Top-Down** if $S_{TD} > S_{BU}$, else **Bottom-Up**.

### Worked Example
Given: $T = 12$ weeks, $N = 6$ subject areas, $R = 4$, $C = 0.7$.

$$
S_{TD} = (4 \times 2) + (6 \times 1.5) - (12 \times 0.1) = 8 + 9 - 1.2 = 15.8
$$

$$
S_{BU} = (12 \times 0.3) + (0.7 \times 5) - (4 \times 0.5) = 3.6 + 3.5 - 2.0 = 5.1
$$

Since $S_{TD} = 15.8 \gg S_{BU} = 5.1$, the **Top-Down** approach is recommended.

## 3.2 Granularity Resolution — Exhaustive Worked Example

**Scenario:** A retail chain wants a sales fact table. The architect is choosing grain.

### Step 1 — List Candidate Grains
- Option A: One row per transaction (line item).
- Option B: One row per transaction (header, total).
- Option C: One row per store per day.

### Step 2 — Check Business Requirements
Marketing wants to analyze "**which product is often bought with which other product**" (market basket analysis).

### Step 3 — Apply the Grain Test
- Option A passes (line item has product).
- Option B fails (header has no product detail).
- Option C fails (aggregated, no product detail).

### Step 4 — Declare Final Grain
> **"One row represents one product line on a customer sales ticket at a store on a given date."**

### Step 5 — Conformance Check (Dimension Compatibility)

$$
\text{Grain} = \text{Store} \times \text{Date} \times \text{Ticket} \times \text{Product}
$$

Every dimension must be **at least** as atomic as this grain. A "Monthly Date" dimension would violate the grain.

### Step 6 — Fact Validity Matrix

| Proposed Fact | Valid at Line-Item Grain? |
|--------------|---------------------------|
| Sales Amount | $\checkmark$ Yes |
| Discount % | $\checkmark$ Yes |
| Store Monthly Rent | $\times$ No (different grain) |
| Tax Collected | $\checkmark$ Yes |

## 3.3 Storage Estimation (KTU Numerical Favorite)

Let:
- $G$ = Number of grain rows per day per store
- $S$ = Number of stores
- $D$ = Number of days in retention window (typically **1825 days = 5 years**)
- $F$ = Average fact row size in bytes (typically **20 bytes** for transaction grain)

$$
\text{Total Rows} = G \times S \times D
$$

$$
\text{Total Size (GB)} = \frac{\text{Total Rows} \times F}{1024^3}
$$

### Numerical Walk-Through
Given: $G = 1500$ line items/day/store, $S = 200$ stores, $D = 1825$ days, $F = 20$ bytes.

$$
\text{Total Rows} = 1500 \times 200 \times 1825 = 547{,}500{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Total Size (GB)} = \frac{547{,}500{,}000 \times 20}{1024^3} \approx 10.2 \text{ GB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Storage Scalability Insight:** Doubling the grain (e.g., from line-item to header) roughly **halves** the storage requirement. Coarsening the grain is a performance/storage trade-off, not a free lunch.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Down Design Flow (Inmon)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Enterprise Business Requirements] --> B[Enterprise Data Model 3NF]
    B --> C[Central Data Warehouse Normalized]
    C --> D[Subject Area Data Mart 1]
    C --> E[Subject Area Data Mart 2]
    C --> F[Subject Area Data Mart N]
    D --> G[Departmental Reporting]
    E --> G
    F --> G
```

## 4.2 Bottom-Up Design Flow (Kimball)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Business Process 1 Sales] --> M1[Data Mart 1 Star Schema]
    P2[Business Process 2 Inventory] --> M2[Data Mart 2 Star Schema]
    P3[Business Process 3 Finance] --> M3[Data Mart 3 Star Schema]
    M1 --> BUS[Bus Architecture Conformed Dimensions]
    M2 --> BUS
    M3 --> BUS
    BUS --> DW[Logical Enterprise Data Warehouse]
```

## 4.3 Granularity Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L4[Coarsest: Yearly Summary per Region]
    L3[Periodic Snapshot: Daily per Store]
    L2[Accumulating Snapshot: Order Lifecycle]
    L1[Atomic Transaction: One Sales Line Item]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
```

## 4.4 Fact Table Granularity Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Identify Business Process] --> S2[List Candidate Grains]
    S2 --> S3{Does it support all business questions?}
    S3 -->|No| S2
    S3 -->|Yes| S4[Declare Grain in Business Terms]
    S4 --> S5[Identify Conformed Dimensions]
    S5 --> S6[Validate Facts at Grain]
    S6 --> S7{Any Mismatch?}
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[Reconcile or Split Fact Table]
    S7 -->|No| S9[Finalize Schema]
    S8 --> S6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Fact Table Granularity. Why is it called the most important design decision in dimensional modeling?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fact Table Granularity is the **atomic level of detail** that one row in a fact table represents (1 mark). It is the most important design decision because it dictates the **meaning of every row**, constrains the choice of dimensions, and determines what facts can be validly stored (1 mark). Once declared, changing granularity requires **reloading the entire warehouse**, making it irreversible and high-impact (1 mark).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any three differences between the Top-Down and Bottom-Up approaches to data warehouse design.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Starting Point:** Top-Down starts with the enterprise data model; Bottom-Up starts with a single business process.
2. **Modeling Style:** Top-Down uses normalized (3NF) schemas; Bottom-Up uses dimensional (Star) schemas.
3. **Time-to-Deliverable:** Top-Down has higher initial delivery time; Bottom-Up delivers the first data mart in weeks.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **Inmon Top-Down** design approach in detail with a neat block diagram. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Kimball Bottom-Up** design approach. Explain the concept of **conformed dimensions** and the **bus architecture** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Inmon's Top-Down Approach (7 marks):**
- The Top-Down approach, proposed by **Bill Inmon**, begins by constructing an **enterprise-wide normalized data model** that captures all business entities and relationships (1 mark).
- A **centralized, subject-oriented, integrated, time-variant, non-volatile** data warehouse is built first using **3NF/ER modeling** (1 mark).
- Departmental **data marts** are then derived from this central warehouse to serve specific analytical needs (1 mark).
- **Diagram (2 marks):** See the Top-Down Flow diagram in Section 4.1.
- **Advantages (1 mark):** Data consistency, single version of truth, enterprise-wide governance, lower long-term maintenance.
- **Limitations (1 mark):** High upfront cost, longer time to first insight, requires deep enterprise analysis before any reporting.

**(b) Kimball's Bottom-Up Approach with Conformed Dimensions (7 marks):**
- Kimball's approach starts with a **single high-value business process** (e.g., Sales) and builds a **dimensional data mart** using a **star schema** (1 mark).
- Subsequent data marts are added iteratively, sharing **conformed dimensions** (e.g., a shared `Dim_Date` or `Dim_Customer`) to enable cross-mart analysis (2 marks).
- The **Bus Architecture** is the matrix/blueprint listing all business processes and the conformed dimensions they share, acting as the integration contract (2 marks).
- **Example (1 mark):** A `Dim_Date` shared between Sales and Inventory marts allows "Sales vs. Stock" cross-analysis.
- **Advantages (1 mark):** Quick ROI, iterative, business-user-friendly.

#### OR

### Question B — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Define **Fact Table Granularity**. Explain the **four canonical granularities** with one real-world example each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A retail company plans a sales warehouse. The candidate grains are: (i) One row per transaction line item, (ii) One row per daily store total, (iii) One row per monthly store total. Marketing needs to perform **market-basket analysis** and **seasonal trend analysis**. Recommend the appropriate grain with justification. Compute the estimated storage in GB for **5 years** if: 1500 line items per day per store, 200 stores, **5 years = 1825 days**, 20 bytes per row. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Four Canonical Granularities (7 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Granularity is the atomic level of detail represented by one row in a fact table.
