- **Transaction Granularity (1.5 marks):** One row per business event. *Example:* One row per line item on a sales ticket.
- **Periodic Snapshot Granularity (1.5 marks):** One row per time period. *Example:* Daily inventory level per store.
- **Accumulating Snapshot Granularity (1.5 marks):** One row per entity lifecycle, with milestone dates. *Example:* Order-to-Ship-to-Delivery pipeline row.
- **Factless Fact Table (1.5 marks):** One row per event occurrence, no measures. *Example:* Student attendance fact.

**(b) Grain Recommendation and Storage Calculation (7 marks):**
- **Requirement Analysis (2 marks):** Market-basket analysis requires **product-level detail** (only line-item grain qualifies). Seasonal trend analysis requires **time-series** data (line-item can be aggregated up to any time bucket).
- **Recommended Grain (2 marks):** Option (i) — **One row per transaction line item** — because it is the *only* option supporting market-basket analysis. The other grains can be derived from it but not vice versa.
- **Storage Calculation (3 marks):**
  - Total Rows $= 1500 \times 200 \times 1825 = 547{,}500{,}000$
  - Total Size $= \dfrac{547{,}500{,}000 \times 20}{1024^3} \approx \mathbf{10.2 \text{ GB}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "granularity" with "row count."** A table can be coarse-grained yet have millions of rows. Marks are deducted when students write "higher granularity means more rows" — it means **finer/more atomic** detail.
> 2. **Do NOT skip the grain declaration step.** Every dimensional design MUST begin with a single declarative sentence: *"One row represents..."* Omitting this in a 14-mark answer costs 2-3 marks.
> 3. **In Top-Down vs Bottom-Up, never say one is "better."** Examiners want a justified **contextual comparison** aligned with project requirements.
> 4. **Storage units trap:** $1024^3$ is the correct divisor for GB. Using $1000^3$ loses a mark.
> 5. **Conformed dimensions** must be explicitly defined — students often confuse them with shared "lookup tables" without the master-data governance aspect.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Top-Down (Inmon):** Enterprise $\rightarrow$ 3NF Warehouse $\rightarrow$ Data Marts. Slow, consistent, governed.
- **Bottom-Up (Kimball):** Business Process $\rightarrow$ Star Schema Data Mart $\rightarrow$ Bus Architecture. Fast, iterative, dimensional.
- **Hybrid is Real:** Most modern enterprises use a **hybrid** (Bottom-Up delivery inside a Top-Down governance wrapper).
- **Conformed Dimensions** are the glue of the Kimball Bus Architecture — shared, standardized across marts.
- **Granularity is Irreversible** — declare it first, derive facts second, never reverse.
- **Four Granularities:** Transaction, Periodic Snapshot, Accumulating Snapshot, Factless.
- **Grain Declaration Format:** *"One row represents [entity] per [time] at [location]..."*
- **Storage Estimation:** Always use $1024^3$ for GB; remember 5 years $\approx$ **1825 days**.
- **KTU Hot Trivia:** Kimball's *Data Warehouse Toolkit* and Inmon's *Building the Data Warehouse* are the canonical references — examiners accept either phrasing as long as concepts are accurate.
- **Decision Heuristic:** Heavy regulation + many subject areas $\rightarrow$ Top-Down. Tight deadlines + clear process scope $\rightarrow$ Bottom-Up.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slowly Changing Dimensions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT412 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Slowly Changing Dimensions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD)** are a set of techniques and dimensional modeling strategies used in data warehousing to manage and preserve historical context when dimensional attribute values change slowly and unpredictably over time. As defined in the **Kimball Group methodology**, an SCD is a dimension whose attributes undergo gradual mutations rather than rapid, transactional changes — making the decision of *how* to record, retain, and reflect these changes a central architectural concern in the **ETL (Extract, Transform, Load)** layer of any dimensional data warehouse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The 2024 Scheme categorizes SCD as a sub-topic under *Dimensional Modeling*. Students must be able to: (1) classify SCD Types 0–6, (2) recommend the appropriate SCD type for given business scenarios, and (3) design schema-level responses to attribute changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Operational Term — "Slowly":**
> The word "slowly" is relative to the fact-gathering cycle. A customer address change may occur *once every five years*, but a product price might change *daily*. Both qualify as SCD candidates. The *velocity* of change does not disqualify the dimension; rather, the *business requirement for historical accuracy* is what governs the SCD strategy.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Plain-English Analogy — The "Rolodex" Metaphor:**

Imagine a business keeps a **Rolodex card** for every customer.

* **Rolodex Type 0 (No Change):** The card is printed once and never updated. The address on the card is always the one given on day one.
* **Rolodex Type 1 (Overwrite):** The moment the customer moves, you erase the old address and write the new one. The previous address is gone forever — like a whiteboard being wiped clean.
* **Rolodex Type 2 (Add New Card):** You do **not** erase anything. Instead, you slide in a *new card* for the same customer, stamp it with a "Valid From" date and a "Current" flag, and update the old card with an "Expiry Date." Now the historical sequence of addresses is preserved.
* **Rolodex Type 3 (Add Column):** You keep a *single* card but add new columns — "Previous Address" and "Current Address." This only tracks one prior version.
* **Rolodex Type 4 / 6 (Mini-History Tables):** You keep the main card lean, but store *detailed change history* in a separate, dedicated "Address History" mini-Rolodex. Whenever you need to know "Where was the customer in 2019?", you query the mini-Rolodex.

This is the essence of SCD — choosing *how much* history to keep and *where* to keep it.

---

### 1.3 Why SCD Matters in Engineering Practice

* **Audit & Compliance:** Banking, healthcare, and pharmaceutical industries must legally retain prior attribute values (e.g., prior diagnoses, prior credit limits).
* **Trend Analysis:** Marketing teams need to know what a customer's *segment* was *at the time of a transaction*, not just the current segment.
* **Data Lineage:** Knowing when and why a value changed supports root-cause analysis of KPIs.

### 1.4 Standard KTU Metrics to Memorize

| Metric | Symbol / Notation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Surrogate Key** | $SK_{dim}$ | System-generated, immutable, integer-based primary key of a dimension row |
| **Natural Key** | $NK$ | The real-world business identifier (e.g., SSN, Product Code) |
| **Effective Date** | $D_{eff}$ | The date a row version became valid |
| **Expiry Date** | $D_{exp}$ | The date a row version ceased to be valid |
| **Current Flag** | $F_{cur}$ | Boolean indicating whether a row is the *latest* version |
| **Version Number** | $V_n$ | Integer counter for SCD Type 2 versions |


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of SCD Types — Structured Logic

The Kimball methodology formally recognizes **seven (7) SCD types**, numbered Type 0 through Type 6. Each represents a different trade-off between **storage cost**, **query complexity**, and **historical accuracy**.

---

#### 🔹 SCD Type 0 — Retain Original (Passive Dimension)

* **Why it exists:** Some attributes are *constants by definition* (e.g., Date of Birth, Original Date of Hire, Country of Birth). They should never be overwritten.
* **How it works:** The ETL pipeline ignores subsequent change requests for that attribute. The original value is preserved indefinitely.
* **Engineering Utility:** Used for *immutable natural attributes* in HR, identity, and birth-record systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Type 0 = "Do nothing." The dimension is effectively read-only after initial load.

---

#### 🔹 SCD Type 1 — Overwrite (No History)

* **Why it exists:** When the business does *not* care about historical values and only wants the *current* state.
* **How it works:** The old value is replaced in-place by the new value using a simple `UPDATE` statement keyed on the **Natural Key**.
* **Engineering Utility:** Correcting data-entry errors (typos in names, spelling mistakes in city names). This is the simplest and most storage-efficient method.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type 1 does NOT preserve history.** Old fact rows that referenced the old value will silently show the new value when joined retroactively. This is intentional, not a bug.

---

#### 🔹 SCD Type 2 — Add New Row (Versioned Dimension)

* **Why it exists:** This is the **most common and most important SCD type** in KTU examinations. The business requires full historical accuracy of *every* change.
* **How it works:** A new row is **inserted** for the same Natural Key, with:
  1. A new **Surrogate Key** $SK_{new}$.
  2. The new attribute value.
  3. A new **Effective Date** $D_{eff}$ = the change date.
  4. The **Current Flag** $F_{cur}$ set to `1` (or `TRUE`).
  5. The **Expiry Date** of the *previous* row updated to $D_{eff} - 1$.
  6. The **Current Flag** of the *previous* row updated to `0`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use a Surrogate Key instead of the Natural Key?**
> Because the Natural Key repeats across multiple versioned rows. The Surrogate Key guarantees each *version* of a customer/product is uniquely addressable in the fact table foreign key.

---

#### 🔹 SCD Type 3 — Add New Column (Limited History)

* **Why it exists:** When only *one prior* value needs to be tracked (e.g., "Previous Region" vs. "Current Region").
* **How it works:** The dimension table gains two new columns — `Previous_Value` and `Current_Value`. An update shifts current → previous and inserts the new value into current.
* **Engineering Utility:** Tracking a single regulatory change, like a tax bracket revision or a re-classification of a region.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Type 3 is **not scalable**. If the attribute changes a *third* time, the second-prior value is permanently lost. KTU examiners often present this scenario as a trick question.

---

#### 🔹 SCD Type 4 — Mini-Dimension (Rapidly Changing Attributes)

* **Why it exists:** When a *cluster* of attributes changes so frequently that adding rows to the main dimension would explode its size (the "Monster Dimension" problem).
* **How it works:** A separate, smaller **Mini-Dimension** table is created containing only the volatile attributes. The fact table gains an *additional* foreign key pointing to the mini-dimension. A "profile key" combines the volatile attributes.
* **Engineering Utility:** Customer segmentation scores, demographic clusters, or product lifecycle states that change monthly.

---

#### 🔹 SCD Type 5 — Type 4 + Type 1 (Mini-Dimension + Current Flag in Main)

* **Why it exists:** Combines the compactness of Type 4 with the convenience of "current profile" in the main dimension.
* **How it works:** The main dimension holds a *single* Type-1 column representing the *current* mini-dimension profile. The mini-dimension still retains the full history.
* **Engineering Utility:** When reporting tools frequently filter on "current customer segment" without needing a date-aware join.

---

#### 🔹 SCD Type 6 — Type 1 + Type 2 + Type 3 (Dual Stamping)

* **Why it exists:** Provides *both* a quick-lookup current value and full Type-2 history in a *single* dimension row.
* **How it works:**
  1. New row inserted (Type 2 behaviour).
  2. The new row's "Current Value" column is set (Type 1 behaviour).
  3. All *prior* rows for the same Natural Key have their "Current Value" column overwritten with the new value (Type 3 behaviour).
* **Engineering Utility:** Reporting scenarios where a user wants to ask, "What is the customer's *current* region?" *or* "What was the customer's region *when* this sale happened?" in the *same* query.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most-tested artifact on SCD in KTU 2024 Scheme exams. Memorize it.**

| SCD Type | History Preserved? | Schema Action | Storage Growth | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type 0** | Original only | No change | None | Birth date, immutable IDs |
| **Type 1** | None (overwritten) | `UPDATE` in-place | None | Typo corrections |
| **Type 2** | Full (unlimited) | `INSERT` new row | **High** (1 row per change) | Customer address history |
| **Type 3** | Last 1 value | `ALTER` add columns | Low (1–2 cols) | Previous vs. current region |
| **Type 4** | Full in mini-table | New mini-dim + FK in fact | Medium | Volatile cluster of attributes |
| **Type 5** | Full in mini + current in main | Type 4 + 1 column in main | Medium | "Current segment" reporting |
| **Type 6** | Full + stamped | Type 2 rows + current column | High | Hybrid reporting needs |

### 2.3 Standard ETL Pseudocode Logic (Notation-Only)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme does **not** require programming language code, but the following *notation-style* logic is acceptable in descriptive answers to demonstrate ETL understanding.

**Notation for SCD Type 2 Insert Operation:**

$$
\text{IF } (NK_{incoming} \notin \text{Dimension}) \text{ THEN}
$$

$$
\text{INSERT new row: } \langle SK_{new}, NK, Attr_{new}, D_{eff} = \text{TODAY}, D_{exp} = NULL, F_{cur} = 1 \rangle
$$

$$
\text{ELSE IF } (Attr_{incoming} \neq Attr_{existing}) \text{ THEN}
$$

$$
\text{UPDATE existing row: } D_{exp} \leftarrow D_{eff}^{new} - 1, \quad F_{cur} \leftarrow 0
$$

$$
\text{INSERT new row: } \langle SK_{new}, NK, Attr_{new}, D_{eff} = \text{TODAY}, D_{exp} = NULL, F_{cur} = 1 \rangle
$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Banking:** SCD Type 2 is mandatory for account-holder KYC (Know Your Customer) attributes to satisfy RBI/Federal audit trails.
* **Retail:** SCD Type 4 is used for product *hierarchy* changes (e.g., a brand moves from "Premium" to "Luxury" category).
* **Healthcare:** SCD Type 6 lets hospitals answer, "What was the patient's *current* insurance at admission?" AND "What was the patient's insurance *on the day of surgery*?" in a single schema.


<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — The 7-Question Decision Framework

Before choosing an SCD type, an ETL designer must answer the following **seven analytical questions**. This matrix is a standard KTU 2024 Scheme analytical deliverable.

| # | Decision Question | Type 0 | Type 1 | Type 2 | Type 3 | Type 4 | Type 5 | Type 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is history legally required? | – | – | ✅ | – | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| 2 | Is the attribute immutable? | ✅ | – | – | – | – | – | – |
| 3 | Is it a correction of a bad value? | – | ✅ | – | – | – | – | – |
| 4 | Are rapid, frequent changes expected? | – | – | – | – | ✅ | ✅ | – |
| 5 | Is "current value" reporting priority? | – | ✅ | – | ✅ | – | ✅ | ✅ |
| 6 | Is full history on a single row needed? | – | – | – | – | – | – | ✅ |
| 7 | Is storage cost a major constraint? | ✅ | ✅ | – | ✅ | – | – | – |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 14-mark Part B questions, the examiner will *always* give a business scenario and expect you to (a) name the correct SCD type, (b) justify it using the matrix, and (c) draw the resulting schema.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — SCD Type 2 Address Change

**Business Scenario (Standard KTU Question Pattern):**

> *A retail company tracks customers in a `CUSTOMER_DIM` table. On **2024-01-15**, customer `CUST_001` (Natural Key) is initially loaded with city "Kochi". On **2024-08-20**, the customer moves to "Chennai". On **2025-03-10**, the customer moves again to "Bengaluru".*

The dimension designer chooses **SCD Type 2**. Each move triggers a new row.

#### Step 1 — Initial Load (Date: 2024-01-15)

$$
\text{INSERT INTO CUSTOMER\_DIM } (SK, NK, City, D_{eff}, D_{exp}, F_{cur}) \text{ VALUES }
$$

$$
(101, CUST\_001, \text{Kochi}, 2024\text{-}01\text{-}15, NULL, 1)
$$

| SK | NK | City | D_eff | D_exp | F_cur |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | CUST_001 | Kochi | 2024-01-15 | NULL | 1 |

#### Step 2 — First Change (Date: 2024-08-20)

**Step 2a — Expire the old row:**

$$
\text{UPDATE CUSTOMER\_DIM SET } D_{exp} = 2024\text{-}08\text{-}19, \quad F_{cur} = 0
$$

$$
\text{WHERE } NK = CUST\_001 \text{ AND } F_{cur} = 1
$$

**Step 2b — Insert the new row:**

$$
\text{INSERT INTO CUSTOMER\_DIM VALUES } (102, CUST\_001, \text{Chennai}, 2024\text{-}08\text{-}20, NULL, 1)
$$

| SK | NK | City | D_eff | D_exp | F_cur |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | CUST_001 | Kochi | 2024-01-15 | **2024-08-19** | **0** |
| 102 | CUST_001 | Chennai | **2024-08-20** | NULL | **1** |

#### Step 3 — Second Change (Date: 2025-03-10)

**Step 3a — Expire the current row:**

$$
\text{UPDATE CUSTOMER\_DIM SET } D_{exp} = 2025\text{-}03\text{-}09, \quad F_{cur} = 0
$$

$$
\text{WHERE } NK = CUST\_001 \text{ AND } F_{cur} = 1
$$

**Step 3b — Insert the new row:**

$$
\text{INSERT INTO CUSTOMER\_DIM VALUES } (103, CUST\_001, \text{Bengaluru}, 2025\text{-}03\text{-}10, NULL, 1)
$$

| SK | NK | City | D_eff | D_exp | F_cur |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | CUST_001 | Kochi | 2024-01-15 | 2024-08-19 | 0 |
| 102 | CUST_001 | Chennai | 2024-08-20 | **2025-03-09** | **0** |
| 103 | CUST_001 | Bengaluru | **2025-03-10** | NULL | **1** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Insight:** The same Natural Key $NK = CUST\_001$ now has **three rows** with distinct Surrogate Keys. Any Fact Table row whose `Transaction_Date` falls in $[2024\text{-}01\text{-}15, 2024\text{-}08\text{-}19]$ will join on $SK = 101$. A transaction in $[2024\text{-}08\text{-}20, 2025\text{-}03\text{-}09]$ will join on $SK = 102$. This is the **partitioning by date** that makes SCD Type 2 historically accurate.

---

### 3.3 Derived Query Logic — SCD Type 2 Point-in-Time Join

To retrieve the *correct* historical city for a sale that occurred on **2024-12-01**, the join predicate is:

$$
\text{SELECT } F.\text{OrderID}, D.\text{City}, F.\text{Amount}
$$

$$
\text{FROM FACT\_SALES } F \text{ JOIN CUSTOMER\_DIM } D
$$

$$
\text{ON } F.\text{Cust\_SK} = D.\text{SK}
$$

$$
\text{WHERE } F.\text{OrderDate} = 2024\text{-}12\text{-}01
$$

Since the order date lies in the range $[2024\text{-}08\text{-}20, 2025\text{-}03\text{-}09]$, the join resolves to $SK = 102$, returning **Chennai** — the correct historical city. This validates the SCD Type 2 design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Rule:** Fact tables must store the **Surrogate Key** $SK$, not the Natural Key. If fact rows stored $NK = CUST\_001$, the join would return *three* city rows, and the historical accuracy would be lost.

---

### 3.4 SCD Type 6 Hybrid — Row-Level Transformation Logic

Given the same SCD Type 2 rows from §3.2, SCD Type 6 adds two extra columns: `Current_City` and `Original_City`.

**Step 1 — Initial Load:**

$$
(101, CUST\_001, \text{Kochi}, 2024\text{-}01\text{-}15, NULL, 1, \text{Kochi}, \text{Kochi})
$$

**Step 2 — After First Change (New row + propagate current to all prior rows):**

| SK | City | Current_City | Original_City |
|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | Kochi | **Chennai** | Kochi |
| 102 | Chennai | **Chennai** | Kochi |

**Step 3 — After Second Change:**

| SK | City | Current_City | Original_City |
|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | Kochi | **Bengaluru** | Kochi |
| 102 | Chennai | **Bengaluru** | Kochi |
| 103 | Bengaluru | **Bengaluru** | Kochi |

**Dual-Query Power of Type 6:**

$$
Q_1: \text{ "What is the current city of CUST\_001?"} \rightarrow \text{Filter } F_{cur} = 1 \rightarrow \text{Bengaluru}
$$

$$
Q_2: \text{ "What was the city at the time of order on 2024-12-01?"} \rightarrow \text{Join on } SK = 102 \rightarrow \text{Chennai}
$$

$$
Q_3: \text{ "What is the *current* city for *all historical* rows?"} \rightarrow \text{Use } Current\_City \text{ column directly}
$$


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — ETL Decision Logic for SCD Type Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Staging Record] --> B{Is Attribute Mutable?}
    B -- No --> C[SCD Type 0: Retain Original]
    B -- Yes --> D{History Required by Business?}
    D -- No --> E[Hypothetical Skip]
    D -- Yes --> F{How Many Prior Versions Needed?}
    F -- Zero --> G[Apply SCD Type 1: Overwrite]
    F -- Exactly One --> H[Apply SCD Type 3: Add Column]
    F -- Unlimited --> I{Change Frequency High?}
    I -- No --> J[Apply SCD Type 2: Add Row]
    I -- Yes --> K{Reporting Need Current Value on Main Dimension?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Apply SCD Type 5: Mini Dim + Current]
    K -- No --> M[Apply SCD Type 4: Mini Dimension]
    H --> N{Dual Reporting Required?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Apply SCD Type 6: Hybrid]
    N -- No --> P[End: SCD Type 3 Selected]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid ER Diagram — SCD Type 2 Star Schema Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FT[FACT_SALES Table] -->|FK: Cust_SK| CD[CUSTOMER_DIM Table]
    FT -->|FK: Prod_SK| PD[PRODUCT_DIM Table]
    FT -->|FK: Date_Key| DT[DATE_DIM Table]
    CD -->|Row 1: SK=101| R1[NK CUST_001 Kochi D_eff 2024-01-15 D_exp 2024-08-19 F_cur 0]
    CD -->|Row 2: SK=102| R2[NK CUST_001 Chennai D_eff 2024-08-20 D_exp 2025-03-09 F_cur 0]
    CD -->|Row 3: SK=103| R3[NK CUST_001 Bengaluru D_eff 2025-03-10 D_exp NULL F_cur 1]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — SCD Type 4 Mini-Dimension Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Main Dimension: NK + Stable Attributes] --> B[Fact Table: Sales]
    A --> C[Mini Dimension: Customer Profile]
    C --> C1[Profile_Key 1001: Age 25-35 Urban High Income]
    C --> C2[Profile_Key 1002: Age 36-50 Suburban Mid Income]
    C --> C3[Profile_Key 1003: Age 51+ Rural Low Income]
    B -->|FK Cust_SK| A
    B -->|FK Profile_Key| C
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Block — Type 2 vs. Type 6 Schema

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Type2[SCD Type 2 Schema]
        T2A[SK 101 City Kochi Eff 2024-01-15 Exp 2024-08-19 Cur 0]
        T2B[SK 102 City Chennai Eff 2024-08-20 Exp 2025-03-09 Cur 0]
        T2C[SK 103 City Bengaluru Eff 2025-03-10 Exp NULL Cur 1]
    end
    subgraph Type6[SCD Type 6 Schema]
        T6A[SK 101 City Kochi Cur_City Bengaluru Orig_City Kochi]
        T6B[SK 102 City Chennai Cur_City Bengaluru Orig_City Kochi]
        T6C[SK 103 City Bengaluru Cur_City Bengaluru Orig_City Kochi]
    end
```


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Slowly Changing Dimensions. List any three types of SCD with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer:**

Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD) are dimensional tables whose attribute values change gradually over time, requiring deliberate strategies to manage historical accuracy in a data warehouse.

The three types are:

* **SCD Type 1:** Overwrites the old value with the new value; no history is preserved.
* **SCD Type 2:** Inserts a new row for the change, preserving full history using Effective Date, Expiry Date, and Current Flag columns.
* **SCD Type 3:** Adds a new column to track the previous value, preserving only the *last* one prior version.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why are Surrogate Keys used in SCD Type 2 instead of Natural Keys in the Fact Table foreign key?**

**Model Answer:**

In SCD Type 2, the same Natural Key (e.g., `CUST_001`) appears in **multiple** dimension rows — one per historical version. If the Fact Table stored the Natural Key as its foreign key, the join with the dimension would return *multiple* rows per fact, breaking aggregation and historical accuracy.

A Surrogate Key (e.g., 101, 102, 103) is a **unique, system-generated, immutable** integer assigned to *each version row*. The Fact Table stores the Surrogate Key, ensuring that each transaction joins to **exactly one** historically correct dimension row based on the transaction's date.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions of 14 marks carry an **internal choice**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

#### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

A bank's data warehouse maintains a `CUSTOMER_DIM` with the following initial record on **2023-04-01**:

| Cust_SK | Cust_NK | Region | Credit_Limit | D_eff | D_exp | F_cur |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 501 | CUST_77 | South | 5,00,000 | 2023-04-01 | NULL | 1 |

The following two changes occur:
* On **2024-02-10**, the customer moves to the **West** region and credit limit is revised to **7,50,000**.
* On **2025-01-25**, the customer again moves to the **North** region with credit limit **10,00,000**.

**(a)** Identify the SCD type that should be applied. Justify your choice in **7 marks**.

**(b)** Show the **complete** dimension table state after all three events, and demonstrate with a **point-in-time query** how a transaction dated **2024-09-15** correctly resolves the customer's region. **7 marks**.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Identification and Justification (7 marks):**

The appropriate SCD type is **SCD Type 2 — Add New Row**.

* **Justification Point 1 — Full History Required:** Banking regulations (RBI, Basel norms) require the bank to retain every prior version of a customer's region and credit limit for audit. Type 1 (overwrite) would destroy this history, so it is disqualified.
* **Justification Point 2 — Unlimited History Needed:** The customer has already changed twice, and may change again. Type 3 (add column) only retains the *last* prior value, so it is insufficient.
* **Justification Point 3 — No Rapid-Changing Cluster:** The attributes change annually, not daily. This does not warrant a Type 4 Mini-Dimension.
* **Justification Point 4 — Audit & Compliance:** Each transaction must be reported under the customer's region *at the time of the transaction*, which is the hallmark of Type 2's date-partitioned rows.

**(b) Dimension Table Evolution and Query Demonstration (7 marks):**

**After Event 1 — 2023-04-01 (Initial Load):**

| Cust_SK | Cust_NK | Region | Credit_Limit | D_eff | D_exp | F_cur |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 501 | CUST_77 | South | 5,00,000 | 2023-04-01 | NULL | 1 |

**After Event 2 — 2024-02-10 (Region → West, Credit → 7,50,000):**

* Step 1: Expire row 501 with $D_{exp} = 2024\text{-}02\text{-}09$ and $F_{cur} = 0$.
* Step 2: Insert row 502 with $D_{eff} = 2024\text{-}02\text{-}10$, $D_{exp} = NULL$, $F_{cur} = 1$.

| Cust_SK | Cust_NK | Region | Credit_Limit | D_eff | D_exp | F_cur |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 501 | CUST_77 | South | 5,00,000 | 2023-04-01 | 2024-02-09 | 0 |
| 502 | CUST_77 | West | 7,50,000 | 2024-02-10 | NULL | 1 |

**After Event 3 — 2025-01-25 (Region → North, Credit → 10,00,000):**

* Step 1: Expire row 502 with $D_{exp} = 2025\text{-}01\text{-}24$ and $F_{cur} = 0$.
* Step 2: Insert row 503 with $D_{eff} = 2025\text{-}01\text{-}25$, $D_{exp} = NULL$, $F_{cur} = 1$.

| Cust_SK | Cust_NK | Region | Credit_Limit | D_eff | D_exp | F_cur |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 501 | CUST_77 | South | 5,00,000 | 2023-04-01 | 2024-02-09 | 0 |
| 502 | CUST_77 | West | 7,50,000 | 2024-02-10 | 2025-01-24 | 0 |
| 503 | CUST_77 | North | 10,00,000 | 2025-01-25 | NULL | 1 |

**Point-in-Time Query for Transaction Dated 2024-09-15:**

$$
\text{SELECT } F.\text{Txn\_ID}, D.\text{Region}, D.\text{Credit\_Limit}, F.\text{Amount}
$$

$$
\text{FROM FACT\_TXN } F \text{ JOIN CUSTOMER\_DIM } D \text{ ON } F.\text{Cust\_SK} = D.\text{Cust\_SK}
$$

$$
\text{WHERE } F.\text{Txn\_Date} = 2024\text{-}09\text{-}15
$$

Since $2024\text{-}09\text{-}15$ lies in the range $[2024\text{-}02\text{-}10, 2025\text{-}01\text{-}24]$, the join resolves to $Cust\_SK = 502$. The query correctly returns **Region = West** and **Credit_Limit = 7,50,000** — the values valid on that day.

---

#### 📗 Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**Q.B)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

A retail e-commerce company observes that the **demographic profile** of its customers (Age Band, Income Bracket, City Tier) changes **every 1–2 months**, while the core customer attributes (Name, Email, Phone) change **rarely**.

**(a)** Identify the SCD strategy that best handles this scenario. Compare it with SCD Type 2 and justify your selection in **7 marks**.

**(b)** Draw the resulting schema architecture (main dimension + mini-dimension + fact table) and explain how a marketing analyst would query "customers who were in the Urban High-Income bracket in 2024 but are now in the Suburban Mid-Income bracket in 2025." **7 marks**.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SCD Strategy Identification (7 marks):**

The appropriate strategy is **SCD Type 4 — Mini-Dimension Approach**.

* **Comparison with SCD Type 2:** In Type 2, a customer moving between demographic brackets every 1–2 months would create 6+ rows per year. Over 5 years, the dimension table would balloon, and joins on the fact table would become slow and unwieldy.
* **Why Type 4 is superior here:**
  * The volatile attributes (Age Band, Income Bracket, City Tier) form a *cluster* of related, frequently changing values — the textbook trigger for Type 4.
  * The Mini-Dimension is small (only the volatile attributes) and grows only when a *new combination* appears, not on every individual change.
  * The main dimension stays lean, and fact table joins remain efficient.

**(b) Schema Architecture and Query Logic (7 marks):**

**Main Dimension `CUSTOMER_MAIN_DIM`:**

| Cust_SK | Cust_NK | Name | Email | Phone | Current_Profile_Key |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1001 | CUST_501 | Rahul | rahul@mail | 9999... | 5 |
| 1002 | CUST_502 | Anjali | anjali@mail | 8888... | 7 |

**Mini-Dimension `CUSTOMER_PROFILE_MINI_DIM`:**

| Profile_Key | Age_Band | Income_Bracket | City_Tier | D_eff | D_exp |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 25-35 | High | Urban | 2023-01-01 | 2024-12-31 |
| 7 | 36-50 | Mid | Suburban | 2024-06-01 | NULL |

**Fact Table `FACT_SALES`:**

| Txn_ID | Cust_SK | Profile_Key | Date_Key | Amount |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TX01 | 1001 | 5 | 20240615 | 5000 |
| TX02 | 1001 | 7 | 20250310 | 7500 |

**Analytical Query — Cohort Comparison:**

$$
\text{SELECT } CM.\text{Name}, P1.\text{Income\_Bracket} \text{ AS Income\_2024}, P2.\text{Income\_Bracket} \text{ AS Income\_2025}
$$

$$
\text{FROM CUSTOMER\_MAIN\_DIM } CM
$$

$$
\text{JOIN CUSTOMER\_PROFILE\_MINI\_DIM } P1 \text{ ON } CM.\text{Cust\_SK} = \text{2024 Fact FK Profile}
$$

$$
\text{JOIN CUSTOMER\_PROFILE\_MINI\_DIM } P2 \text{ ON } CM.\text{Cust\_SK} = \text{2025 Fact FK Profile}
$$

$$
\text{WHERE } P1.\text{Income\_Bracket} = \text{High} \text{ AND } P2.\text{Income\_Bracket} = \text{Mid}
$$

The marketing analyst joins the mini-dimension *twice* — once using the 2024 fact rows and once using the 2025 fact rows — and filters on the respective `Income_Bracket` columns to identify the down-migrating cohort.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Marks Are Lost:**
> * **Pitfall 1 (2 marks lost):** Writing the Fact Table foreign key as the *Natural Key* instead of the *Surrogate Key*. This breaks SCD Type 2 entirely.
> * **Pitfall 2 (2 marks lost):** Forgetting to update the `D_exp` and `F_cur` of the *previous* row when inserting a new Type 2 row. The previous row would incorrectly remain "current."
> * **Pitfall 3 (3 marks lost):** Confusing SCD Type 3 with Type 2. Type 3 *alters* the table schema; Type 2 *inserts* a row. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> * **Pitfall 4 (2 marks lost):** Recommending SCD Type 4 without identifying a *cluster* of rapidly changing attributes. Type 4 is not a general-purpose upgrade of Type 2.
> * **Pitfall 5 (1 mark lost):** Failing to mention the **Surrogate Key** rationale in any Type 2 / Type 6 answer. Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for this justification.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for SCD — KTU 2024 Scheme:**

* 🔑 **SCD = Slowly Changing Dimension** — a dimension whose attribute values change gradually and unpredictably, requiring deliberate history-management.
* 🔑 **Type 0:** No change. Original value preserved. Use for *immutable* attributes (Date of Birth, Country of Birth).
* 🔑 **Type 1:** Overwrite. Use for *corrections* (typos, name spellings). History is *destroyed*.
* 🔑 **Type 2:** Insert new row. **Most-tested in KTU.** Use `D_eff`, `D_exp`, `F_cur`, and a **Surrogate Key** (`SK`).
* 🔑 **Type 3:** Add new column. Tracks only the *last* prior value. Not scalable beyond one prior version.
* 🔑 **Type 4:** Mini-Dimension. Use when a *cluster* of attributes changes rapidly. Fact Table gains a *second* foreign key.
* 🔑 **Type 5:** Type 4 + a Type 1 "current profile" column in the main dimension. Best of both worlds for "current state" reporting.
* 🔑 **Type 6:** Type 2 + Type 3 hybrid. Adds `Current_Value` and `Original_Value` columns that are *stamped* across all versioned rows. Enables *both* date-aware and current-value queries.
* 🔑 **Surrogate Keys are mandatory** in Type 2/6 to allow one Natural Key to map to many historical version rows.
* 🔑 **Fact Tables store Surrogate Keys**, never Natural Keys, in SCD Type 2/6 designs.
* 🔑 **Effective Date** $D_{eff}$ = date the new version became valid; **Expiry Date** $D_{exp}$ = $D_{eff}^{new} - 1$ for the outgoing row.
* 🔑 **Current Flag** $F_{cur}$ = 1 for the latest version, 0 for all historical versions.
* 🔑 **Point-in-time queries** use the transaction date to select the correct SCD Type 2 version via the Surrogate Key.
* 🔑 **Kimball's rule of thumb:** When in doubt, default to **SCD Type 2** — it is the most flexible and most widely accepted in industry.
* 🔑 **Storage vs. Accuracy trade-off:** Type 1 is cheapest, Type 2/6 are most accurate, Type 4 balances both.
* 🔑 **Type 6 is the exam-favorite "trick"** — examiners love asking students to design a hybrid schema that supports *both* historical and current reporting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advances in Dimensional Modeling: Aggregates & Roll-Up, Time Dimension, Junk Dimensions, Degenerate Dimensions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT412 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advances in Dimensional Modeling: Aggregates & Roll-Up, Time Dimension, Junk Dimensions, Degenerate Dimensions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Advanced Dimensional Modeling"?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Dimensional Modeling** is a logical design technique that structures data into **Fact** tables (measurable business events) and **Dimension** tables (descriptive context). The *advances* in this discipline, pioneered by **Ralph Kimball**, are the practical extensions that solve real-world complexity beyond the basic star schema.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Advanced Dimensional Modeling* refers to the set of design patterns—**Aggregates, Time Dimensions, Junk Dimensions, and Degenerate Dimensions**—that optimize query performance, manage slowly changing context, and eliminate sparse, low-cardinality flags from a star schema. These techniques transform a textbook star schema into a production-grade, query-optimized **Data Warehouse (DW)**.

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Advanced Dimensional Modeling

### 🔹 Pillar 1: Aggregates & Roll-Up
An **Aggregate** is a pre-computed summary table that stores data rolled up to a higher level of granularity than the base fact table.

### 🔹 Pillar 2: Time Dimension
A **Time Dimension** (or Date Dimension) is a dedicated, robust dimension table that supports multiple hierarchies (Day → Month → Quarter → Year) and temporal intelligence (fiscal calendars, holidays, weekdays).

### 🔹 Pillar 3: Junk Dimensions
A **Junk Dimension** is a consolidated dimension table that groups together low-cardinality flags and indicators (e.g., `IsHoliday`, `PaymentMode`, `OrderStatus`) that would otherwise create many sparse foreign-key columns in the fact table.

### 🔹 Pillar 4: Degenerate Dimensions
A **Degenerate Dimension** is a dimension key (like an Invoice Number or Order ID) that exists in the fact table without a corresponding dimension table. It serves as a unique transaction identifier for drill-through operations.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Grocery Store Receipt

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Supermart Receipt System"**
>
> Imagine you are the manager of a massive supermarket chain. Every day, millions of items are scanned. Your "Dimensional Model" works as follows:
>
> - 🧾 **Fact Table** = The line items on every receipt (Product, Price, Quantity, Date).
> - 🏬 **Dimension Tables** = Product info, Store info, Cashier info, Customer info.
> - ⏫ **Aggregate / Roll-Up** = You don't read every receipt; you ask the computer: "What were *total sales per store per month*?" The answer comes from a pre-calculated roll-up, like a monthly summary board on the wall.
> - 📅 **Time Dimension** = A calendar hanging on the wall with columns for Day, Week, Month, Quarter, Year, Fiscal Period, and "IsHoliday" flags.
> - 🗑️ **Junk Dimension** = Instead of having 10 separate checkbox columns on the receipt (`PaidByCard`, `IsDiscounted`, `IsGift`, `IsReturned`...), all these little flags are neatly stuffed into **one** small lookup table called "Receipt_Attributes".
> - 🔢 **Degenerate Dimension** = The **Receipt Number** itself. It sits on the fact table but has no separate "Receipt" table. It is its own identifier—the "ghost dimension."

![Analogy Banner](https://dummyimage.com/1000x60/0a3d62/ffffff.png&text=Advanced+Dimensional+Modeling+%E2%9D%96+The+Supermart+Analogy)

---

## 1.4 Why KTU Emphasizes These Four Patterns

| Pattern | Core Problem Solved | KTU 2024 Module 2 Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **Aggregates** | Slow queries on billion-row fact tables | **High** (frequent 14-mark questions) |
| **Time Dimension** | Time-based reporting and hierarchies | **High** |
| **Junk Dimensions** | Sparse, low-cardinality flags | **Medium** |
| **Degenerate Dimensions** | Transactional traceability | **Medium** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** These four patterns appear in nearly every KTU Data Warehousing university exam. Master their **schemas** (the diagrams) and their **justification** (the "why"), and you will secure full marks in the dimensional modeling section.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Aggregates & Roll-Up: The Theory of Pre-Computation

### 2.1.1 What is an Aggregate?

An **aggregate fact table** is a summary table where one or more dimensions of the base fact table have been "rolled up" to a higher level, and one or more measures have been aggregated using a **summary function** (typically `SUM`, `COUNT`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Roll-Up Definition:**
> A *Roll-Up* is the OLAP operation (and corresponding design pattern) of climbing up a dimensional hierarchy and applying an aggregation function. For example: *Daily Sales → Monthly Sales → Quarterly Sales → Yearly Sales.*

### 2.1.2 The Hierarchy of Granularity

Consider a retail sales fact table. The grain (atomic level) might be:

**One row per product per customer per store per day.**

A roll-up hierarchy looks like this:

```
Atomic Grain (Day × Store × Product)
        ↓  ROLL-UP (apply SUM)
Month × Store × Product
        ↓  ROLL-UP
Quarter × Region × Product Category
        ↓  ROLL-UP
Year × Country × Department
```

### 2.1.3 Why Use Aggregates? The Performance Math

Aggregates trade **storage space** for **query speed**. The query time savings can be estimated using the **Cardinality Reduction Ratio (CRR)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** A well-designed aggregate can reduce query time by **10× to 1000×** for the corresponding roll-up query, because the database must scan far fewer rows.

### 2.1.4 Drawbacks of Aggregates

- **Storage cost**: Each aggregate duplicates summary data.
- **ETL complexity**: The ETL pipeline must load and maintain all aggregates.
- **Constraint**: A query *can only* use an aggregate if the query's `GROUP BY` columns are a **superset** of the aggregate's grouping columns.

---

## 2.2 Time Dimension: The Most Important Dimension

### 2.2.1 Why a Dedicated Time Dimension?

Time is unique in dimensional modeling: it is the **only dimension guaranteed to be present in every fact table**. Rather than storing dates as a single `date` column, Kimball recommends a full **Time Dimension Table** (`dim_date`) with rich attributes.

### 2.2.2 Essential Attributes of a Time Dimension

A robust time dimension includes:

- **Date Key** (integer surrogate, e.g., `20240115`)
- **Full Date** (`2024-01-15`)
- **Day of Week**, **Day Name**, **Day Number in Month**, **Day Number in Year**
- **Week Number**, **Week End Date**
- **Month Name**, **Month Number**, **Month-Year Key**
- **Quarter**, **Quarter Name**
- **Year**, **Fiscal Year**, **Fiscal Quarter**, **Fiscal Period**
- **IsWeekend**, **IsHoliday**, **IsLastDayOfMonth**
- **Season**, **Academic Term**
- **Event Flags** (e.g., `IsChristmasWeek`, `IsDiwaliWeek`)

### 2.2.3 Multiple Hierarchies

The Time Dimension supports **multiple parallel hierarchies**:

1. **Gregorian Calendar** (Day → Month → Year)
2. **Fiscal Calendar** (Day → Fiscal Period → Fiscal Year)
3. **Reporting Calendar** (Day → 4-4-5 Retail Week → Quarter)
4. **Academic Calendar** (Day → Term → Academic Year)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Why not just store `DATE` as a column in the fact table?"* — Answer: Because the fact table would have to compute all the time intelligence attributes (week, month, quarter, holiday flags) at query time, which is slow and inflexible. A dedicated Time Dimension **pre-computes** these attributes and supports **role-playing** (one date dimension serving Order Date, Ship Date, Due Date simultaneously via views/aliases).

---

## 2.3 Junk Dimensions: Consolidating the "Random Flags"

### 2.3.1 The Problem

Consider an **Order Fact Table**. It has many low-cardinality flags:

- `IsExpress` (Y/N)
- `PaymentMode` (Cash/Card/UPI/Cheque) — only 4 values
- `OrderStatus` (New/Shipped/Delivered/Cancelled) — only 4 values
- `IsGift` (Y/N)
- `IsInternational` (Y/N)

If each of these were a separate foreign key in the fact table, the fact table would become bloated with 5 extra columns, and the dimension tables would be tiny (only 2–4 rows each).

### 2.3.2 The Solution: Junk Dimension

**Combine all these low-cardinality flags into ONE dimension table.** Each unique combination of the flags becomes one row, assigned a junk dimension key.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Junk Dimension Rule:** A junk dimension is created when you have a group of **unrelated**, **low-cardinality** flags and indicators that do not naturally belong to a core dimension (Customer, Product, etc.).

### 2.3.3 Example Junk Dimension

| JunkKey | IsExpress | PaymentMode | OrderStatus | IsGift | IsInternational |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Y | Card | Shipped | N | N |
| 2 | N | UPI | New | Y | N |
| 3 | Y | Cash | Cancelled | N | N |
| 4 | N | Cheque | Delivered | N | Y |
| 5 | Y | UPI | Delivered | Y | N |

The fact table simply holds a `JunkKey` foreign key (e.g., `1`).

---

## 2.4 Degenerate Dimensions: The Dimension That Lives in the Fact

### 2.4.1 Definition

A **Degenerate Dimension (DD)** is a dimension attribute (typically a transaction identifier) that is stored in the fact table **without being joined to a separate dimension table**.

### 2.4.2 Classic Examples

- **Invoice Number** (e.g., `INV-2024-00045`)
- **Order Number** (e.g., `ORD-998877`)
- **Ticket Number** (e.g., `TKT-12345`)
- **Transaction ID**
- **Bill of Lading Number**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "degenerate"?** Because the dimension key has "degenerated" out of the star schema—it is no longer a separate table but exists as a column *inside* the fact table itself.

### 2.4.3 When to Use a Degenerate Dimension

Use a DD when:
1. The attribute is a **transaction/document identifier**.
2. There is **no additional descriptive context** beyond the ID (no separate attributes to maintain).
3. The ID is needed for **drill-through** to the original operational system or to the line-item detail.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all **mathematically verifiable** formulas and metrics relevant to advanced dimensional modeling. Memorize this for Part A and Part B answers.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cardinality Reduction Ratio | $\text{CRR} = \dfrac{\text{Rows in Base Fact}}{\text{Rows in Aggregate}}$ | Measures how much an aggregate shrinks the fact table. |
| 2 | Query Speedup (Estimated) | $\text{Speedup} \approx \text{CRR} \times \text{Index Efficiency Factor}$ | Approximate query time reduction when using an aggregate. |
| 3 | Storage Overhead | $\text{Storage}_\text{agg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \text{Rows}_i \times \text{RowSize}_i \right)$ | Total disk space required for all $n$ aggregate tables. |
| 4 | Roll-Up Operation | $\text{Agg}_{L+1} = \text{SUM}(\text{Agg}_{L})$ | To roll up from level $L$ to level $L+1$, sum the lower-level measure. |
| 5 | Aggregation Function Set | $F = \{\text{SUM}, \text{COUNT}, \text{MIN}, \text{MAX}, \text{AVG}\}$ | Standard set of summary functions allowed in roll-ups. |
| 6 | Additive Measure | $\text{SUM}(\text{SUM}_{L}) = \text{SUM}_{L+1}$ | A measure is additive if it can be summed across all dimensions. |
| 7 | Semi-Additive Measure | Sum across some dimensions, not others (e.g., account balances). | Cannot be summed across time; use `MIN`/`MAX`/`AVG` instead. |
| 8 | Non-Additive Measure | $\text{AVG}(\text{AVG}_L) \neq \text{AVG}_{L+1}$ | Ratios and percentages must be recomputed, not averaged. |
| 9 | Time Hierarchy Depth | $H_{\text{time}} = \{\text{Day}, \text{Week}, \text{Month}, \text{Quarter}, \text{Year}, \text{Decade}\}$ | Standard Gregorian hierarchy depth. |
| 10 | Junk Dimension Cardinality | $\vert D_{\text{junk}} \vert \leq \prod_{i=1}^{k} \vert c_i \vert$ | Upper bound = product of cardinalities of combined flags. |
| 11 | Days in Time Dimension | $\text{Rows}_{\text{time}} = 365.25 \times \text{Years} + \text{LeapAdjustments}$ | Typically 20–30 years of history = ~7,300–11,000 rows. |
| 12 | Grain of Fact Table | $\text{Grain} = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \text{Card}(D_i)$ | Combined cardinality of all dimension foreign keys. |
| 13 | Role-Playing Dimensions | $R_{\text{time}} = \{ \text{OrderDate}, \text{ShipDate}, \text{DueDate} \}$ | One physical time dimension, multiple foreign keys in fact. |
| 14 | Star Schema Tables Count | $T = 1 + n$ | One central fact + $n$ dimension tables. |
| 15 | Snowflake Normalization Cost | $\text{Joins}_{\text{snowflake}} = n$ | Snowflake requires $n$ joins; star requires $1$ join. |

> **Real-World Utility:** In real **E-Commerce, Banking, and Telecom** data warehouses, these formulas govern the trade-off between **query latency** (SLAs) and **storage costs** (Cloud billing on AWS Redshift, Snowflake, BigQuery). A well-tuned aggregate strategy can save **millions of dollars** in cloud compute costs annually.

---

## 2.6 Comparative Summary of the Four Advanced Patterns

| Pattern | Cardinality | Joins to Fact | Main Use Case | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Aggregate** | Lower than base | Same keys (subset) | Speed up summary queries | `Sales_Monthly_By_Store` |
| **Time Dimension** | ~10,000 rows | 1 (often role-played) | Time-based hierarchies & flags | `dim_date` with fiscal/gregorian |
| **Junk Dimension** | Usually < 1,000 | 1 | Combine low-cardinality flags | `dim_order_attributes` |
| **Degenerate Dimension** | Equal to fact row count | 0 (lives in fact) | Transaction ID, drill-through | `InvoiceNumber` column |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, step-by-step derivations** and worked examples that KTU examiners expect. Every algebraic transition is shown in full, using properly aligned LaTeX blocks.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> Use this decision matrix in your 14-mark answers to demonstrate **Apply-level (Bloom Level III)** thinking—the hallmark of a KTU 2024 high-scoring answer.

| Scenario | Symptom | Correct Pattern | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Queries on a 5-billion-row fact are too slow | High latency on `GROUP BY Year, Region` | **Aggregate** | Pre-compute the summary. |
| Need to report by `IsWeekend`, `IsHoliday` | Many boolean columns in fact | **Time Dimension** | Embed these in `dim_date`. |
| Fact has 6 small text/flag columns | Fact table is "wide" with sparse data | **Junk Dimension** | Consolidate flags into one dim. |
| Need to print an invoice number on a report | No other invoice attributes | **Degenerate Dimension** | Store in fact table. |
| `OrderDate` and `ShipDate` both needed | Two date columns in fact | **Role-Playing Time Dim** | Use one time dim twice via views. |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Roll-Up Computation

### Problem
A retail fact table `FACT_SALES` contains daily sales. The measure is `TotalSales` (Additive).

**Base Fact Table** (5 sample rows):

| DateKey | StoreKey | ProductKey | TotalSales |
|---|---|---|---|
| 20240115 | 1 | 100 | ₹500 |
| 20240115 | 1 | 101 | ₹300 |
| 20240116 | 1 | 100 | ₹700 |
| 20240116 | 2 | 100 | ₹400 |
| 20240116 | 2 | 101 | ₹600 |

### Step 1: Identify the Hierarchies
- **Date** hierarchy: Day → Month → Quarter → Year
- **Store** hierarchy: Store → City → State → Country
- **Product** hierarchy: SKU → Sub-Category → Category → Department

### Step 2: Define the Roll-Up Target
We want an aggregate: `AGG_SALES_MONTHLY_BY_CITY` with columns `MonthKey`, `CityKey`, `ProductCategoryKey`, and `TotalSales`.

### Step 3: Apply the Roll-Up Formula

For an **additive measure**, the roll-up operation is:

$$
\text{TotalSales}_{L+1} = \sum_{i \in L} \text{TotalSales}_i
$$

where $L$ is the lower granularity level and $L+1$ is the higher level.

### Step 4: Derive the Aggregate Row Values

**Compute January 2024 total sales for City = 1, Category = Electronics** (where Store 1 and Store 2 are in City 1, and Products 100 and 101 are in Electronics):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TotalSales}_{\text{Jan, City1, Electronics}} &= \sum_{\text{all rows where DateKey is in Jan 2024, StoreKey in City 1, ProductKey in Electronics}} \text{TotalSales} \\
&= 500 + 300 + 700 + 400 + 600 \\
&= \text{Rs. } 2500
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Verify the Cardinality Reduction Ratio (CRR)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CRR} &= \frac{\text{Rows in Base Fact}}{\text{Rows in Aggregate}} \\
&= \frac{5 \text{ rows}}{1 \text{ row}} \\
&= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

In a real warehouse with **5 billion rows** rolled up to **5 million monthly-city-category rows**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CRR}_{\text{real}} &= \frac{5{,}000{,}000{,}000}{5{,}000{,}000} \\
&= 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

This means the aggregate query scans **1000× fewer rows**, leading to dramatic speedup.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Non-Additive Measure (Percentage) Roll-Up

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **classic KTU trap**. Many students incorrectly average the averages.

### Problem
A fact table has `ProfitMargin` (a percentage). The base rows are:

| Region | Quarter | ProfitMargin |
|---|---|---|
| North | Q1 | 12% |
| South | Q1 | 8% |
| North | Q2 | 15% |
| South | Q2 | 5% |

### Step 1: Recognize Non-Additivity
Percentages are **non-additive**. The correct roll-up is **NOT**:

$$
\dfrac{12\% + 8\%}{2} = 10\% \quad \text{(WRONG)}
$$

### Step 2: Use the Correct Formula

To roll up a ratio, you must roll up its **numerator and denominator separately**, then re-divide:

$$
\text{ProfitMargin}_{\text{roll-up}} = \frac{\sum \text{Profit}}{\sum \text{Revenue}} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 3: Apply

Assume (Profit, Revenue) tuples: (12k, 100k), (8k, 100k), (15k, 100k), (5k, 100k):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ProfitMargin}_{\text{Year}} &= \frac{12{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 5{,}000}{100{,}000 + 100{,}000 + 100{,}000 + 100{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{40{,}000}{400{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= 10\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The **correct** annual margin is **10%**, computed by re-aggregation, not by averaging.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Junk Dimension Construction

### Step 1: Identify Candidate Flags

In a `FACT_ORDERS` table:
- `IsExpress` (2 values: Y, N)
- `PaymentMode` (4 values: Cash, Card, UPI, Cheque)
- `OrderStatus` (4 values: New, Shipped, Delivered, Cancelled)
- `IsGift` (2 values: Y, N)
- `IsInternational` (2 values: Y, N)

### Step 2: Calculate Maximum Possible Combinations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert D_{\text{junk}} \vert_{\max} &= \prod_{i=1}^{k} \vert c_i \vert \\
&= 2 \times 4 \times 4 \times 2 \times 2 \\
&= 128 \text{ rows}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Build the Junk Dimension Table

Typically only **20–30 unique combinations** actually occur in production data (sparse). Create the dimension with only those present:

| JunkKey | IsExpress | PaymentMode | OrderStatus | IsGift | IsInternational |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Y | Card | Shipped | N | N |
| 2 | N | UPI | New | Y | N |
| 3 | Y | Cash | Delivered | N | N |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 28 | N | UPI | Cancelled | Y | Y |

### Step 4: Update the Fact Table

Replace 5 columns with **1** `JunkKey` foreign key:

| OrderID (DD) | DateKey | CustomerKey | ProductKey | JunkKey | OrderAmount |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ORD-001 | 20240115 | 501 | 100 | 1 | ₹2,500 |
| ORD-002 | 20240115 | 502 | 101 | 2 | ₹800 |

This **shrinks the fact table row width** and improves cache performance.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Proof: Why a Time Dimension Improves Performance

### Step 1: Without a Time Dimension
The fact table stores only `date_value` (e.g., `2024-01-15`). To compute "monthly sales", the database must call a date function on **every row**.

Number of rows scanned: $N_{\text{base}}$

### Step 2: With a Time Dimension
The fact table has a foreign key `DateKey` (e.g., `20240115`) referencing `dim_date`. The date dimension row contains `MonthKey` (e.g., `202401`).

The optimizer can:
1. **Pre-aggregate** using the integer `DateKey` (faster than parsing dates).
2. **Pre-filter** the date dimension to January (only 31 rows out of ~3,650).
3. **Use bitmap indexes** on `MonthKey`.

### Step 3: Quantitative Comparison

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rows scanned (without Time Dim)} &= N_{\text{base}} \\
\text{Rows scanned (with Time Dim + Month Index)} &= \frac{N_{\text{base}}}{12} + 31 \\
\text{Speedup Factor} &\approx \frac{N_{\text{base}}}{\frac{N_{\text{base}}}{12} + 31} \\
&\approx 12 \text{ (for monthly queries)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **quarterly** query:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speedup}_{\text{quarterly}} &\approx 4
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves the time dimension delivers **O($\frac{N}{H}$)** speedup where $H$ is hierarchy depth.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Schema Diagrams (Star vs. Snowflake with Advanced Dims)

The base star schema uses a **single denormalized dimension table** for each dimension. The snowflake schema **normalizes** dimensions into multiple related tables.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Note:** Always draw a **star schema** in your answers unless specifically asked for a snowflake. Star schemas are **denormalized** for query speed.

**Fact Table Grain Equation:**

$$
\text{Grain} = \text{Transaction event} \times \text{Date} \times \text{Customer} \times \text{Product}
$$

For a retail warehouse with daily granularity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rows}_{\text{fact, year}} &= \text{Transactions/day} \times \text{Stores} \times \text{SKUs} \times 365 \\
&= 100{,}000 \times 50 \times 1000 \times 365 \\
&= 1.825 \times 10^{12} \text{ rows/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

Aggregates reduce this by a factor of 100–1000 for executive dashboards.

---

## 3.7 Decision Algorithm: When to Use Each Pattern

```
Is the query slow on the base fact table?
    YES → Build an AGGREGATE (Roll-Up)
    NO  → Continue
    
Are there many low-cardinality flags/indicators in the fact?
    YES → Create a JUNK DIMENSION
    NO  → Continue
    
Is there a transactional/document identifier with no other attributes?
    YES → Make it a DEGENERATE DIMENSION
    NO  → Continue
    
Does the fact need time intelligence (month, quarter, fiscal, holidays)?
    YES → Create a TIME DIMENSION
    NO  → Use existing structure
```

This algorithm appears in 14-mark KTU questions as a "design scenario" where you must justify each design choice.
