## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define an ETL tool. List any four common ETL tools used in data warehousing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **ETL Tool** is a software application that automates the extraction of data from heterogeneous sources, transforms it according to business rules, and loads it into a target Data Warehouse, while managing metadata, scheduling, and error handling.

Four common ETL tools are:
1. **Informatica PowerCenter** (Informatica LLC)
2. **Talend Open Studio / Data Fabric** (Talend / Qlik)
3. **Microsoft SSIS** (SQL Server Integration Services)
4. **IBM DataStage** (optional, any one more like Pentaho, Oracle ODI)

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Definition = 1 mark, listing 4 tools = 2 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of a scheduler in ETL automation? Name two common scheduling triggers.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **scheduler** in ETL automation is responsible for triggering ETL jobs at the right time or event, managing dependencies between jobs, retrying failed jobs, and notifying stakeholders on completion or failure.

Two common scheduling triggers:
1. **Time-based trigger** (e.g., daily at 02:00 AM)
2. **Event-based trigger** (e.g., arrival of a new file in a watched directory / database change)

> [!NOTE]
> Mentioning a third trigger (dependency-based / manual) earns a bonus half-mark in some evaluations.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Compare **Informatica, Talend, and Microsoft SSIS** on the following parameters: (i) Vendor and License Model, (ii) Architecture and IDE, (iii) Code Generation, (iv) Repository, (v) Scheduler, (vi) Best Use Case. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **5-layer generic architecture of an ETL tool** with a neat diagram. Describe the role of the **Metadata Repository** in detail. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Answer for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks**

| Parameter | Informatica | Talend | SSIS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vendor & License** | Informatica LLC; Proprietary Commercial | Talend (Qlik); Open-Source + Commercial | Microsoft; Bundled with SQL Server |
| **Architecture & IDE** | Client-Server; PowerCenter Designer (proprietary GUI) | Eclipse-based Talend Studio | Component-based; Visual Studio (SSDT) |
| **Code Generation** | No code exposed; compiled binary | Generates Java/perl code | Generates XML packages and C# scripts |
| **Repository** | RDBMS-based (Oracle/SQL Server) | Git-friendly XML metadata | SSISDB catalog in SQL Server |
| **Scheduler** | Workflow Manager + pmcmd | Job Conductor (TAC) | SQL Server Agent |
| **Best Use Case** | Large enterprise DWs (Fortune 500) | Cloud + Big Data projects | Microsoft-only mid-market firms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split (a):** Table with 6 rows = 6 × 1 = 6 marks; Conclusion = 1 mark.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks**

The **5-layer generic architecture of an ETL tool** is as follows:

**Layer 1 — Client Designer IDE:** Provides a graphical interface for developers to design mappings, define sources/targets, and create transformations without writing low-level code.

**Layer 2 — Repository / Metadata Layer:** A centralized RDBMS that stores all metadata — source definitions, target schemas, transformation rules, mappings, sessions, and workflow schedules. It ensures version control, reuse, and consistency across the team.

**Layer 3 — Transformation Engine:** The runtime component that reads data, applies transformations (lookups, filters, joins, aggregations), and produces the output. It is the "muscle" of the ETL tool.

**Layer 4 — Scheduler / Workflow Manager:** Decides *when* jobs run, manages dependencies (Job A → Job B), and handles retries on failure.

**Layer 5 — Monitoring & Logging Console:** Captures row counts, error logs, performance metrics, and sends real-time alerts. (e.g., Informatica Workflow Monitor, SSIS Reports)

**Role of the Metadata Repository (detailed):**
- Acts as the **single source of truth** for all ETL definitions.
- Enables **impact analysis** — if a source column changes, the repository shows all dependent mappings.
- Supports **versioning** and **auditing** for compliance.
- Facilitates **collaboration** — multiple developers can work on the same project with conflict detection.
- Used by the scheduler and monitoring tools to fetch job definitions and parameters at runtime.

(Refer to Section 4.1 for the diagram.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split (b):** 5 layers × 1 mark = 5 marks; Metadata Repository role in detail = 2 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **scheduling strategies** used in ETL processes. Differentiate between **batch, micro-batch, and event-driven** scheduling with examples. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **end-to-end automation workflow of an ETL process** covering trigger detection, execution, error handling, and notification. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Answer for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks**

ETL scheduling strategies determine *when* and *how* ETL jobs are triggered. The three main paradigms are:

**1. Batch Window Scheduling (Traditional DW):**
ETL jobs run in a fixed time window, usually during off-peak hours (e.g., 02:00 – 06:00 AM). Suitable for daily/weekly reporting DWs.
- *Example:* Nightly sales aggregation from 50 branches into a star-schema DW.
- *Formula:* Window ≥ Σ Job Durations + 3σ buffer.

**2. Micro-Batch / Near-Real-Time Scheduling:**
Jobs run at short intervals (every 5–15 minutes) using Change Data Capture (CDC) to process only newly inserted/updated rows.
- *Example:* E-commerce order pipeline refreshing dashboards every 10 minutes.
- *Tools:* Informatica CDC, Talend CDC, SQL Server Change Tracking.

**3. Event-Driven / Streaming Scheduling:**
ETL is triggered by each new data event (e.g., Kafka message, IoT sensor reading) processed in real time.
- *Example:* Fraud detection system flagging transactions within 500 ms.
- *Tools:* Spark Streaming, Kafka Streams, Talend + Kafka.

**Comparative Summary:**

| Strategy | Latency | Volume | Complexity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Batch | Hours | High | Low | Nightly DW |
| Micro-Batch | Minutes | Medium | Medium | Operational BI |
| Event-Driven | Seconds | Variable | High | Real-time analytics |

---

#### **Model Answer for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks**

The **end-to-end automation workflow** of an ETL process consists of 7 stages:

**Stage 1 — Trigger Detection:**
The scheduler (e.g., SQL Server Agent, Informatica Scheduler) detects the trigger — time-based, event-based, or dependency-based — and prepares the next job in the queue.

**Stage 2 — Pre-Validation:**
Before execution, the system checks source availability, network connectivity, disk space on the target, and credential validity. Failures here skip the job and trigger an alert.

**Stage 3 — Job Initiation:**
The ETL engine reads configuration parameters from the metadata repository, opens connections to source and target systems, and starts the data extraction.

**Stage 4 — Execution Monitoring:**
During execution, the engine logs row counts, processing time per transformation, and any warnings. Some tools (e.g., Informatica, SSIS) support checkpointing — if the job fails midway, it can resume from the last checkpoint.

**Stage 5 — Error Handling:**
On failure, the system executes the *OnFailure* event handler. Typical actions include:
- Up to 3 retry attempts with exponential back-off.
- Sending an email/SMS alert to the on-call engineer.
- Writing the failed batch to a "dead-letter" directory for later inspection.

**Stage 6 — Post-Validation:**
After load completion, the engine reconciles source vs target row counts, runs data quality checks (nulls, duplicates, referential integrity), and generates a summary report.

**Stage 7 — Notification & Archival:**
The scheduler emails a success/failure report to stakeholders, archives the logs to long-term storage, and updates the DW audit tables.

(Refer to Section 4.3 for the visual workflow.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split (b):** 7 stages × 1 mark = 7 marks. Diagrams are optional but earn bonus half-mark.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and lose marks):**
> 1. **Confusing ETL with ELT** — In modern cloud DWs (Snowflake, BigQuery), data is loaded first and transformed later. Be clear about the context.
> 2. **Listing tools without comparing** — A comparison question demands a *table* with *parameters*. Just listing names = 0 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting metadata repository** — Almost every ETL tool answer must mention the metadata repository; omitting it costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing "Scheduler" with "Workflow Manager"** — Workflow Manager *defines* the schedule; Scheduler *executes* it.
> 5. **Not mentioning error handling** — In automation answers, retry logic and alert escalation are mandatory for full marks.
> 6. **Writing Informatica-specific code** — KTU expects *architecture* and *concepts*, not vendor-specific syntax. Avoid writing repository SQL or PowerCenter commands.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ✅ **ETL Tool =** GUI + Metadata Repo + Transformation Engine + Scheduler + Monitor.
- ✅ **Informatica** = Enterprise-grade, proprietary, expensive, PowerCenter.
- ✅ **Talend** = Open-source, Eclipse-based, generates Java, cloud-friendly.
- ✅ **SSIS** = Microsoft, Visual Studio IDE, SQL Server Agent scheduler, SQL Server bundled.
- ✅ **5-Layer Architecture =** Designer → Repository → Engine → Scheduler → Monitor.
- ✅ **Metadata Repository** stores *all* metadata: source/target defs, mappings, sessions, schedules.
- ✅ **Scheduling Triggers =** Time / Event / Dependency / Manual.
- ✅ **Automation Stages (7) =** Trigger → Pre-Validate → Execute → Monitor → Handle Errors → Post-Validate → Notify.
- ✅ **Scheduling Strategies =** Batch / Micro-Batch / Event-Driven.
- ✅ **3-Sigma Buffer Formula:** $T_{worst} = T_{mean} + 3\sigma_{total}$.
- ✅ **Parallelization** of independent extract jobs reduces total window time.
- ✅ **CDC (Change Data Capture)** converts full loads to incremental, saving ~70% time.
- ✅ **Error Handling** = Retry logic + Alerts + Dead-letter queue + Checkpoint restart.
- ✅ **SSIS Hierarchy:** Package → Control Flow → Data Flow Task → Components.
- ✅ **Precedence Constraints in SSIS =** Success, Failure, Completion (green, red, blue arrows).
- ✅ **For comparison questions:** Use a *parameter-wise table* with *at least 5–6 parameters*.
- ✅ **Mention "metadata" and "automation"** at least once in every ETL answer.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — ETL Tools and Platforms**

topics.title: Data Quality and Data Governance in ETL: Handling Incomplete and Inconsistent Data, Ensuring Data Accuracy, Consistency, and Integrity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT412 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Quality and Data Governance in ETL: Handling Incomplete and Inconsistent Data, Ensuring Data Accuracy, Consistency, and Integrity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Data Quality in ETL?

In the context of **Extract, Transform, Load (ETL)** processes, **Data Quality** refers to the measure of fitness of data to serve its intended purpose in business operations, analytics, and decision-making. It is the discipline of ensuring that data entering the data warehouse is **accurate, complete, consistent, timely, unique, and valid**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Data Quality is the cumulative set of techniques, policies, procedures, and metrics used during the ETL process to measure, monitor, cleanse, and maintain data assets so that they conform to the predefined business rules and integrity constraints of the target data warehouse.*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Reception Desk"

Imagine a large hospital reception desk. Every day, thousands of patient records arrive via phone calls, online forms, walk-ins, and referrals. The reception team is the **ETL layer** of the hospital.

- **Incomplete data** → A patient gives a name but forgets their date of birth. If the doctor prescribes medication based on this record, it could be dangerous.
- **Inconsistent data** → The same patient is listed as "Rahul K." in the morning register and "Rahul Kumar" in the evening register — the system thinks they are two different people.
- **Inaccurate data** → A patient's blood group is wrongly typed as "O+" when it is actually "B−", leading to a transfusion error.
- **Lack of governance** → Every receptionist uses a different format for addresses, phone numbers, and IDs. There is no master rulebook.

**Data Quality in ETL is exactly this**: ensuring that before any record is "admitted" into the warehouse, it is clean, complete, standardized, and trustworthy.

## 1.2 What is Data Governance?

**Data Governance** is the overarching framework of **policies, roles, standards, processes, and technologies** that an organization uses to manage, control, and protect its data assets across the entire lifecycle (from source systems to the warehouse, to the consumers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:**
> - **Data Quality** = the *WHAT* (the condition of the data itself).
> - **Data Governance** = the *WHO* and *HOW* (the people, rules, accountability, and stewardship that ensure quality is maintained over time).

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Trustworthy Data in ETL

| Pillar | One-Line Meaning | ETL Stage Where it Matters Most |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | Data correctly represents the real-world entity it describes | Extraction & Transformation |
| **Consistency** | The same data element has the same value across all systems | Transformation |
| **Integrity** | Relationships between data entities remain valid (FK, PK, domain rules) | Loading |

## 1.4 Standard Data Quality Metrics (Industry Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **six canonical dimensions** of data quality as recognized by DAMA (Data Management Association) and adopted by KTU 2024 syllabus:

- **Completeness** — % of populated fields vs. expected fields.
- **Accuracy** — % of values that correctly match the real-world source of truth.
- **Consistency** — % of values that agree across different source systems.
- **Timeliness** — Degree to which data is up-to-date for the task at hand.
- **Uniqueness** — Degree to which an entity appears only once (no duplicates).
- **Validity** — Degree to which data conforms to defined formats, types, and domain rules.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 Why Data Quality Problems Arise in ETL

Data flowing into a warehouse comes from **heterogeneous sources**: legacy databases, CRMs, ERPs, flat files, APIs, IoT streams, web logs, and third-party feeds. Each source has its own:

- Schema definition
- Data entry conventions
- Encoding standards
- Update frequency
- Quality controls (or lack thereof)

When these streams are merged in the **Staging Area** of the ETL pipeline, conflicts and errors are inevitable.

### 2.1.1 Common Categories of Data Quality Issues

1. **Incomplete Data (Missing Values)**
   - Fields left blank (`NULL`, empty strings, zeros used as placeholders).
   - *Example:* Customer's email is `NULL` in 12% of records.

2. **Inconsistent Data**
   - Same entity represented differently across sources.
   - *Example:* "Kerala", "KERALA", "KL", "Kerla" all refer to the same state.

3. **Inaccurate Data**
   - Values that do not reflect reality.
   - *Example:* Date of birth recorded as `1900-01-01` (a default placeholder).

4. **Duplicate Data**
   - Multiple records for the same real-world entity.
   - *Example:* "John Smith" and "Jon Smith" with the same phone number.

5. **Invalid Data**
   - Violates business rules or domain constraints.
   - *Example:* Age = `250`, Order Date in the future, Negative salary.

6. **Stale / Outdated Data**
   - Data that was correct at one point but no longer reflects reality.

## 2.2 Techniques to Handle Incomplete Data in ETL

| Technique | How it Works | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Ignore the Tuple** | Discard rows with missing values | When missing value is in the target/class attribute and dataset is large |
| **Manual Imputation** | Domain expert fills the missing value | Small datasets, high-stakes records (medical, financial) |
| **Constant / Default Fill** | Replace NULL with a default value (e.g., `"Unknown"`, `0`, `-1`) | Categorical fields with no predictive weight |
| **Mean / Median / Mode Imputation** | Replace with central tendency statistic | Numerical fields with low missing % |
| **Forward / Backward Fill** | Use previous or next valid value | Time-series data (stock prices, sensor readings) |
| **Predictive Imputation** | Use ML model (regression, KNN) to predict missing value | When missingness pattern is non-random and high-value |
| **Source Re-query** | Go back to the source system and re-fetch | When the source system is the authoritative owner |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always state that the choice of imputation technique depends on (a) the **percentage of missingness**, (b) the **data type**, (c) the **missingness mechanism** (MCAR, MAR, MNAR), and (d) the **downstream business use case**.

## 2.3 Techniques to Handle Inconsistent Data

1. **Standardization** — Apply uniform formats (e.g., all dates in `YYYY-MM-DD`, all names in `Title Case`, all phone numbers in `+91-XXXXXXXXXX`).
2. **Lookup-Based Correction** — Replace abbreviations with full forms using reference/master tables (e.g., "KL" → "Kerala").
3. **Data Profiling** — Analyze distributions, patterns, and outliers to detect anomalies before transformation.
4. **Parsing & Tokenization** — Split complex fields into atomic components (e.g., "House No. 12B, MG Road, Kochi" → `HouseNo`, `Street`, `City`).
5. **Cross-Source Reconciliation** — When the same entity appears in multiple systems, define a **survivorship rule** (e.g., "Master System wins", "Most recent value wins", "Highest priority source wins").

## 2.4 Ensuring Data Accuracy, Consistency, and Integrity

### 2.4.1 Accuracy
- Use **validation rules** against trusted reference data.
- Implement **outlier detection** (Z-score, IQR method).
- Periodic **audit sampling** by data stewards.

### 2.4.2 Consistency
- Apply **master data management (MDM)** to enforce a single source of truth.
- Use **deterministic matching** keys (e.g., hashed national ID).
- Enforce **schema-on-read and schema-on-write** validations.

### 2.4.3 Integrity
- Enforce **Entity Integrity** (Primary Keys unique and not null).
- Enforce **Referential Integrity** (Foreign Keys valid).
- Enforce **Domain Integrity** (column values lie in allowed set).
- Enforce **User-Defined Integrity** (custom business rules).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Exam Cheat Table)

| Concept | Definition / Formula / Rule |
|---|---|
| **Completeness Ratio** | $C \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Number of Non-NULL Values}}{\text{Total Expected Values}} \times 100\%$ |
| **Accuracy Ratio** | $A \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Number of Correct Values}}{\text{Total Values Verified}} \times 100\%$ |
| **Consistency Ratio** | $CS \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Values Matching Across Sources}}{\text{Total Cross-Source Comparisons}} \times 100\%$ |
| **Uniqueness Ratio** | $U \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Distinct Entities}}{\text{Total Records}} \times 100\%$ |
| **Data Quality Score (Composite)** | $DQ_{score} \;=\; w_1 C + w_2 A + w_3 CS + w_4 U$ where $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| **Survivorship Rule** | Tie-breaker logic for conflicting multi-source values (newest, highest priority, master wins) |
| **MCAR** | Missing Completely At Random — no pattern |
| **MAR** | Missing At Random — pattern depends on observed data |
| **MNAR** | Missing Not At Random — missingness depends on the missing value itself |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking & Finance** — Customer KYC data must be 100% accurate and consistent across credit, debit, and loan systems to comply with RBI regulations.
- **Healthcare** — Patient records must be complete and accurate; mis-typed blood groups or drug allergies can be fatal.
- **Retail / E-commerce** — Product catalog data must be consistent across web, mobile, and warehouse to avoid wrong pricing or stock-outs.
- **Telecommunications** — CDR (Call Detail Record) integrity is essential for accurate billing.
- **IoT & Smart Systems** — Sensor data must be timely and consistent for real-time analytics and anomaly detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Quality Pipeline

## 3.1 The Data Quality Layer of an ETL Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step operational flow** of how data quality and governance are enforced during ETL. Every step is mandatory for KTU long-answer (14-mark) questions.

### Step 1 — Data Profiling (Pre-Transformation Audit)

Before any transformation begins, the data engineer performs **data profiling** on the staging area. This is the discovery phase.

**Activities performed:**
- Count total rows, distinct values, NULLs per column.
- Detect data types actually present vs. declared.
- Identify outliers (using min, max, mean, std deviation).
- Build histograms and frequency distributions.

**Output:** A **Data Quality Baseline Report** that becomes the benchmark against which improvement is measured.

### Step 2 — Apply Validation Rules (Schema & Domain Checks)

Each column is validated against **declarative rules**.

**Types of Rules:**
- **Type Rule:** `Order_Date` must be of type `DATE`.
- **Format Rule:** `Phone` must match regex `^[+]?[0-9]{10,15}$`.
- **Range Rule:** `Age` must lie in `[0, 120]`.
- **Mandatory Rule:** `Customer_ID` cannot be `NULL`.
- **Uniqueness Rule:** `Email` must be unique across the dataset.

### Step 3 — Cleansing (Handling Incomplete Data)

For each violation detected, apply the appropriate **cleansing strategy** chosen during profiling.

- *Missing email* → Impute with `"unknown@placeholder.com"` OR route to a **quarantine table** for manual review.
- *Missing age* → Compute from `Date_of_Birth` if available; else use median of similar demographic group.
- *Default placeholder values like `0`, `-1`, `9999`* → Replace with `NULL` first, then impute.

### Step 4 — Standardization (Handling Inconsistent Data)

- Apply a **canonical format** to every column.
- Use **lookup/master tables** for abbreviations (States, Countries, Currencies).
- Normalize text to a consistent case (UPPER, lower, Title).
- Convert all dates to a warehouse-standard format (e.g., `YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS`).

### Step 5 — De-duplication

- Apply **deterministic matching** on hashed business keys (e.g., PAN, SSN, Phone).
- Apply **fuzzy matching** (Levenshtein distance, Jaro-Winkler) for name/address fields.
- Apply the **survivorship rule** to merge duplicates into a single golden record.

### Step 6 — Referential & Business Rule Validation

- Verify that every Foreign Key in the fact table exists in the corresponding dimension.
- Verify business rules (e.g., `Order_Date ≤ Ship_Date ≤ Delivery_Date`).
- Verify slowly changing dimensions (SCD) logic where applicable.

### Step 7 — Data Quality Scoring & Audit Logging

For each batch, compute and store:

$$
DQ_{score} = w_1 \cdot C + w_2 \cdot A + w_3 \cdot CS + w_4 \cdot U
$$

where each component is computed as defined in the cheat table above.

**Log entries** must include: source system, load timestamp, row counts (read / accepted / rejected / quarantined), reason for rejection.

### Step 8 — Governance Hand-Off

- Tag each record with **lineage metadata** (source, transform applied, owner).
- Notify the **data steward** if any quality threshold is breached.
- Publish the **DQ dashboard** for stakeholder visibility.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Composite Data Quality Score)

> **Problem:** A data warehouse batch of **10,000** customer records was audited. Results:
> - Non-null values across mandatory fields: **9,200**
> - Values matching trusted reference: **9,500**
> - Values consistent across two source systems: **8,800**
> - Distinct customer entities found: **9,400** (after de-duplication)
>
> Weights assigned: $w_1 = 0.30$, $w_2 = 0.30$, $w_3 = 0.25$, $w_4 = 0.15$.
>
> Compute the **Composite Data Quality Score**.

### Solution (Exhaustive Steps):

**Step 1 — Compute Completeness Ratio ($C$):**

$$
C = \frac{9200}{10000} \times 100\% = 92.00\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Accuracy Ratio ($A$):**

$$
A = \frac{9500}{10000} \times 100\% = 95.00\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Consistency Ratio ($CS$):**

$$
CS = \frac{8800}{10000} \times 100\% = 88.00\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Uniqueness Ratio ($U$):**

$$
U = \frac{9400}{10000} \times 100\% = 94.00\%
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Composite Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DQ_{score} &= w_1 \cdot C + w_2 \cdot A + w_3 \cdot CS + w_4 \cdot U \\
&= (0.30 \times 92.00) + (0.30 \times 95.00) + (0.25 \times 88.00) + (0.15 \times 94.00) \\
&= 27.60 + 28.50 + 22.00 + 14.10 \\
&= 92.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

A score of **92.20%** indicates a healthy data warehouse load. Industry benchmarks typically require $> 95\%$ for mission-critical analytics and $> 90\%$ for operational reporting.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Cleansing Strategy?

| Data Type | Missing % < 5% | Missing % 5–20% | Missing % > 20% |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Numeric (Continuous)** | Mean / Median imputation | Median + flag | Drop column or predictive model |
| **Categorical** | Mode imputation | Mode + "Unknown" bucket | Drop or merge category |
| **Date / Time** | Forward fill | Interpolation | Re-query source |
| **Identifier (PK/FK)** | Reject record → quarantine | Reject record → manual fix | Halt load and escalate |
| **Free Text** | Ignore (no imputation) | NLP-based completion | Drop column |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Data Quality & Governance Pipeline in ETL

The diagram below shows the full lifecycle from heterogeneous sources through the ETL staging area into the governed warehouse, with quality checkpoints at every stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source 1 Legacy ERP] --> S[Staging Area]
    B[Source 2 CRM] --> S
    C[Source 3 Flat Files] --> S
    D[Source 4 IoT Streams] --> S
    E[Source 5 Third Party API] --> S

    S --> P[Step 1 Data Profiling]
    P --> V[Step 2 Validation Rules]
    V --> CL[Step 3 Cleansing Missing Incomplete]
    CL --> ST[Step 4 Standardization Inconsistent]
    ST --> DD[Step 5 De duplication Fuzzy Matching]
    DD --> RI[Step 6 Referential Integrity Check]
    RI --> QS[Step 7 Quality Scoring and Audit Log]
    QS --> G{Governance Gate}

    G -->|DQ Score Above Threshold| W[Data Warehouse Fact and Dimension Tables]
    G -->|Below Threshold| Q[Quarantine Table]
    G -->|Critical Violation| N[Alert Data Steward]

    W --> M[Master Data Management Hub]
    N --> DS[Data Steward Manual Review]
    Q --> DS
    DS --> S

    M --> BI[BI Dashboards and Analytics]
    W --> BI

    style S fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px
    style W fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px
    style DS fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Data Governance Framework (RACI-Style Responsibility Map)

This diagram illustrates the **people-and-process** side of governance that surrounds the technical ETL pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POLICY[Policy Layer]
        DG[Data Governance Council]
        POL[Data Policies and Standards]
    end

    subgraph PEOPLE[People Layer]
        CDO[Chief Data Officer]
        DS1[Data Stewards Domain Owners]
        DE[Data Engineers ETL Team]
        DA[Data Analysts Consumers]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[Process Layer]
        PROF[Data Profiling]
        CL2[Cleansing Workflow]
        AUD[Audit and Monitoring]
        RES[Issue Resolution SLA]
    end

    subgraph TECH[Technology Layer]
        META[Metadata Repository]
        MDM[Master Data Management]
        DQTOOL[Data Quality Tools]
        CAT[Data Catalog]
    end

    DG --> CDO
    POL --> CDO
    CDO --> DS1
    CDO --> DE
    CDO --> DA
    DS1 --> PROF
    DE --> CL2
    DA --> AUD
    AUD --> RES
    PROF --> DQTOOL
    CL2 --> DQTOOL
    AUD --> META
    RES --> MDM
    DQTOOL --> CAT

    style POLICY fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e
    style PEOPLE fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af
    style PROCESS fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
    style TECH fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#86198f
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Handling Inconsistent Records

This flowchart captures the **branching logic** an ETL job uses when it encounters conflicting values for the same entity across multiple sources.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Record Arrives in Staging] --> CK{Check for Duplicate Keys}
    CK -->|No Duplicate| OK[Load to Warehouse]
    CK -->|Duplicate Found| CMP{Compare Field Values}

    CMP -->|All Identical| OK
    CMP -->|Conflicting| SURV{Apply Survivorship Rule}

    SURV --> S1[Newest Value Wins]
    SURV --> S2[Master Source Wins]
    SURV --> S3[Highest Priority Source Wins]
    SURV --> S4[Most Frequent Value Wins]

    S1 --> GLD[Produce Single Golden Record]
    S2 --> GLD
    S3 --> GLD
    S4 --> GLD

    GLD --> LOG[Log Conflict and Resolution in Audit Table]
    LOG --> OK

    style OK fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style GLD fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412
    style LOG fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**Define Data Quality. List any FOUR dimensions of data quality relevant to ETL processes.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Data Quality is a measure of the fitness of data for its intended use in business operations and analytics. In ETL, it refers to the degree to which extracted and transformed data is accurate, complete, consistent, timely, unique, and valid for loading into the data warehouse.

**Four Dimensions (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Completeness** — All required data values are present.
2. **Accuracy** — Data correctly represents the real-world entity.
3. **Consistency** — Same data is represented uniformly across sources.
4. **Validity** — Data conforms to defined formats and domain rules.

*(Other acceptable dimensions: Timeliness, Uniqueness.)*

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**Differentiate between Data Quality and Data Governance.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Aspect | Data Quality | Data Governance |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | The *condition* of the data itself | The *management framework* around the data |
| **Scope** | Technical — measurements, cleansing, validation | Strategic — policies, roles, accountability |
| **Question Answered** | "Is the data good?" | "Who ensures the data is good and how?" |
| **Components** | Profiling, cleansing, scoring, monitoring | Policies, stewards, council, SLAs, MDM |
| **Owner** | Data Engineers / ETL team | Chief Data Officer / Governance Council |

**Conclusion:** Data Quality is the *outcome* and Data Governance is the *system* that sustains it. Both are interdependent — without governance, quality cannot be maintained at scale.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a) Explain the major causes of data quality issues during ETL. Discuss the techniques used to handle incomplete and inconsistent data with examples. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Explain the concept of Data Governance. Describe the key components of a Data Governance framework and how it ensures data accuracy, consistency, and integrity in a data warehouse. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Data quality issues arise because modern enterprises aggregate data from multiple heterogeneous sources, each with different schemas, formats, and business rules. The ETL layer is the convergence point where these conflicts surface.

**Major Causes of Data Quality Issues (3 Marks):**

1. **Heterogeneous Sources** — Different systems use different data types and formats (e.g., dates as `DD/MM/YYYY` vs `MM-DD-YY`).
2. **Manual Data Entry Errors** — Typos, misspellings, omissions in human-entered forms.
3. **Lack of Standardization** — No enterprise-wide data dictionary or master reference.
4. **System Integration Bugs** — Failed mappings, encoding mismatches during extraction.
5. **Evolving Business Rules** — Domain constraints change over time but are not propagated.
6. **Legacy Data** — Old systems contain placeholders like `9999`, `0000-00-00`, `unknown`.

**Techniques to Handle Incomplete Data (1.5 Marks):**

- **Ignore the tuple** — Drop rows with missing values.
- **Manual imputation** — Domain expert fills in.
- **Default / constant fill** — `NULL` → `"Unknown"`, `0`.
- **Statistical imputation** — Mean, median, mode.
- **Predictive imputation** — Regression, KNN-based filling.
- **Source re-query** — Re-fetch from origin system.

**Example:** A customer record with missing `Email` is imputed as `"unknown@placeholder.com"` and flagged for future manual verification.

**Techniques to Handle Inconsistent Data (1.5 Marks):**

- **Standardization** — Uniform formats across all values.
- **Lookup-based correction** — Master table maps `KL` → `Kerala`.
- **Parsing** — Split `"12B, MG Road, Kochi"` into atomic fields.
- **Survivorship rules** — When multiple sources disagree, pick one based on policy.

**Example:** `"Mr. Rahul Kumar"`, `"rahul k."`, `"RAHUL KUMAR"` are all standardized to `"Mr. Rahul Kumar"`.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Data Governance is the system of policies, roles, standards, and processes that an organization uses to manage and control its data assets across their lifecycle.

**Key Components of a Data Governance Framework (3 Marks):**

1. **Governance Council / Steering Committee** — Sets enterprise-wide policies.
2. **Chief Data Officer (CDO)** — Senior executive accountable for data strategy.
3. **Data Stewards** — Domain experts who ensure quality in their business area.
4. **Data Policies & Standards** — Written rules for naming, formats, retention, security.
5. **Master Data Management (MDM)** — Single source of truth for core entities.
6. **Metadata Repository & Data Catalog** — Documentation of what data exists, where, and who owns it.
7. **Data Quality Tools** — Automated profiling, cleansing, and monitoring.
8. **Issue Resolution SLAs** — Time-bound escalation paths for quality breaches.

**How Governance Ensures Accuracy, Consistency, Integrity (3 Marks):**

- **Accuracy** — Stewards audit samples regularly; reference data is maintained centrally.
- **Consistency** — MDM enforces one canonical form across all systems; survivorship rules apply during ETL merge.
- **Integrity** — Policies mandate PK/FK enforcement, domain validation, and audit logging; ETL jobs reject violating records to quarantine tables.

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):**
Without governance, ETL cleanses data once but it degrades again. Governance is what keeps it clean continuously.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) Describe the ETL pipeline components that handle data quality. Explain in detail the data quality scoring methodology with a suitable example. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Discuss the various imputation techniques used to handle missing values in ETL. How does the choice of technique depend on the missingness mechanism (MCAR, MAR, MNAR)? (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**ETL Pipeline Components for Data Quality (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Staging Area** — Raw landing zone where source data is captured unchanged. Profiling happens here.
2. **Data Profiler** — Component that analyzes distributions, NULL counts, type mismatches, outliers.
3. **Validation Engine** — Applies declarative rules (type, format, range, mandatory, uniqueness).
4. **Cleansing Engine** — Applies imputation, deduplication, standardization transformations.
5. **Quarantine Module** — Stores rejected records for steward review instead of silently dropping them.
6. **Audit Log** — Records every transform applied, row counts, DQ metrics per load.
7. **Master Data Hub** — Provides reference lookups for standardization.
8. **DQ Dashboard** — Visualizes scores and trends over time.

**Data Quality Scoring Methodology (3.5 Marks):**

The **Composite Data Quality Score** is a weighted average of the four most critical dimensions:

$$
DQ_{score} = w_1 \cdot C + w_2 \cdot A + w_3 \cdot CS + w_4 \cdot U
$$

with weights summing to 1.

**Example (Worked Out):**

Suppose for a daily batch of **20,000** customer records:
- Non-NULL mandatory fields: **19,000** → $C = 95\%$
- Values matching reference: **19,600** → $A = 98\%$
- Values consistent across CRM and ERP: **18,800** → $CS = 94\%$
- Distinct entities after dedup: **19,200** → $U = 96\%$

With weights $w_1 = 0.25$, $w_2 = 0.30$, $w_3 = 0.25$, $w_4 = 0.20$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DQ_{score} &= (0.25 \times 95) + (0.30 \times 98) + (0.25 \times 94) + (0.20 \times 96) \\
&= 23.75 + 29.40 + 23.50 + 19.20 \\
&= 95.85
\end{aligned}
$$

A score of **95.85%** indicates excellent quality, suitable for mission-critical analytics.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Imputation Techniques (4 Marks):**

1. **Mean Imputation** — Replace missing numeric value with column average.
2. **Median Imputation** — Replace with column median (robust to outliers).
3. **Mode Imputation** — Replace categorical NULLs with most frequent category.
4. **Forward / Backward Fill** — Propagate last/next valid value (time series).
5. **KNN Imputation** — Use nearest neighbors to estimate value.
6. **Regression Imputation** — Predict from other correlated columns.
7. **Hot-Deck Imputation** — Fill from a similar record in the same dataset.
8. **Multiple Imputation** — Generate several plausible values and average.

**Dependency on Missingness Mechanism (3 Marks):**

| Mechanism | Description | Recommended Technique |
|---|---|---|
| **MCAR — Missing Completely At Random** | Missingness has no pattern; unrelated to any data | Mean, Median, Mode, or simple deletion |
| **MAR — Missing At Random** | Missingness depends on observed data (e.g., men less likely to fill `Pregnancy` field) | Regression imputation, KNN imputation |
| **MNAR — Missing Not At Random** | Missingness depends on the value itself (e.g., high earners skip `Salary`) | Domain modeling, source re-query, sophisticated ML methods |

**Conclusion:**
The choice of imputation is not arbitrary — it must align with the underlying mechanism. Misclassifying MNAR as MCAR leads to biased warehouse analytics.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on Data Quality Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Data Quality with Data Governance** — These are *related but different*. Examiners specifically look for the distinction. Marks are deducted if used interchangeably.
> 2. **Skipping the Formula** — For any question involving DQ scoring, you *must* write the composite formula with weights and units. A verbal description without math loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting Real Examples** — Generic answers without concrete examples (`"Mr. Rahul Kumar"` standardization, missing `Email` imputation) are marked as vague.
> 4. **Not Mentioning Survivorship Rules** — When discussing inconsistent data handling, examiners expect explicit mention of *which value wins* when sources conflict.
> 5. **Ignoring the Audit / Quarantine Step** — Many students describe only cleansing but forget to mention that rejected records go to a quarantine table, not silently dropped.
> 6. **Missing the Governance People Layer** — Governance is not just technology; it is roles (CDO, Stewards) and policies. Answering only tools loses half the marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision)

- ✅ **Data Quality** is the *condition*; **Data Governance** is the *system*.
- ✅ Six canonical DQ dimensions: **Completeness, Accuracy, Consistency, Timeliness, Uniqueness, Validity**.
- ✅ Missing data handling techniques: **Ignore, Mean/Median/Mode, Forward Fill, Predictive Imputation, Source Re-query**.
- ✅ Missingness mechanisms: **MCAR, MAR, MNAR** — choice of technique must match the mechanism.
- ✅ Inconsistent data is resolved using **standardization, lookup tables, parsing, and survivorship rules**.
- ✅ The **composite DQ score** is a weighted average: $DQ_{score} = w_1 C + w_2 A + w_3 CS + w_4 U$.
- ✅ The ETL **Staging Area** is where profiling and cleansing happen — never transform in place on the source.
- ✅ **Quarantine tables** hold rejected records; **audit logs** record every transformation.
- ✅ Data Governance has **four layers**: Policy, People, Process, Technology.
- ✅ Key governance roles: **CDO, Data Stewards, Data Engineers, Data Council**.
- ✅ **Integrity** has three forms: **Entity (PK), Referential (FK), Domain (value range)**.
- ✅ Real-world impact: faulty DQ in banking, healthcare, and telecom can cause legal, financial, and safety disasters.
- ✅ For 14-mark answers, always include: definition + causes + techniques + example + diagram mention + conclusion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Warehouse Optimization & OLAP: Query Optimization in Data Warehouses, Indexing Strategies, Partitioning, Performance Tuning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT412 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Warehouse Optimization & OLAP: Query Optimization in Data Warehouses, Indexing Strategies, Partitioning, Performance Tuning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Query Optimization in Data Warehouses — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Query Optimization in Data Warehouses** is the process of transforming a high-level declarative query (typically SQL) into an efficient **query execution plan (QEP)** by selecting the optimal sequence of physical operations (joins, scans, aggregations) and access paths (indexes, partitions) that minimize the total cost — measured in I/O, CPU, and response time.

A Data Warehouse (DW) is fundamentally read-heavy, holding **terabytes to petabytes** of historical, consolidated data. Unlike OLTP systems where optimization focuses on short transactions, DW optimization emphasizes:

- **Star/Snowflake join ordering**
- **Star-join optimization** (Cartesian product → late binding)
- **Bitmap index exploitation**
- **Partition pruning**
- **Parallel query execution**
- **Materialized view rewriting**

The optimizer must therefore be **cost-based**, not rule-based, and must exploit the **read-mostly, append-only, denormalized** nature of dimensional data.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 emphasizes the **Differential** between OLTP query optimization and OLAP/DW query optimization. Board questions are highly likely on cost estimation, index selection, and partition pruning.

---

### 1.2 Indexing Strategies — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **Index** is a physical access structure (a separate disk-resident data structure) that allows the DB engine to locate rows satisfying a predicate **without scanning the entire fact table**. In a DW, indexes are crucial because the fact table is typically 100×–10,000× larger than dimension tables.

The standard DW indexing taxonomy:

| Index Type | Best Suited For | Structure |
|---|---|---|
| **B-Tree Index** | High-cardinality columns (e.g., Order_ID, Customer_ID) | Balanced tree |
| **Bitmap Index** | Low-cardinality columns (e.g., Gender, Region, Status) | Bit arrays per distinct value |
| **Star Join Index** | Pre-joined fact + dimension row IDs | Composite bitmap |
| **Join Index** | Pre-computed join results between fact and dimension | (Fact_RID, Dim_RID) pairs |
| **B-Tree Clustered Index** | Range queries on date/time | Sorted physical storage |
| **Function-Based Index** | Queries using functions (e.g., `UPPER(name)`, `YEAR(date)`) | Indexed expression |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cardinality** = Number of distinct values in a column. **High cardinality** = B-Tree. **Low cardinality** = Bitmap.

---

### 1.3 Partitioning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Partitioning** is the physical decomposition of a large table (especially the fact table) into smaller, more manageable, independently stored **partitions** that share the same logical schema but reside on different physical segments (files, tablespaces, disks). Queries that touch only some partitions benefit from **partition pruning**.

Three canonical partitioning schemes (and KTU's frequent exam favourite):

1. **Horizontal (Range) Partitioning** — splits rows by value range (e.g., by year, by region).
2. **Vertical Partitioning** — splits columns into separate tables (rare in DWs).
3. **Composite (Hybrid) Partitioning** — combines range + hash, e.g., range by year, then hash by customer_id within year.

A special DW technique is **Star Partitioning**, where the fact table is partitioned by one dimension (commonly Time) and all dimension tables are also partitioned on the same key to enable **partition-wise joins**.

---

### 1.4 Performance Tuning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Tuning** in a DW is the iterative, measurement-driven process of optimizing the system across **four axes**: (1) **Schema tuning** (star schemas, denormalization, materialized views), (2) **Index tuning** (creating, dropping, monitoring indexes), (3) **Physical tuning** (partitioning, clustering, tablespace allocation), and (4) **Query tuning** (rewriting SQL, hints, hint-less cost evaluation).

The goal is to reduce the three classical DW bottleneck metrics:
- **Query response time**
- **Throughput (queries/hour)**
- **Load window time (ETL duration)**

---

### 1.5 Intuitive Analogies (Plain-English Mental Models)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Giant Library (Indexing):** Imagine a library with 10 million books but no card catalog. A B-Tree index is the **Dewey Decimal System** (sorted, hierarchical) — you go to shelf 5, then row 3, then slot 2. A Bitmap index is a **wall of checkboxes** labeled "Horror", "Romance", "Sci-Fi" — one checkbox per book, so to find all "Romance" you just look at the "Romance" column. For a column with 5 values, the bitmap takes almost no space and answers queries instantly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Filing Cabinet (Partitioning):** Imagine a 10,000-page legal ledger. Instead of one giant binder, you split it into 12 binders, one per month. A query asking "How many contracts in March?" only requires opening **1 binder**, not all 12. This is **partition pruning**. The same ledger split horizontally by year then by alphabet is **composite partitioning**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — The Google Maps Route Planner (Query Optimizer):** When you ask Google Maps for directions, it doesn't just give you *a* route — it evaluates millions of routes and picks the **fastest** based on real-time traffic, distance, and road quality. A cost-based query optimizer does the same: it estimates the cost of dozens of possible execution plans (full scan vs. index scan, nested loop vs. hash join) and chooses the cheapest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 4 — The Cached Calculator (Performance Tuning):** Computing 365 × 24 × 7 every day is wasteful. You pre-compute it once and store the result. That's a **materialized view** — a performance tuning tool that trades storage for speed.

---

### 1.6 The Optimization Stack — Conceptual Pyramid

```
                ┌────────────────────────┐
                │   Query / Application  │  ← SQL rewrites, hints
                ├────────────────────────┤
                │     Materialized       │  ← Pre-computed aggregates
                │        Views           │
                ├────────────────────────┤
                │       Indexes          │  ← B-Tree, Bitmap, Join
                ├────────────────────────┤
                │     Partitioning       │  ← Range, Hash, Composite
                ├────────────────────────┤
                │   Physical Storage     │  ← Tablespaces, Disk I/O
                └────────────────────────┘
        (Top: high abstraction, easy to change
         Bottom: low abstraction, expensive to change)
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always state the **layer of the optimization pyramid** in your answer. A "good" KTU answer connects the chosen technique to the *layer* it belongs to (index = access path, partition = physical, view = logical).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cost-Based Query Optimization — The Heart of DW Optimization

A cost-based optimizer computes an estimated cost for every candidate execution plan and picks the minimum. The total cost is a weighted sum of **I/O cost, CPU cost, and communication cost**.

#### 2.1.1 Cost Model Decomposition

A query execution plan is a tree of relational operators. The cost of each operator is estimated independently, then summed up the tree (with weighted adjustments for pipelining).

**Logical steps the optimizer follows:**

1. **Parse** SQL → syntax tree
2. **Validate** → semantic check (table/column existence, types)
3. **Logical optimization** → query rewrite (predicate pushdown, subquery flattening, view merging)
4. **Physical optimization** → enumerate physical plans (join order, join algorithm, access path)
5. **Cost estimation** → assign estimated cost to each plan
6. **Plan selection** → choose minimum-cost plan
7. **Plan execution** → execute with runtime statistics

> [!NOTE]
> In modern DW systems (Oracle Exadata, Teradata, Snowflake, Redshift), this is augmented by **adaptive query execution**, where the optimizer re-optimizes mid-query if actual row counts deviate significantly from estimates.

---

### 2.2 Selectivity — The Most Important Statistic in DW Optimization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selectivity** is the fraction of rows in a relation that satisfy a given predicate. It directly drives the cost estimate for every access path and join.

**Cardinalities KTU loves:**

- **Cardinality of relation R** = `|R|` (number of tuples)
- **Selectivity of predicate p** = `|σ_p(R)| / |R|`
- **Number of distinct values (NDV)** in column C = `D(C)`

### 2.3 Index Selection Decision Logic (Step-by-Step)

The optimizer chooses between a **Full Table Scan (FTS)** and an **Index Scan** based on the **Selectivity Threshold Rule**:

- If **selectivity ≤ ~5–10%** → Index Scan is cheaper (fetch few index entries + few table blocks)
- If **selectivity > 10%** → Full Table Scan is cheaper (sequential I/O is faster than random I/O on large tables)

> [!NOTE]
> The 5–10% threshold is approximate; the true threshold depends on **clustering factor**, **index height**, and **buffer cache hit rate**.

---

### 2.4 Indexing Strategies — Deep Dive

#### 2.4.1 B-Tree Index

A balanced tree where:
- **Leaf level** = sorted `(key, ROWID)` pairs
- **Branch levels** = routing nodes
- **Height h** ≈ `⌈log_F(|R|)⌉` where F = fan-out (typical F ≈ 100–200)

**Strengths:** Excellent for high-cardinality columns, supports range scans, equality, and ORDER BY.
**Weaknesses:** Space overhead ~1.2×–2× of data; poor for low-cardinality columns.

#### 2.4.2 Bitmap Index

A bitmap index on a column C with `k` distinct values stores `k` bitmaps, each of length `|R|`. Bit `i` of bitmap `j` is 1 iff row `i` has value `v_j`.

**Strengths:**
- Extremely **space-efficient for low cardinality** (k ≤ 100, ideally ≤ 20)
- **AND/OR/NOT** operations become bitwise operations — extremely fast
- Perfect for **star schema fact table** queries that filter on multiple low-cardinality dimension columns

**Weaknesses:**
- **Inferior for high cardinality** (millions of distinct values → huge bitmaps)
- **Costly updates** — modifying one row requires updating *every* bitmap (DWs are mostly read-only, so this is fine)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Why is Bitmap Indexing preferred in DWs over B-Tree for low-cardinality columns?"* → The exact answer: it uses **bitwise operations** instead of tree traversal, and on low-cardinality data it has dramatically lower space overhead.

#### 2.4.3 Join Index

A pre-computed structure that stores `(Fact_RID, Dim_RID)` pairs for one or more join columns. When a query joins fact F to dimension D on key K, the join index provides the matching RIDs **without actually performing the join**.

**Mathematical representation:**

$$JI_{F \bowtie_{K} D} = \{(r_f, r_d) \mid r_f \in F,\ r_d \in D,\ r_f.K = r_d.K\}$$

#### 2.4.4 Star Join Optimization

A **star schema** has a central fact table connected to multiple dimension tables. The naive plan joins fact × dim1 × dim2 × dim3 → Cartesian explosion. Modern optimizers use **star transformation**:

1. Transform each dimension predicate into a **bitmap** on the dimension's primary key.
2. Convert the bitmap to a **fact-table row-id bitmap** using the join index.
3. AND/OR the row-id bitmaps together.
4. Retrieve only the matching fact rows at the end.

This avoids the Cartesian product entirely — KTU's "trick question" favourite.

---

### 2.5 Partitioning — Deep Dive

#### 2.5.1 Partition Pruning

If a query has a predicate on the partition key, the optimizer can **eliminate entire partitions** from the scan. If a query scans only 1 of 12 monthly partitions, the effective I/O is reduced by **12×**.

**Pruning effectiveness:**

$$\text{Pruning Ratio} = \frac{\text{Partitions Scanned}}{\text{Total Partitions}}$$

A pruning ratio of `1/12` means **92% of the table is skipped**.

#### 2.5.2 Partition-Wise Joins

If both tables in a join are partitioned on the join key, the join can be executed as `N` independent sub-joins in parallel — one per partition pair. This is **linear speedup** with `N` partitions (and `N` parallel workers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite Partitioning (Range + Hash):** Most common in production DWs. The outer level is **range** (e.g., month) for time-series pruning, the inner level is **hash** (e.g., customer_id) for even data distribution and parallel join support.

#### 2.5.3 Partitioning vs. Indexing — When to Use What?

| Scenario | Best Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Filter on date range | **Range Partitioning** | Pruning eliminates whole months |
| Filter on high-cardinality attribute | **B-Tree Index** | Selectivity is low; index seeks win |
| Filter on low-cardinality attribute (e.g., status) | **Bitmap Index** | Bitwise operations dominate |
| Join of fact with multiple dimensions | **Star Join Index / Bitmap Star Transform** | Avoids Cartesian product |
| Massive ETL loads | **Range Partition + Exchange** | Old partitions are archived, new ones pre-created |
| Parallel aggregation | **Hash Partition on group-by key** | Enables partition-wise parallel aggregation |

---

### 2.6 Performance Tuning — The Four Axes

#### 2.6.1 Schema Tuning

- Replace snowflake with **star** (denormalize) to reduce joins
- Use **fact constellations** (multiple fact tables sharing dimensions) for multi-subject analysis
- Apply **slowly changing dimensions (SCD Type 1/2/3)** correctly

#### 2.6.2 Index Tuning

- **Drop** unused indexes (every index slows down loads)
- **Create** bitmap indexes on fact table's foreign keys (low-cardinality)
- **Rebuild** B-Tree indexes periodically to reduce fragmentation
- **Monitor** index usage via `DBA_INDEX_USAGE` (Oracle) or `pg_stat_user_indexes` (PostgreSQL)

#### 2.6.3 Physical Tuning

- **Partition** fact tables by time
- **Cluster** tables to match common sort order (e.g., sort fact by date_key)
- **Distribute** tablespaces across disks (RAID, separate I/O channels)
- **Pre-size** extents to avoid fragmentation during bulk loads

#### 2.6.4 Query Tuning

- **Predicate pushdown** — push filters as close to the scan as possible
- **Star transformation** — avoid Cartesian joins
- **Avoid `SELECT *`** — fetch only required columns (columnar stores make this trivial)
- **Use set-oriented SQL** — avoid row-by-row cursors
- **Materialized view rewriting** — let the optimizer substitute pre-computed aggregates

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Selectivity** of predicate p | $\sigma_p = \frac{\mid\sigma_p(R)\mid}{\mid R\mid}$ | Dimensionless ratio ∈ [0, 1] |
| 2 | **Cost of Full Table Scan** | $C_{FTS} = \mid R \mid \cdot C_{row}$ | `C_row` = cost per row read (in time units) |
| 3 | **Cost of Index Scan (range)** | $C_{idx} = h + S \cdot \mid R \mid \cdot C_{row} + \text{lookup}$ | `h` = index height; `S` = selectivity |
| 4 | **B-Tree Height** | $h \approx \lceil \log_F(\mid R \mid)\rceil$ | F = fan-out (typically 100–200) |
| 5 | **Bitmap Size** | $S_{bitmap} = k \cdot \mid R \mid$ bits $= \frac{k \cdot \mid R \mid}{8}$ bytes | k = number of distinct values |
| 6 | **Bitmap AND/OR cost** | $O(\mid R \mid / 8)$ per operation | Bytes processed = bitmaps compared |
| 7 | **Bitmap vs B-Tree crossover** | When $\mid R \mid \cdot k / 8 < 1.2 \cdot \mid R \mid$ | Roughly when $k < 10$ |
| 8 | **Partition Pruning Ratio** | $P = N_{scanned} / N_{total}$ | Lower is better; 1/12 = 91.7% pruned |
| 9 | **Star Join Cost Reduction** | From $O(\mid F \mid \cdot \prod \mid D_i \mid)$ → $O(\mid F \mid + \sum \mid D_i \mid)$ | Linear in fact table, not Cartesian |
| 10 | **Materialized View Speedup** | Speedup = Query Time (re-compute) / Query Time (view) | Trade-off: storage + refresh cost |
| 11 | **Parallel Speedup (Partition-wise)** | $T_{parallel} \approx T_{serial} / N$ + overhead | Amdahl's Law applies |
| 12 | **Index Storage Overhead** | $S_{index} \approx 1.2 \cdot \mid R \mid$ for B-Tree | Bitmap is `k/8` bytes per row |
| 13 | **ETL Load Window Rule** | $T_{load} < T_{available}$ | Else → schedule more parallel loaders |
| 14 | **Clustering Factor** | $CF \approx \mid R \mid$ → good, $CF \approx \mid blocks \mid$ → bad | Lower CF → more efficient range scan |
| 15 | **Buffer Cache Hit Ratio Target** | $> 95\%$ for OLAP | Below → increase buffer cache size |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention (KTU standard):** Costs are typically measured in *time units* (e.g., disk page fetches at 5 ms each) or in *I/O block counts*. Always state your units in a board answer.

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

These techniques are **industry-critical**:

- **Snowflake / Redshift** → uses zone maps, micro-partitions, and columnar storage
- **Teradata** → pioneered hash-partitioned, parallel DW engines
- **Oracle Exadata** → uses Smart Scans (offload processing to storage), bitmap indexes, and hybrid columnar compression
- **Google BigQuery** → automatically partitions and clusters tables
- **Apache Hive / Spark SQL** → uses cost-based optimizer (CBO) in Hive 0.14+, with similar index/partition logic

> [!TIP]
> In a **Big Data context**, partitioning maps directly to **HDFS partitioning** (e.g., `partition(year=2024, month=03)`) and indexing maps to **Parquet/ORC min-max statistics** (zone maps).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: B-Tree Index Height and Cost

> **Goal:** Derive the cost of a B-Tree range scan in a DW and show the crossover with Full Table Scan.

**Step 1 — Number of leaf pages**

A B-Tree with fan-out `F` (entries per page) and `|R|` rows has approximately `|R|/F` leaf pages. Branch pages multiply by `F` at each level up.

**Step 2 — Height of the tree**

$$h = \left\lceil \log_F \left( \frac{|R|}{F} \right) \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{\log(|R|/F)}{\log(F)} \right\rceil$$

For a fact table with `|R| = 10^9` rows and `F = 200`:

$$\frac{|R|}{F} = \frac{10^9}{200} = 5 \times 10^6$$

$$h = \left\lceil \frac{\log(5 \times 10^6)}{\log(200)} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{6.699}{2.301} \right\rceil = \lceil 2.91 \rceil = 3$$

So 3 page reads to reach any leaf.

**Step 3 — Cost of a range scan returning `S · |R|` rows**

$$C_{idx} = h \cdot C_{page} + S \cdot |R| \cdot C_{row} + S \cdot |R| \cdot C_{lookup}$$

**Step 4 — Full Table Scan cost**

$$C_{FTS} = N_{blocks} \cdot C_{page} = \left\lceil \frac{|R|}{rows\_per\_block} \right\rceil \cdot C_{page}$$

**Step 5 — Find the selectivity threshold** where `C_idx = C_FTS`

Setting `C_idx = C_FTS` and solving for `S`:

$$S \cdot |R| \cdot C_{row} \cdot (1 + C_{lookup}) = N_{blocks} \cdot C_{page} - h \cdot C_{page}$$

$$S = \frac{C_{page} \cdot (N_{blocks} - h)}{|R| \cdot C_{row} \cdot (1 + C_{lookup})}$$

For typical DW values (`C_page = 5 ms`, `C_row = 0.01 ms`, `C_lookup = 1`, `|R| = 10^9`, `N_blocks = 10^6`, `h = 3`):

$$S = \frac{5 \times (10^6 - 3)}{10^9 \times 0.01 \times 2} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^7} = 0.25$$

So **selectivity above 25%** favors FTS. The rule of thumb "5–10%" applies to **OLTP-sized** tables; DW fact tables can tolerate **higher** selectivity for index use because of higher `C_page` / `C_row` ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The DW threshold is *higher* than OLTP because the cost of a random I/O in DWs is much larger relative to the cost of a sequential I/O (mechanical disks, large blocks). On SSDs, the threshold drops back to ~5%.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Bitmap Index Space

**Step 1 — Size of one bitmap**

A bitmap for `|R|` rows uses `|R|` bits. In bytes:

$$S_{one\ bitmap} = \frac{|R|}{8}\ \text{bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Total size for k distinct values**

$$S_{total} = k \cdot \frac{|R|}{8} = \frac{k \cdot |R|}{8}\ \text{bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to MB**

$$S_{MB} = \frac{k \cdot |R|}{8 \times 1024^2} = \frac{k \cdot |R|}{8{,}388{,}608}$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example: fact table with 10⁹ rows, 5 distinct regions**

$$S_{MB} = \frac{5 \times 10^9}{8{,}388{,}608} \approx 596\ \text{MB}$$

**Step 5 — Compare to B-Tree on the same column**

B-Tree of `5 \times 10^6` leaf entries × 16 bytes per entry = 80 MB, plus branch pages ≈ 81 MB. Comparable size for k=5. **As k grows, bitmap size grows linearly**, while B-Tree stays roughly constant — so bitmap wins only for **low k**.

> [!TIP]
> **Crossover Point:** Bitmap and B-Tree have similar space at `k ≈ 8–10`. Below that, bitmap wins. Above that, B-Tree wins.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Partition Pruning Speedup

**Step 1 — Total cost without pruning**

$$C_{no\_prune} = N_{partitions} \cdot C_{scan\_per\_partition}$$

**Step 2 — Cost with pruning**

$$C_{pruned} = N_{scanned} \cdot C_{scan\_per\_partition}$$

**Step 3 — Speedup factor**

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{C_{no\_prune}}{C_{pruned}} = \frac{N_{partitions}}{N_{scanned}}$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example: Monthly partitions, query for March only**

`N_partitions = 12`, `N_scanned = 1`

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{12}{1} = 12\times$$

**Step 5 — Account for non-uniform partition sizes**

If partitions are of unequal size (e.g., some years have 5× more data), use size-weighted pruning:

$$C_{pruned} = \sum_{i \in scanned} \text{size}(P_i)$$

The speedup is no longer purely a ratio of partition counts.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Four Strategies

| Optimization Strategy | Layer | Cost (Pre) | Cost (Post) | Storage Overhead | Maintenance Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **B-Tree Index** | Access Path | `h + S·|R|·C_row` | `h` if exact match | ~120% of table | Medium (rebuilds) | High-cardinality, range |
| **Bitmap Index** | Access Path | `k·|R|/8` bytes | Bitwise AND/OR | `k/8` bytes/row | Low (read-only) | Low-cardinality dimensions |
| **Join Index** | Access Path | Join compute cost | O(1) lookup | O(\|join result\|) | Medium (refresh on load) | Frequent fact-dim joins |
| **Materialized View** | Logical | Re-compute aggregate | Pre-computed | O(\|result\|) | High (refresh on update) | Repeated aggregations |
| **Range Partitioning** | Physical | Full scan | `N_scanned/N_total · C` | Negligible | Low (auto) | Time-series queries |
| **Hash Partitioning** | Physical | Serial processing | `1/N` parallel | Negligible | Low (auto) | Parallel joins/aggregations |
| **Composite Partitioning** | Physical | Combined above | Both benefits | Negligible | Medium | Large fact tables |
| **Query Rewrite** | Logical | Original cost | Reduced plan cost | None | None | Suboptimal SQL |
| **Star Transformation** | Logical | `O(|F|·∏|D_i|)` | `O(|F| + ∑|D_i|)` | None (uses bitmaps) | None | Star schema queries |
| **Columnar Storage** | Physical | Reads full rows | Reads only columns | Same or less | Low | Analytical scans |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — End-to-End

> **Scenario:** A retail DW fact table `Sales_Fact` has `|F| = 5 × 10^8` rows, 20 columns, 8 KB blocks → 250 rows per block → `2 × 10^6` blocks. Block I/O = 5 ms, row processing = 0.001 ms. The query is:
>
> `SELECT SUM(amount) FROM Sales_Fact WHERE year=2024 AND region='APAC' AND product_category='Electronics';`
>
> Cardinalities: `D(year)=5`, `D(region)=4`, `D(category)=10`. Assume uniform distribution.

**Step 1 — Estimate selectivity of each predicate**

$$S_{year} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20$$
$$S_{region} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$
$$S_{category} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.10$$

**Step 2 — Combined selectivity (independence assumption)**

$$S_{total} = 0.20 \times 0.25 \times 0.10 = 0.005 = 0.5\%$$

**Step 3 — Estimated rows returned**

$$|result| = S_{total} \cdot |F| = 0.005 \times 5 \times 10^8 = 2.5 \times 10^6\ \text{rows}$$

**Step 4 — Full Table Scan cost**

$$C_{FTS} = 2 \times 10^6 \times 5\ \text{ms} = 10^7\ \text{ms} = 10{,}000\ \text{seconds} \approx 2.78\ \text{hours}$$

**Step 5 — Bitmap Star Transformation cost**

- Each dimension predicate → 1 bitmap lookup (cost = `|F|/8` bytes = 62.5 MB scanned from cache)
- 3 bitmaps ANDed → `O(|F|/8)` = 62.5 MB
- Result bitmap has 2.5 × 10⁶ bits set
- Final fetch: 2.5 × 10⁶ rows = 10,000 blocks = 50,000 ms ≈ 50 seconds

**Step 6 — Speedup**

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{10{,}000\ \text{s}}{50\ \text{s}} = 200\times$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The 200× speedup is the entire reason DW query optimization matters.** Without the bitmap star transformation, this query would take 3 hours; with it, 50 seconds.

---

### 3.6 Derivation: Cost-Based Join Order Selection

For a 3-way join `F ⨝ D1 ⨝ D2`, the cost varies dramatically with order. Assume:

- `|F| = 10^9`, `|D1| = 10^4`, `|D2| = 10^5`
- All joins are on foreign keys (1:∞)
- Hash join cost ≈ `C_{hash}(outer) + C_{hash}(inner) + C_{probe}`

**Plan A: `(F ⨝ D1) ⨝ D2`**

$$C_A = C_{hash}(F) + C_{hash}(D_1) + C_{probe}(F) + C_{hash}(result_1) + C_{hash}(D_2) + C_{probe}(result_2)$$

Since `F ⨝ D1` produces ~|F| rows (1:∞):

$$C_A \approx 2C_{hash}(F) + C_{hash}(D_1) + C_{hash}(D_2) + 2C_{probe}(F)$$

**Plan B: `(D1 ⨝ D2) ⨝ F`**

This is the "smallest intermediate first" plan:

$$C_B = C_{hash}(D_1) + C_{hash}(D_2) + C_{probe}(D_1) + C_{hash}(result_{1,2}) + C_{hash}(F) + C_{probe}(F)$$

`D1 ⨝ D2` produces ~|D1| rows (small), then join with F:

$$C_B \approx C_{hash}(D_1) + C_{hash}(D_2) + C_{probe}(D_1) + C_{hash}(F) + C_{probe}(F)$$

**Comparison:**

$$C_A - C_B \approx C_{hash}(F) + C_{probe}(F)$$

Plan B saves the cost of one hash of F. The optimizer always tries to **build on smaller intermediate results**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favorite:** "Why does the optimizer prefer `(Small ⨝ Small) ⨝ Large` over `(Large ⨝ Small) ⨝ Small`?" → Answer: building hash tables on the largest relation twice costs far more than building once on a small result.
