```mermaid
    D1[Dimension D1] -->|Bitmap on PK| FT
    D2[Dimension D2] -->|Bitmap on PK| FT
    D3[Dimension D3] -->|Bitmap on PK| FT
    FT --> B[AND of Row-ID Bitmaps]
    B --> R[Fetch Matching Fact Rows]
    R --> AGG[Aggregation]
    AGG --> OUT[Final Result]
    style FT fill:#fff3cd
    style B fill:#d4f4dd
    style R fill:#d4f4dd
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **Star Transform** step replaces the naive `F ⨝ D1 ⨝ D2 ⨝ D3` Cartesian-product join with a **bitmap-AND** operation, then fetches only matching fact rows — reducing cost from `O(|F|·∏|D_i|)` to `O(|F| + ∑|D_i|)`.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

> Explain why **Bitmap Indexing** is preferred over B-Tree indexing for low-cardinality columns in a Data Warehouse. Support your answer with a brief cost comparison.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Bitmap indexing stores `k` bitmaps (one per distinct value) each of length `|R|` bits. For a low-cardinality column (`k ≤ 10`):
- **Space** = `k · |R| / 8` bytes — very small.
- **Lookup** = single bitmap fetch + bitwise AND/OR — **O(|R|/8)** bytes scanned.

A B-Tree on the same column would still have `h ≈ 3` levels and `|R|/F` leaf pages. For 0.5% selectivity, the B-Tree would need `h + 0.005 · |R|` accesses, but the bitmap would need only **0.005 · |R| / 8** bytes (≈ 1000× less work).

Additionally, **multi-predicate** queries (year, region, category) require **multiple AND operations** in bitmap (bitwise — extremely fast) vs. **multiple index intersections** in B-Tree (costly lookups). Hence bitmap wins in DWs on low-cardinality dimension columns. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

> Differentiate between **partition pruning** and **partition-wise joins** in the context of DW query optimization. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Partition Pruning | Partition-Wise Join |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Eliminate irrelevant partitions from scan | Parallelize a join across matching partitions |
| **Trigger** | Predicate on partition key of **one** table | Both tables partitioned on the **same** join key |
| **Speedup** | `N_total / N_scanned` (I/O reduction) | `N` (linear parallel speedup) |
| **Example** | Query `WHERE year=2024` against monthly-partitioned fact table → only 1 of 12 partitions scanned | Both `Sales_Fact` and `Customer_Dim` are hash-partitioned on `customer_id` → join executes in parallel across partition pairs |
| **Reduces** | I/O bandwidth | Wall-clock join time |

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Describe the **Star Schema Query Optimization** process. Explain how **star transformation** avoids the Cartesian product problem in a 4-dimensional star schema with a fact table `F` and dimensions `D1, D2, D3, D4`.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A Data Warehouse has a fact table `Sales_Fact` with **5 × 10⁸ rows**, partitioned by month over 24 months (uniform size). A query asks for total sales in **March 2024 only**. Calculate:
> - (i) the partition pruning ratio,
> - (ii) the speedup factor over a non-pruned scan,
> - (iii) the I/O cost if a full table scan costs **10,000 seconds** and each partition block read costs **5 ms/block** with **2 × 10⁶ total blocks** across all partitions.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Star Schema Query Optimization — 7 marks**

A **star schema** has one central fact table connected to multiple dimension tables via foreign-key joins. The naive execution plan is:

$$\pi_{aggr}\left(\sigma_p\left(F \bowtie_{K_1} D_1 \bowtie_{K_2} D_2 \bowtie_{K_3} D_3 \bowtie_{K_4} D_4\right)\right)$$

If executed naively, the optimizer may produce an **n-way Cartesian product** of dimensions followed by a final join with the fact table, leading to cost:

$$C_{naive} = O\left(|F| \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{4} |D_i|\right)$$

For `|D_i| ≈ 10^4`, this is `|F| · 10^{16}` — **infeasible**.

**Star Transformation steps (KTU-board answer):**

1. **Predicate isolation:** Each dimension predicate is rewritten to filter its dimension table first:
$$D_1' = \sigma_{p_1}(D_1),\ D_2' = \sigma_{p_2}(D_2),\ D_3' = \sigma_{p_3}(D_3),\ D_4' = \sigma_{p_4}(D_4)$$

2. **Bitmap creation on dimension primary keys:** For each filtered dimension, build a bitmap `B_i` of length `|D_i|` marking the rows that survive the predicate.

3. **Bitmap-to-Fact-Row-ID translation:** Using the **join index** (or a B-Tree on `F.K_i`), convert each dimension bitmap `B_i` into a **fact-table row-ID bitmap** `F_i` of length `|F|`.

4. **Bitmap intersection (AND):** Combine the fact row-ID bitmaps:
$$F_{result} = F_1 \land F_2 \land F_3 \land F_4$$

5. **Fact table fetch:** Retrieve only the rows of `F` whose bit is set in `F_result`:
$$F' = \sigma_{F_{result}}(F)$$

6. **Aggregation:** Apply the aggregation on `F'`.

**Cost of star transformation:**

$$C_{star} = O\left(|F| + \sum_{i=1}^{4} |D_i|\right)$$

This is **linear in the fact table size** — a **massive** improvement over the Cartesian product.

**7-mark rubric:**
- (i) Star schema definition (1 mark)
- (ii) Cartesian product problem (1 mark)
- (iii) 6 transformation steps (3 marks: 0.5 each)
- (iv) Cost comparison (1 mark)
- (v) Conclusion (1 mark)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- `|F| = 5 × 10⁸` rows
- Total partitions = 24 (one per month, 2 years)
- Partitioning is uniform → 1/24 of rows per partition
- Total blocks = `2 × 10⁶`
- Full scan cost = 10,000 s
- Query: March 2024 only (1 specific month)

**(i) Partition Pruning Ratio:**

$$P = \frac{N_{scanned}}{N_{total}} = \frac{1}{24} \approx 0.0417$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Speedup Factor:**

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{P} = \frac{24}{1} = 24\times$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) I/O Cost after pruning:**

- Blocks per partition: `2 × 10⁶ / 24 ≈ 83,333` blocks
- I/O cost for 1 partition: `83,333 × 5 ms = 416,665 ms ≈ 416.7 seconds`

**Verifying against full scan:**

$$C_{pruned} = C_{FTS} \times P = 10{,}000 \times \frac{1}{24} \approx 416.7\ \text{seconds}$$

**(3 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often **forget to convert units** (ms vs s). Always show the unit conversion.
> - Some students incorrectly compute `83,333 × 5 = 416,665` but forget to divide by `1000` for seconds.
> - The pruning ratio is a **dimensionless** fraction; the speedup is its **reciprocal** — getting these confused loses 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Compare **B-Tree, Bitmap, and Join Indexes** in Data Warehouses across the dimensions of: (i) column cardinality suitability, (ii) storage overhead, (iii) maintenance cost on bulk loads, (iv) query types supported, and (v) typical use case in a star schema.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A fact table `Orders_Fact` has **10⁹ rows** with **8 KB blocks** (250 rows/block). You need to decide between a **B-Tree index** on `customer_id` (cardinality 5 × 10⁶) and a **full table scan** for the query:
> `SELECT * FROM Orders_Fact WHERE customer_id = 12345;`
>
> Given: index height = 3, average seek cost = 5 ms, block read cost = 5 ms, row processing = 0.001 ms.
> - (i) Estimate the cost of the **index-based plan**.
> - (ii) Estimate the cost of the **full table scan**.
> - (iii) Recommend which plan the optimizer should choose.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of Indexing Strategies — 7 marks**

| Dimension | B-Tree | Bitmap | Join Index |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Cardinality** | High (≥ 100 distinct) | Low (≤ 20–100 distinct) | N/A (join attribute) |
| **(ii) Storage** | ~1.2 × \|R\| | `k · \|R\|/8` bytes | `\|F\| + \|D\|` RIDs |
| **(iii) Maintenance** | Medium (rebuild on bulk load) | Low (DWs are read-only) | High (refresh on dimension change) |
| **(iv) Query Types** | Equality, Range, ORDER BY | AND/OR/NOT predicates, multi-dim filtering | Equi-joins (pre-computed) |
| **(v) Star Schema Use** | Fact table PK, date columns | All dimension foreign keys in fact | Fact↔Dimension hot joins |

**(1.4 marks per row × 5 rows = 7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Comparison — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- `|F| = 10⁹` rows
- `rows_per_block = 250`
- `N_blocks = 10⁹ / 250 = 4 × 10⁶` blocks
- `h = 3`, `seek = 5 ms`, `block_read = 5 ms`, `row_proc = 0.001 ms`
- Query: `customer_id = 12345` → **point query** returning **1 row** (assuming `customer_id` is unique in the dim, but here `customer_id` has NDV = 5 × 10⁶, so expected rows = `10⁹ / 5 × 10⁶ = 200`)

**(i) Cost of Index Plan (B-Tree):**

- Tree traversal: `3 seeks × 5 ms = 15 ms`
- Leaf entry: 1 entry (or 200 if non-unique)
- Table block lookup: 200 / 250 ≈ 1 block
- Read 1 block: `5 ms`
- Process 200 rows: `200 × 0.001 ms = 0.2 ms`

$$C_{idx} = 15 + 5 + 0.2 = 20.2\ \text{ms}$$

**(2.5 marks)**

**(ii) Cost of Full Table Scan:**

$$C_{FTS} = 4 \times 10^6 \times 5\ \text{ms} = 2 \times 10^7\ \text{ms} = 20{,}000\ \text{seconds} \approx 5.56\ \text{hours}$$

**(2.5 marks)**

**(iii) Recommendation:**

The optimizer should choose the **B-Tree index plan** with cost **20.2 ms** vs. **FTS at 20,000 seconds** — a speedup of:

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{20{,}000\ \text{s}}{0.0202\ \text{s}} \approx 10^6\times$$

The B-Tree wins by 6 orders of magnitude. **(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B Option):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Computing selectivity as `1/5×10⁶` (treating as unique) and getting 1 row, then forgetting the table-lookup block cost.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the index tree traversal cost (3 levels × seek time). The 15 ms for traversal is non-trivial.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Reporting FTS cost in **ms** instead of converting to **seconds** — unit inconsistency costs marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not stating the **recommendation explicitly** with the **speedup factor** at the end.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print This Before the Exam):**

- [ ] **Query optimization in DWs is cost-based**, not rule-based. The optimizer enumerates plans, estimates cost, picks the cheapest.
- [ ] **Selectivity** is the #1 driver of cost: low selectivity → index scan; high selectivity → full table scan.
- [ ] **B-Tree** = high cardinality, range queries, equality. **Bitmap** = low cardinality (≤ 20 distinct), multi-dim filtering. **Join Index** = pre-computed joins.
- [ ] **Star transformation** uses bitmaps to avoid the Cartesian product — the cornerstone of DW optimization.
- [ ] **Partition pruning** eliminates irrelevant partitions; **partition-wise joins** parallelize joins. Composite partitioning (Range + Hash) is the production default.
- [ ] **Range partitioning** is best for **time-series** data (the most common DW fact-table pattern).
- [ ] **Materialized views** trade storage for query speed. Use them for **repeated aggregations**.
- [ ] **Performance tuning has 4 axes**: Schema, Index, Physical, Query — always tune from the **bottom** up (cheaper to change).
- [ ] **DW threshold for index use** is **higher** than OLTP (~25% selectivity) because of high I/O cost.
- [ ] **Cardinality crossover** between Bitmap and B-Tree occurs at `k ≈ 8–10` distinct values.
- [ ] **Always state the layer** (access path, physical, logical) of any optimization in your answer — KTU examiners reward explicit categorization.
- [ ] **Bitmap size** = `k · |R| / 8` bytes; **B-Tree height** = `⌈log_F(|R|/F)⌉`; **Pruning ratio** = `N_scanned / N_total`.
- [ ] **Star transformation cost** = `O(|F| + Σ|D_i|)` vs. **naive cost** = `O(|F| · ∏|D_i|)` — a difference of **orders of magnitude**.
- [ ] **Avoid** `SELECT *` in DW queries — use **columnar storage** benefits.
- [ ] **Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD Type 2)** preserve history; this directly affects index and partition design.
- [ ] **Clustering Factor** of an index: closer to `|R|` = good for range scans; closer to `N_blocks` = bad (random order).
- [ ] **Production DW systems** (Teradata, Exadata, Snowflake, Redshift, BigQuery) all implement these same principles — knowing the **vendor-neutral concept** is what KTU tests.
- [ ] **ETL loads** benefit from **partition exchange** (load into staging, then swap with empty production partition) — no downtime.
- [ ] **Buffer cache hit ratio** for OLAP should be **> 95%** — below this, your index strategy is wasting random I/O.
- [ ] **Amdahl's Law** applies to parallel DW queries: speedup is bounded by the serial fraction. Don't expect `N×` speedup with `N` partitions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Board Tip:** In any 14-mark question, structure your answer as **(a) Theory → (b) Numerical**. Theory gets 7 marks for **definition + diagram + 3 advantages + 2 disadvantages**. Numerical gets 7 marks for **3 clearly labeled steps with units in every line**. Memorize the 4 formulas: **Selectivity, Bitmap Size, Pruning Ratio, Star Cost**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OLAP Architectures: MOLAP, ROLAP, HOLAP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT412 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** OLAP Architectures: MOLAP, ROLAP, HOLAP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of OLAP Architectures

**Online Analytical Processing (OLAP)** is a category of software tools that enables analysts, managers, and executives to gain insight into data through fast, consistent, interactive access to a wide variety of possible views of information. OLAP architectures are the underlying storage and processing frameworks that enable multidimensional analysis of data warehouse contents.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus terminology, OLAP architectures are classified into three principal categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OLAP Architecture (KTU Syllabus Definition):** A logical and physical arrangement of data, metadata, indexes, and aggregation structures designed specifically to support multidimensional queries (slice, dice, drill-down, roll-up, pivot) over historical, consolidated data sourced from a Data Warehouse.

The three principal OLAP architectures are:

1. **MOLAP (Multidimensional OLAP)** – Uses a specialized multidimensional storage engine (cube) with pre-computed aggregates.
2. **ROLAP (Relational OLAP)** – Implements multidimensional views directly on top of a Relational Database Management System (RDBMS).
3. **HOLAP (Hybrid OLAP)** – Combines the strengths of both MOLAP and ROLAP by partitioning data intelligently between the two engines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Library System**
>
> Imagine you are a researcher who needs statistics about every book in a national library:
>
> - **ROLAP** is like asking the librarian to walk through the massive stacks every time you need a count, total, or average. He reads the catalogues (tables) on the fly, uses his intelligence (SQL), and gives you an accurate answer. It works for huge libraries but is **slow** for complex statistical questions.
>
> - **MOLAP** is like having a separate "Statistics Room" where every possible report (total books per genre, per year, per author) has already been pre-printed and arranged on shelves. You just walk in, pick up the report, and go. It is **blazingly fast**, but the room itself takes up enormous space, and not every conceivable report can be stored.
>
> - **HOLAP** is the **smart compromise**: high-level summary reports are pre-printed in the Statistics Room (MOLAP), while the raw detail is left in the main library (ROLAP). When you need a deep drill-down, the system fetches it from the library on demand.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terms

The following standardized metrics govern the design and evaluation of OLAP architectures (used in industry benchmarks and **KTU theory questions**):

- **Cube Cardinality**: The total number of cells in a fully materialized multidimensional cube.
- **Aggregation Factor (ρ)**: The ratio between the number of source fact tuples and the number of aggregated tuples.
- **Query Response Time (τ)**: The wall-clock latency between query submission and result display.
- **Storage Overhead (σ)**: The additional disk space required beyond the original fact table, expressed as a multiplicative factor.
- **Sparse Ratio (ψ)**: The fraction of cube cells that contain **no factual data** (NULL).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sparse Data** means most cells are empty; **Dense Data** means most cells contain values. This single property dictates whether MOLAP or ROLAP is the better fit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 MOLAP — Multidimensional OLAP

#### 2.1.1 Operational Logic

- **Storage Engine:** MOLAP uses a proprietary multidimensional array (often proprietary binary files, e.g., *Essbase*, *Microsoft Analysis Services* native format, *Apache Kylin* cuboids).
- **Data Restructuring:** Source fact and dimension tables are extracted, transformed, and **fully re-modeled** into n-dimensional hypercubes.
- **Pre-Aggregation:** All possible combinations of dimension members are pre-computed and stored at load time (Full Materialization).

#### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Working Principle

1. **Extract** cleaned data from the Data Warehouse staging area.
2. **Cube Build Engine** maps dimension hierarchies into array axes (e.g., Time × Product × Geography).
3. **Aggregate Navigator** computes SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX for every level of every hierarchy.
4. **Storage** writes the dense sub-cubes in optimized proprietary format.
5. **Query Time** is essentially a direct array lookup — no joins, no SQL parsing.

#### 2.1.3 Why and How It Works

- **Why pre-compute?** Because analytical queries are repetitive and predictable (e.g., "Sales by Quarter, Region, Product Category"). Caching the answers trades storage for speed.
- **How is query so fast?** It bypasses the relational engine entirely. The MDX/OLAP query is resolved by a coordinate-based lookup similar to array indexing.

### 2.2 ROLAP — Relational OLAP

#### 2.2.1 Operational Logic

- **Storage Engine:** The original relational star/snowflake schema is left intact in an RDBMS (Oracle, PostgreSQL, Teradata, etc.).
- **Aggregation Strategy:** Summaries are typically stored in materialized views, aggregate tables, or summary tables managed by the RDBMS.
- **Query Engine:** A middleware layer (the **ROLAP server**, e.g., *MicroStrategy Intelligence Server*, *Mondrian*) translates MDX/OLAP requests into optimized SQL.

#### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Working Principle

1. Client submits an OLAP query (e.g., pivot on Time × Product).
2. **RDBMS Optimizer** decides whether to hit the fact table directly or use a materialized aggregate.
3. **ROLAP middleware** rewrites the query into star-join SQL with `GROUP BY` and aggregate functions.
4. **Bitmapped indexes / B-tree indexes / star-join indexes** accelerate dimension filtering.
5. The RDBMS returns tuples which the middleware formats as a multidimensional result set.

#### 2.2.3 Why and How It Works

- **Why use the relational engine?** Because data volumes can reach tens of terabytes — too large to cube-build. ROLAP scales horizontally with disk and uses the RDBMS's mature query optimizer.
- **How is it dynamic?** There is no pre-built cube. The SQL is generated on-the-fly for *every* unique query combination.

### 2.3 HOLAP — Hybrid OLAP

#### 2.3.1 Operational Logic

- **Storage Strategy:** Splits the dataset into two tiers:
  - **MOLAP tier:** High-level summaries and frequently accessed aggregates.
  - **ROLAP tier:** Detailed atomic fact data.
- **Routing Logic:** A query router inspects the requested level of detail:
  - If the query can be satisfied by the MOLAP summary → answered from MOLAP.
  - If the query requires drill-down to atomic detail → routed to ROLAP.

#### 2.3.2 Step-by-Step Working Principle

1. Administrator defines a **partition threshold** (e.g., the top 20% of aggregation levels become MOLAP).
2. During ETL, the cube builder populates only those high-level cuboids.
3. Atomic data is loaded/refreshed in the ROLAP star schema.
4. At query time, the routing engine decides the optimal storage tier.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** and frequently asked in KTU 2024 ESE Module 4 questions. Master these for guaranteed marks.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables | Unit |
|---|---------|----------------------|-----------|------|
| 1 | Full Cube Cardinality | $C = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \mid D_i \mid$ | $D_i$ = cardinality of dimension $i$, $n$ = number of dimensions | cells |
| 2 | Total Aggregates (Lattice) | $A = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (\mid D_i \mid + 1) - 1$ | The "+1" includes the `ALL` level; "-1" excludes the empty tuple | aggregates |
| 3 | Storage Overhead (MOLAP) | $\sigma = \dfrac{S_{cube}}{S_{fact}}$ | $S_{cube}$ = cube size, $S_{fact}$ = fact table size | ratio |
| 4 | Aggregation Factor | $\rho = \dfrac{N_{fact}}{N_{agg}}$ | $N_{fact}$ = fact tuples, $N_{agg}$ = aggregated tuples | ratio |
| 5 | Query Response (MOLAP) | $\tau_{MOLAP} \approx t_{lookup} + t_{network}$ | Array coordinate lookup + network overhead | seconds |
| 6 | Query Response (ROLAP) | $\tau_{ROLAP} \approx t_{parse} + t_{optimize} + t_{join} + t_{agg}$ | Includes SQL parsing, join, aggregation | seconds |
| 7 | Sparse Ratio | $\psi = 1 - \dfrac{C_{populated}}{C}$ | $C_{populated}$ = non-NULL cells | fraction |
| 8 | MOLAP Suitability Index | $\text{MSI} = \dfrac{1}{\psi \cdot \sigma}$ | Higher is worse; MOLAP fails when MSI is small | dimensionless |
| 9 | Effective Density | $\delta = 1 - \psi$ | Fraction of cube containing data | fraction |
| 10 | Compression Ratio (RLE) | $\gamma = \dfrac{S_{orig}}{S_{compressed}}$ | Run-Length Encoding efficiency for sparse MOLAP | ratio |

> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> These formulas are used by data architects at firms like **Amazon Redshift**, **Google BigQuery**, and **Microsoft** to decide whether to deploy Analysis Services (MOLAP) vs. Power BI DirectQuery (ROLAP). They directly influence **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** of Business Intelligence platforms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cube Cardinality and Lattice of Cuboids

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A retail data warehouse has a fact table `SALES` with 3 dimensions: `TIME` (365 days), `PRODUCT` (1,000 SKUs), `STORE` (50 outlets). Compute (a) full cube size, (b) total aggregates including the `ALL` level, (c) effective density if only 10% of cells are populated.

**Given:**
- $\mid D_1 \mid = 365$ (TIME)
- $\mid D_2 \mid = 1000$ (PRODUCT)
- $\mid D_3 \mid = 50$ (STORE)
- $n = 3$
- Density $\delta = 0.10$

#### Step 1 — Full Cube Cardinality

The total number of cells in a fully materialized cube is the Cartesian product of all dimension cardinalities.

$$
C = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \mid D_i \mid
$$

$$
C = \mid D_1 \mid \times \mid D_2 \mid \times \mid D_3 \mid
$$

$$
C = 365 \times 1000 \times 50
$$

$$
C = 18{,}250{,}000 \text{ cells}
$$

This is **18.25 million cells**, even before adding the `ALL` level.

#### Step 2 — Total Aggregates in the Lattice

A cube lattice includes not just the base cuboid but all combinations of grouping levels. Each dimension contributes $(\mid D_i \mid + 1)$ levels (the "+1" accounts for the `ALL` level representing total aggregation).

$$
A = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (\mid D_i \mid + 1) - 1
$$

$$
A = (365 + 1) \times (1000 + 1) \times (50 + 1) - 1
$$

$$
A = 366 \times 1001 \times 51 - 1
$$

$$
A = 18{,}682{,}866 - 1 = 18{,}682{,}865 \text{ aggregates}
$$

Subtracting 1 excludes the empty tuple `(ALL, ALL, ALL)` which is the grand total but conventionally excluded.

#### Step 3 — Effective Density and Populated Cells

The sparse ratio is the fraction of cells with no data.

$$
\psi = 1 - \delta
$$

$$
\psi = 1 - 0.10 = 0.90
$$

Populated cells:

$$
C_{populated} = C \cdot \delta
$$

$$
C_{populated} = 18{,}250{,}000 \times 0.10 = 1{,}825{,}000
$$

Empty cells (sparse):

$$
C_{empty} = C \cdot \psi = 18{,}250{,}000 \times 0.90 = 16{,}425{,}000
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 90% sparse cube wastes enormous storage if fully materialized. This is precisely the failure mode that drove the industry from MOLAP toward **ROLAP** and eventually **HOLAP** in the 2000s.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Storage Overhead and Compression Strategy

**Given:**
- Each cell stores an 8-byte numeric measure (e.g., `sales_amount`).
- Fact table size $S_{fact} = 1$ GB.
- Compression via Run-Length Encoding gives $\gamma = 50$.

#### Step 1 — Uncompressed MOLAP Storage

$$
S_{cube}^{raw} = C \times B_{cell}
$$

$$
S_{cube}^{raw} = 18{,}250{,}000 \times 8 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
S_{cube}^{raw} = 146{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes}
$$

Converting:

$$
S_{cube}^{raw} = \dfrac{146{,}000{,}000}{1024^3} \approx 0.136 \text{ GB}
$$

#### Step 2 — Storage Overhead Ratio

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{S_{cube}^{raw}}{S_{fact}}
$$

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{0.136 \text{ GB}}{1 \text{ GB}} = 0.136
$$

Wait — this is *less* than the fact table! That is because we only stored the **base cuboid** (full granularity). When we include **all aggregates**, the picture changes:

$$
S_{cube}^{all} = A \times B_{cell}
$$

$$
S_{cube}^{all} = 18{,}682{,}865 \times 8 = 149{,}462{,}920 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\sigma_{all} = \dfrac{149{,}462{,}920}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824} \approx 0.139
$$

#### Step 3 — Apply Compression

For a sparse cube, RLE performs exceptionally well on the NULL regions.

$$
S_{cube}^{comp} = \dfrac{S_{cube}^{all}}{\gamma}
$$

$$
S_{cube}^{comp} = \dfrac{149{,}462{,}920}{50}
$$

$$
S_{cube}^{comp} = 2{,}989{,}258 \text{ bytes} \approx 2.85 \text{ MB}
$$

> **Interpretation:** After compression, the entire lattice of 18.6 million aggregates fits in **~2.85 MB**, while the original 1 GB fact table remains untouched. This is the engineering magic that kept MOLAP alive for sparse cubes.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board-Ready)

| Parameter | MOLAP | ROLAP | HOLAP |
|-----------|-------|-------|-------|
| **Storage Type** | Proprietary multidimensional array | Relational tables (star/snowflake) | Mixed (cube + relational) |
| **Query Speed** | **Very Fast** (constant-time lookup) | **Moderate** (depends on optimizer & indexes) | **Fast for summary, Moderate for detail** |
| **Storage Overhead** | **High** (all aggregates) | **Low** (only materialized views) | **Medium** (selective pre-aggregation) |
| **Data Freshness** | **Stale** (refresh cycle needed) | **Near Real-time** (live SQL) | **Configurable** |
| **Scalability** | **Poor** (cube explosion beyond ~50 GB) | **Excellent** (scales to PB) | **Very Good** |
| **Best Suited For** | Sparse, low-cardinality cubes with predictable queries | High-cardinality, frequently changing data | Mixed analytical workloads |
| **Indexing** | Array coordinates, sparse compression | Bitmap, B-tree, star-join indexes | Hybrid index strategy |
| **Query Language** | MDX, XMLA | SQL | MDX + SQL routing |
| **Maintenance Cost** | High (cube rebuild) | Low (incremental SQL) | Medium (dual pipeline) |
| **Industry Examples** | MS Analysis Services, Essbase, Kylin | Mondrian, MicroStrategy, Redshift | Microsoft SSAS Hybrid Mode, IBM Cognos Dynamic Cubes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architectural Overview — All Three OLAP Models

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientNode[OLAP CLIENT LAYER] --> routerNode{QUERY ROUTER}
    routerNode --> molapTierNode[MOLAP TIER]
    routerNode --> rolapTierNode[ROLAP TIER]
    routerNode --> holapTierNode[HOLAP TIER]
    molapTierNode --> molapCubeNode[MULTIDIMENSIONAL CUBE]
    molapCubeNode --> molapAggNode[PRE AGGREGATED SLICES]
    rolapTierNode --> rdbmsNode[RDBMS STAR SCHEMA]
    rdbmsNode --> factNode[FACT TABLE]
    rdbmsNode --> dimNode1[DIM TABLE TIME]
    rdbmsNode --> dimNode2[DIM TABLE PRODUCT]
    rdbmsNode --> dimNode3[DIM TABLE STORE]
    holapTierNode --> hybridPolicyNode[HYBRID STORAGE POLICY]
    hybridPolicyNode --> hybridSummaryNode[MOLAP SUMMARY TIER]
    hybridPolicyNode --> hybridDetailNode[ROLAP DETAIL TIER]
```

### 4.2 MOLAP Internal Cube Build Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dwNode[DATA WAREHOUSE] --> etlNode1[ETL PIPELINE]
    etlNode1 --> cubeBuilderNode[CUBE BUILDER ENGINE]
    cubeBuilderNode --> dimMapNode[DIMENSION MAPPING]
    cubeBuilderNode --> measureNode[MEASURE AGGREGATION]
    dimMapNode --> arrayNode[N DIMENSIONAL ARRAY]
    measureNode --> arrayNode
    arrayNode --> compressNode[RLE COMPRESSION]
    compressNode --> storageNode[PROPRIETARY CUBE FILE]
    storageNode --> queryNode[MDX QUERY RESOLVER]
    queryNode --> resultNode[MULTIDIMENSIONAL RESULT SET]
```

### 4.3 ROLAP Query Processing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientNode2[CLIENT MDX QUERY] --> middlewareNode[ROLAP MIDDLEWARE]
    middlewareNode --> translatorNode[SQL TRANSLATOR]
    translatorNode --> optimizerNode[RDBMS COST BASED OPTIMIZER]
    optimizerNode --> indexNode1[BITMAP INDEX ON DIM]
    optimizerNode --> indexNode2[B TREE INDEX ON FACT]
    optimizerNode --> indexNode3[STAR JOIN INDEX]
    optimizerNode --> factScanNode[FACT TABLE SCAN OR INDEX SEEK]
    factScanNode --> joinNode[STAR JOIN EXECUTION]
    joinNode --> groupByNode[GROUP BY AND AGGREGATE]
    groupByNode --> resultSetNode[RELATIONAL RESULT TUPLES]
    resultSetNode --> formatterNode[MULTIDIMENSIONAL FORMATTER]
    formatterNode --> clientResultNode[CLIENT DISPLAY]
```

### 4.4 HOLAP Routing Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    queryInputNode[INCOMING OLAP QUERY] --> levelCheckNode{QUERY LEVEL OF DETAIL}
    levelCheckNode -->|HIGH LEVEL SUMMARY| molapLookupNode[FETCH FROM MOLAP CACHE]
    levelCheckNode -->|ATOMIC DETAIL| rolapLookupNode[EXECUTE SQL ON ROLAP]
    levelCheckNode -->|MIXED HYBRID| hybridResolveNode[PARTIAL MOLAP PLUS DRILL DOWN TO ROLAP]
    molapLookupNode --> mergeResultNode[RESULT BUILDER]
    rolapLookupNode --> mergeResultNode
    hybridResolveNode --> mergeResultNode
    mergeResultNode --> outputNode[UNIFIED MULTIDIMENSIONAL RESULT]
```

### 4.5 Storage Allocation Decision Matrix (KTU Diagram Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startNode[START ANALYSIS] --> cardCheckNode{CARDINALITY OF DIMENSIONS}
    cardCheckNode -->|LESS THAN 10 PER DIM| sparseCubeNode[USE MOLAP WITH RLE COMPRESSION]
    cardCheckNode -->|GREATER THAN 100 PER DIM| denseCubeNode[USE ROLAP WITH BITMAP INDEX]
    cardCheckNode -->|MIXED HIGH AND LOW| hybridChoiceNode[USE HOLAP WITH PARTITIONED STORAGE]
    sparseCubeNode --> endA[END]
    denseCubeNode --> endB[END]
    hybridChoiceNode --> endC[END]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1. Differentiate between ROLAP and MOLAP architectures in terms of storage mechanism and query performance. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | MOLAP | ROLAP |
|--------|-------|-------|
| **Storage** | Uses a proprietary multidimensional array (cube). Data is physically restructured into n-dimensional hypercubes. | Uses the relational star or snowflake schema of an RDBMS. No physical restructuring. |
| **Aggregates** | All possible aggregates are pre-computed at load time (full materialization). | Aggregates are computed dynamically via SQL `GROUP BY` or stored in materialized views. |
| **Query Performance** | Very fast; queries are essentially array coordinate lookups with constant-time complexity. | Slower; performance depends on the RDBMS optimizer, indexing strategy, and join complexity. |
| **Drawback** | Cube explosion, stale data, refresh latency. | High I/O for complex queries, requires well-tuned indexes. |

> **Examiner Tip:** A 3-mark answer must contain a **clear tabular comparison** plus one concluding sentence. Diagrams are bonus marks.

---

**Q2. What is HOLAP? State any two situations where HOLAP is preferred over pure MOLAP or ROLAP. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** HOLAP (Hybrid Online Analytical Processing) is an OLAP architecture that combines the high performance of MOLAP for summarized data with the scalability and detail-level accessibility of ROLAP for atomic data. It partitions the dataset so that frequently accessed summaries are stored in a multidimensional cube, while detailed records remain in the relational warehouse.

**Two situations where HOLAP is preferred:**

1. **Mixed query patterns:** When the user community runs both high-level dashboard queries (e.g., quarterly sales totals) and drill-down queries (e.g., individual transaction receipts). MOLAP handles the former; ROLAP handles the latter.

2. **Storage cost optimization for large warehouses:** When the full cube size (lattice of cuboids) would exceed storage budgets, but the top-level summaries are still needed at MOLAP speeds. HOLAP stores only the top $k$ aggregation levels as MOLAP, leaving the rest as ROLAP.

3. *(Optional third)* **Incremental refresh requirements:** When high-level summaries change less frequently than detailed data, HOLAP allows independent refresh policies for each tier.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 4 | ESE Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **MOLAP architecture** in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss its **advantages and limitations**. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A data warehouse has the following dimensions and cardinalities: `Region` (5), `Product` (100), `Time` (366 days). Compute the **(i) Full cube cardinality**, **(ii) Total number of aggregates in the lattice**, and **(iii) Total storage in MB if each cell stores 8 bytes, assuming 80% sparsity and Run-Length Encoding compression ratio of 20:1**. *(7 Marks)*

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**MOLAP Architecture — Detailed Explanation:**

MOLAP (Multidimensional OLAP) is the classical OLAP architecture that uses a specialized multidimensional storage engine. The defining characteristic is the use of an **n-dimensional hypercube** as the primary storage structure, in which dimensions are mapped to array axes and measures are stored at the intersections (cells).

**Working Mechanism:**

1. Data is extracted from the operational sources or data warehouse staging area via an ETL pipeline.
2. The MOLAP engine restructures the relational data into a multidimensional array. Each dimension has one or more hierarchies (e.g., Day → Month → Quarter → Year for Time).
3. The cube builder computes **all possible aggregates** (SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX) across every level of every hierarchy. This is called **full materialization**.
4. The pre-computed cube is stored in a proprietary compressed binary format.
5. At query time, MDX (Multidimensional Expressions) or XMLA queries are resolved by direct coordinate-based lookup — no joins, no SQL parsing.

**Advantages of MOLAP:**

- **Blazing query speed** — constant-time array lookups (typically sub-second).
- **Powerful analytical functions** — time intelligence, ranking, and forecasting are natively supported.
- **Compact storage** — sparse cubes compress exceptionally well (RLE, dictionary encoding).
- **Consistent performance** — query time is independent of data volume.

**Limitations of MOLAP:**

- **Cube explosion** — the lattice size grows multiplicatively with dimension cardinality.
- **Data latency** — full rebuilds can take hours; incremental updates are complex.
- **Limited scalability** — practical limit around 50–100 GB of cube data.
- **Vendor lock-in** — proprietary format hinders data portability.

*(Board diagram: ETL → Cube Builder → Compressed Cube → MDX Resolver → Client. Refer to Section 4.2.)*

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $\mid R \mid = 5$, $\mid P \mid = 100$, $\mid T \mid = 366$
- $n = 3$
- Sparsity $\psi = 0.80$, so density $\delta = 0.20$
- Compression ratio $\gamma = 20$
- Cell size $B_{cell} = 8$ bytes

**(i) Full Cube Cardinality:**

$$
C = \prod_{i=1}^{3} \mid D_i \mid = 5 \times 100 \times 366
$$

$$
C = 183{,}000 \text{ cells}
$$

**(ii) Total Aggregates in the Lattice:**

$$
A = \prod_{i=1}^{3} (\mid D_i \mid + 1) - 1
$$

$$
A = (5+1) \times (100+1) \times (366+1) - 1
$$

$$
A = 6 \times 101 \times 367 - 1
$$

$$
A = 222{,}402 - 1 = 222{,}401 \text{ aggregates}
$$

**(iii) Total Compressed Storage:**

Step 1 — Uncompressed size of the full lattice:

$$
S_{raw} = A \times B_{cell}
$$

$$
S_{raw} = 222{,}401 \times 8 \text{ bytes} = 1{,}779{,}208 \text{ bytes}
$$

Step 2 — Apply RLE compression (which is effective primarily on sparse NULL regions). For a cube with 80% sparsity, the effective compression ratio scales:

$$
S_{comp} = \dfrac{S_{raw}}{\gamma \cdot (1 + \psi)}
$$

$$
S_{comp} = \dfrac{1{,}779{,}208}{20 \times (1 + 0.80)} = \dfrac{1{,}779{,}208}{36}
$$

$$
S_{comp} \approx 49{,}422 \text{ bytes}
$$

Convert to MB:

$$
S_{comp}^{MB} = \dfrac{49{,}422}{1024^2} \approx 0.0471 \text{ MB}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $C = 183{,}000$ cells; $A = 222{,}401$ aggregates; $S_{comp} \approx 0.047 \text{ MB}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **MOLAP, ROLAP, and HOLAP** architectures across **any five parameters**. Explain the **query processing flow in ROLAP** with a block diagram. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** What are the **factors that influence the choice** between MOLAP and ROLAP for a given business scenario? Discuss **how HOLAP attempts to combine the strengths of both**. *(7 Marks)*

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Five-Parameter Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | MOLAP | ROLAP | HOLAP |
|-----------|-------|-------|-------|
| 1. Storage Structure | Proprietary multidimensional array (cube) | Relational tables (star/snowflake) | Cube (summaries) + Relational (details) |
| 2. Query Speed | Fastest (constant-time lookup) | Slowest (SQL joins + aggregation) | Fast (summary) / Moderate (detail) |
| 3. Storage Cost | High (full materialization) | Low (only atomic + materialized views) | Medium (selective aggregation) |
| 4. Data Freshness | Latency-bound (refresh cycles) | Real-time (live SQL queries) | Mixed |
| 5. Scalability | Limited (~50 GB practical) | Excellent (Petabyte scale) | Very Good |

**Query Processing Flow in ROLAP (Block Diagram in text form):**

```
[Client MDX Query]
        |
        v
[ROLAP Middleware - Query Translator]
        |
        v
[Generate Star-Join SQL with GROUP BY]
        |
        v
[RDBMS Cost-Based Optimizer]
        |
        +---> [Bitmap Index on Dimension Keys]
        |
        +---> [B-tree Index on Fact Foreign Keys]
        |
        +---> [Partition Pruning (if Star Schema)]
        |
        v
[Star Join Execution: Fact JOIN Dim1 JOIN Dim2 ...]
        |
        v
[Aggregation: SUM, AVG, COUNT, etc.]
        |
        v
[Result Set returned to Middleware]
        |
        v
[Formatted as Multidimensional Cube Slice]
        |
        v
[Client Display / Pivot Table]
```

*(For a drawn diagram, refer to Section 4.3 of these notes.)*

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Factors Influencing the MOLAP vs. ROLAP Choice:**

1. **Data Volume:** If the fact table is below ~50 GB, MOLAP is feasible. Beyond that, MOLAP suffers from cube explosion; ROLAP is mandated.

2. **Query Pattern Predictability:** MOLAP excels when queries are **predictable and repetitive** (e.g., dashboards refreshed on schedules). ROLAP is required for **ad-hoc, unpredictable** queries.

3. **Dimension Cardinality:** Low cardinalities (e.g., Region, Department) favor MOLAP. High cardinalities (e.g., Customer ID with 10 million distinct values) make MOLAP impractical.

4. **Sparsity (ψ):** High sparsity (ψ > 0.85) makes MOLAP efficient because of RLE compression. Low sparsity (dense cubes) makes MOLAP storage prohibitive.

5. **Data Refresh Latency Requirements:** If real-time or near-real-time data is essential (e.g., fraud detection), ROLAP is the only option. MOLAP requires batch rebuilds.

6. **Budget and Hardware:** MOLAP demands high I/O and storage subsystems; ROLAP leverages existing RDBMS infrastructure.

7. **User Skills:** ROLAP requires SQL-literate administrators; MOLAP requires cube-design specialists.

**How HOLAP Combines the Strengths:**

HOLAP operates on the **principle of selective pre-aggregation**:

- **From MOLAP**, it inherits **constant-time query performance** for high-level summary slices that drive executive dashboards.
- **From ROLAP**, it inherits **unlimited scalability** and **freshness** for atomic drill-down operations.
- The **storage partition policy** (which levels become MOLAP and which remain ROLAP) is the central design decision. Common policies include: *"Keep the top 3 aggregation levels as MOLAP; leave the rest as ROLAP"* or *"Keep aggregates where density > 0.5 as MOLAP; otherwise ROLAP."*

The result is a system where **80% of queries (the high-level ones) execute at MOLAP speed**, while the **20% that require detail are answered by ROLAP** without storing a massive cube.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the lattice count (A) with cube cardinality (C).** Students often write $A = \prod \mid D_i \mid$ instead of $A = \prod (\mid D_i \mid + 1) - 1$. This is a **3-mark deduction** in numerical problems.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the `+1` for the `ALL` level.** The `ALL` level represents the grand total. Without it, the lattice is incomplete.
>
> 3. **Confusing MOLAP indexing with relational indexing.** MOLAP uses **array coordinates**, not B-trees or bitmaps. Stating "MOLAP uses B-tree indexes" is a definitive wrong answer.
>
> 4. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark sub-questions.** KTU examiners explicitly award **1 to 2 marks for a labeled diagram**. Always include one.
>
> 5. **Writing MDX and SQL interchangeably.** MDX is for MOLAP/HOLAP; SQL is for ROLAP. Mixing them shows conceptual confusion.
>
> 6. **Treating HOLAP as merely "MOLAP + ROLAP stacked."** The defining feature of HOLAP is the **routing policy and partition threshold**. Explain this explicitly for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 | OLAP Architectures**

- ⭐ **MOLAP** = Multidimensional OLAP = proprietary cube + full pre-aggregation = fastest queries, biggest storage cost, worst scalability.
- ⭐ **ROLAP** = Relational OLAP = star schema + dynamic SQL = slowest queries, smallest storage, best scalability and freshness.
- ⭐ **HOLAP** = Hybrid OLAP = summary tier (MOLAP) + detail tier (ROLAP) + smart routing = balanced trade-off.
- ⭐ **Cube Cardinality:** $C = \prod \mid D_i \mid$ — Cartesian product of dimension cardinalities.
- ⭐ **Lattice Size (All Aggregates):** $A = \prod (\mid D_i \mid + 1) - 1$ — includes the `ALL` level.
- ⭐ **Sparsity $\psi$** is the single most important property deciding MOLAP feasibility. High sparsity = MOLAP-friendly.
- ⭐ **Cube Explosion** is the MOLAP scaling bottleneck — the reason ROLAP and HOLAP exist.
- ⭐ **ROLAP Query Flow:** MDX → SQL translator → Cost optimizer → Star join → Aggregation → Result.
- ⭐ **HOLAP Partition Policy** is a design-time decision (top-k levels vs. density-based).
- ⭐ **MDX** is the standard query language for MOLAP and HOLAP; **SQL** for ROLAP.
- ⭐ **Industry Examples to Memorize:**
  - MOLAP → Microsoft SSAS (Multidimensional Mode), Essbase, Apache Kylin
  - ROLAP → Mondrian, MicroStrategy, Amazon Redshift Spectrum
  - HOLAP → Microsoft SSAS (Hybrid Mode), IBM Cognos Dynamic Cubes, Oracle OLAP
- ⭐ **Compression Techniques for MOLAP:** RLE (Run-Length Encoding), Dictionary Encoding, Bitmap Indexing on sparse coordinates.
- ⭐ **KTU Hot Topic:** "Compare MOLAP, ROLAP, HOLAP" appears almost every December exam cycle — **memorize the comparison table in Section 3.3**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OLAP Operations: Drill-Down, Roll-Up, Slicing, Dicing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT412 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** OLAP Operations: Drill-Down, Roll-Up, Slicing, Dicing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**OLAP (Online Analytical Processing)** is a category of software tools and techniques that enables analysts, managers, and executives to gain insight into data through fast, consistent, interactive access to a wide variety of possible views of information. OLAP operations are a set of navigational and computational primitives that allow users to query and analyze multidimensional data stored in data cubes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Verbatim Expectation):**
> *OLAP operations are the interactive, multidimensional query operations performed on a data cube. The four foundational OLAP operations are **Roll-Up, Drill-Down, Slice, and Dice**, which together allow users to navigate, summarize, and filter the data cube along its various dimensions and hierarchies.*

The four primary OLAP operations are formally defined as:

| # | Operation | Formal Definition | Direction |
|---|-----------|-------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Roll-Up** | Aggregation of data by climbing up a concept hierarchy or by reducing the number of dimensions | Coarse / Summary |
| 2 | **Drill-Down** | Dis-aggregation of data by descending a concept hierarchy or by adding new dimensions | Fine / Detail |
| 3 | **Slice** | Selection of a single value (or sub-cube) of one dimension, producing a sub-cube with one fewer dimension | Filter (single dim) |
| 4 | **Dice** | Selection of ranges of values across **two or more** dimensions, producing a sub-cube | Filter (multi dim) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Google Maps of Business Data"**
> Imagine you are the **Regional Sales Manager of a multinational company** like Apple or Nestlé, sitting in front of your computer. You have a giant 3D spreadsheet of sales numbers.
>
> - **Roll-Up** is like **zooming out on Google Earth** — you stop looking at your specific store in Kochi and instead see the total sales for the whole of Kerala, then the whole of India, then the whole of Asia. You are **summarizing** — going from detail to summary.
> - **Drill-Down** is the **opposite** — you click on India, then zoom into Kerala, then into Kochi, then into a particular store. You are going from **summary to detail**, asking "but *why* was it this way?"
> - **Slice** is like taking a **knife and cutting a single thin sheet** out of a cube of cheese. You say: "Show me ONLY the year 2023." All the other dimensions (product, location) remain, but you've fixed one dimension to one value — that's a slice.
> - **Dice** is like **cutting a small dice-shaped block** out of the cheese. You say: "Show me the sales of *iPhones in Kochi during 2023*." You are filtering **two or more** dimensions simultaneously, producing a small, focused sub-cube.

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary (Syllabus-Highlighted Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know Terms for KTU Board Exam:**
>
> - **Data Cube**: A multidimensional array of values, with each cell representing a measure (e.g., sales) at the intersection of coordinates from multiple dimensions (e.g., Time, Product, Location).
> - **Dimension**: A structural attribute of the data cube along which data is organized (e.g., Time, Geography, Product). Dimensions have members organized into hierarchies.
> - **Measure**: A numerical quantity (e.g., `Total_Sales`, `Profit`) stored in the body of the cube.
> - **Hierarchy**: The organization of dimension members into levels of granularity. For example, *Time* hierarchy: Day → Month → Quarter → Year.
> - **MOLAP / ROLAP / HOLAP**: The three implementation models for OLAP (Multidimensional / Relational / Hybrid).
> - **Schema**: Star Schema / Snowflake Schema define how the cube's tables are arranged fact-table and dimensionally.

## 1.4 Standard Boundary Conditions & Granularity Levels

- **Minimum number of dimensions** for a meaningful cube: **3** (though 2D is degenerate, often shown as base table).
- **Roll-Up** climbs up the hierarchy in steps. For the Time dimension: `Day → Month → Quarter → Year → All`.
- **Drill-Down** descends in the reverse direction: `Year → Quarter → Month → Day`.
- **Slice** reduces dimensionality by exactly **1** dimension.
- **Dice** preserves the dimensionality of the cube but reduces the **range** of values across 2 or more dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 3-Dimensional Base Reference Cube

To explain all four operations consistently, we will use a canonical KTU textbook example. Consider a sales data cube with the following 3 dimensions and 1 measure:

- **Dimensions**:
  - `Location` ∈ {Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Kochi}
  - `Time` ∈ {Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4}
  - `Product` ∈ {Laptop, Mobile, Tablet}
- **Measure**: `Total_Sales` (in lakhs of ₹)

> **Cube Notation (Lattice):**
> A 3-D cube can be described as `C(Location, Time, Product, Sales)`. Each cell `c(i, j, k)` holds the sales value for that unique triplet. The total number of base cells (0-D cuboids) = 4 × 4 × 3 = **48**.

## 2.2 Operation 1 — Roll-Up (Aggregation / Consolidation)

**Definition (Rigorous):** Roll-Up, also called *consolidation* or *aggregation*, applies an aggregate function (typically `SUM`, `AVG`, `COUNT`, `MAX`, `MIN`) to climb up a concept hierarchy or to drop a dimension.

**Mechanism — Two Distinct Modes:**

| Mode | Trigger | Action | Example |
|------|---------|--------|---------|
| **Climb-up** | Time dimension has a hierarchy | Roll from Month → Quarter → Year | Daily → Monthly → Yearly sales |
| **Drop-dim** | Remove a dimension entirely | Collapse a 2D face into a 1D line | Drop Product → see only Location × Time |

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. Identify the dimension along which you want to aggregate.
2. If a hierarchy exists, select the **higher-level** node in the hierarchy.
3. Apply the **aggregation function** (most commonly `SUM`) across all members at the lower level that roll into the higher-level member.
4. The result is a new cuboid with coarser granularity.

**Formal Mathematical Formulation of Roll-Up (SUM aggregation):**

For dimension $D_i$ with hierarchy $D_i^{(0)} \rightarrow D_i^{(1)} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow D_i^{(k)}$, a roll-up from level $l$ to level $l+1$ computes:

$$
\text{RollUp}_{SUM}\bigl(c\bigl(i_1, \dots, i_l, \dots, i_n\bigr)\bigr) = \sum_{\forall\, j \in \text{children}(i_l)} c\bigl(i_1, \dots, j, \dots, i_n\bigr)
$$

where $j$ ranges over all child members of $i_l$ in the next-finer level.

## 2.3 Operation 2 — Drill-Down (Dis-Aggregation / Navigation Down)

**Definition (Rigorous):** Drill-Down is the **inverse** of Roll-Up. It navigates from coarser (less detailed) data to finer (more detailed) data, either by descending a hierarchy or by adding a new dimension.

**Mechanism — Two Distinct Modes:**

| Mode | Trigger | Action | Example |
|------|---------|--------|---------|
| **Descend** | Navigate down a hierarchy | Year → Quarter → Month → Day | 2023 → Q1-2023 → Jan-2023 |
| **Add-dim** | Add a new dimension | 2D cube → 3D cube | Add *Customer Age Group* dimension |

**Mathematical Inverse Relation:**

$$
\text{DrillDown}\bigl(R\bigr) = R \times \text{descendants}
$$

The drill-down operation is **information-preserving in theory** but **lossy in storage** when pre-computed aggregations are dropped. In ROLAP systems, drill-down re-issues the SQL query at the finer level; in MOLAP systems, it traverses the pre-aggregated lattice.

## 2.4 Operation 3 — Slice (Single-Dimension Filter)

**Definition (Rigorous):** A slice operation performs a **selection on one dimension** of the cube, fixing it to a single value (or a small set of values), thereby producing a sub-cube with **one fewer dimension**.

**Mathematical Notation:**

$$
\text{Slice}_{D_i = v}(C) = \{ c(d_1, \dots, d_n) \;\vert\; d_i = v \}
$$

**Important Distinction for KTU Exam:** A slice **does not** change the dimensionality of the conceptual model. It simply *restricts* one dimension. Visually, slicing a 3D cube along the Time dimension at `Time = Q1` produces a 2D sheet (Location × Product) for Q1 only.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write "Slice reduces the number of dimensions by 1 permanently." This is **wrong**. Slice *temporarily fixes* one dimension. The full cube still has all dimensions; the slice is a *view*.

## 2.5 Operation 4 — Dice (Multi-Dimension Filter)

**Definition (Rigorous):** A dice operation performs a **selection on two or more dimensions** simultaneously, producing a sub-cube. Unlike slice, dice preserves the dimensionality of the original cube but reduces the range of values in **multiple** dimensions.

**Mathematical Notation:**

$$
\text{Dice}_{D_{i_1} \in S_1, \dots, D_{i_k} \in S_k}(C) = \{ c(d_1, \dots, d_n) \;\vert\; d_{i_j} \in S_j \text{ for } j = 1, \dots, k \}
$$

**Example:** `Dice[Location ∈ {Mumbai, Chennai} AND Time ∈ {Q1, Q2} AND Product = Laptop]` produces a focused sub-cube containing 2 × 2 × 1 = **4 cells** instead of the full 48.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning | Used In |
|-------------------|---------|---------|
| $C(D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n, M)$ | Cube with $n$ dimensions and 1 measure $M$ | All |
| $D_i^{(l)}$ | Level $l$ of dimension $D_i$ | Roll-Up, Drill-Down |
| $\Sigma(\cdot)$ | Sum aggregation function | Roll-Up |
| $\text{RollUp}_{SUM}$ | Aggregation operator on a cell | Roll-Up |
| $\text{Slice}_{D_i = v}(C)$ | Restrict dimension $D_i$ to value $v$ | Slice |
| $\text{Dice}_{D_{i_1} \in S_1, \dots}(C)$ | Restrict multiple dimensions to sets $S_j$ | Dice |
| $N_{\text{cells}}$ | Total number of base cells: $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert D_i \vert$ | Cube size |
| $L$ | Number of hierarchy levels in a dimension | Roll-Up depth |
| $A_{\text{avg}}$ | Average aggregate function | Roll-Up |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always explicitly state the **aggregation function** used (SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX) when describing Roll-Up. Vague answers like "aggregating" will lose 1–2 marks.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

OLAP operations are the **analytical backbone** of:

1. **Banking & Financial Services** — Risk modeling, fraud detection (slice by transaction type, dice by region and time window).
2. **Retail & E-Commerce** — Amazon, Flipkart use OLAP cubes for product recommendation engines (drill-down from category to SKU).
3. **Healthcare Analytics** — Hospital management: roll-up mortality by country, drill-down by hospital.
4. **Telecommunications** — Call Detail Record (CDR) analysis for network optimization.
5. **Supply Chain & Logistics** — Roll-up inventory by region, drill-down by warehouse.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Complete Roll-Up Derivation

**Problem Statement (KTU Board Style):**
Consider a 3D data cube for sales with dimensions `Location` ∈ {Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Kochi}, `Time` ∈ {Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4}, and `Product` ∈ {Laptop, Mobile, Tablet}. The measure is `Total_Sales` (₹ in lakhs). Apply a **Roll-Up on the Time dimension** from `Quarter` to `Year`, using the SUM aggregation function. Show the resulting aggregated values.

**Step 1 — State the Initial Cuboid Structure**

The base cuboid contains all four quarters. Each cell holds a sales value. For simplicity, we use a representative subset of values shown in the table below (Laptop sales, all locations summed per quarter):

| Time Level | Sales (₹ lakhs) |
|------------|-----------------|
| Q1 | 120 |
| Q2 | 150 |
| Q3 | 135 |
| Q4 | 175 |

**Step 2 — Identify the Hierarchy Transition**

We are moving from level `Time^(0) = Quarter` to level `Time^(1) = Year`. There is only one Year member for 2024, so all four quarters roll up into a single "2024" cell.

**Step 3 — Apply the Roll-Up Aggregation Formula**

$$
\text{RollUp}_{SUM}\bigl(C_{2024}\bigr) = \sum_{q \in \{Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4\}} \text{Sales}(q)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Compute**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RollUp}_{SUM}\bigl(C_{2024}\bigr) &= \text{Sales}(Q1) + \text{Sales}(Q2) + \text{Sales}(Q3) + \text{Sales}(Q4) \\
&= 120 + 150 + 135 + 175 \\
&= 580 \text{ ₹ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Result**

The 4 quarterly cells of the base cuboid are collapsed into **one** yearly cell holding the value `580`. The number of cells along the Time dimension reduces from 4 to 1. If the Location and Product dimensions remain, the new cuboid still has `4 × 1 × 3 = 12` cells instead of `4 × 4 × 3 = 48`.

**Conversion Logic Note:** Each aggregation function `SUM` here assumes that the measure `Sales` is *additive*. For non-additive measures (e.g., average temperature, stock price), the Roll-Up would use `AVG` or another appropriate function.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Complete Drill-Down Derivation

**Problem Statement:**
Starting from the aggregated value computed in Worked Example 1 (₹580 lakhs for 2024), perform a **Drill-Down on Time** back to the Quarter level, assuming equal-weight uniform distribution is not required — the original quarterly values must be reconstructed. Use the **descend mode** of Drill-Down.

**Step 1 — State the Source Cuboid**

We have a single aggregated cell: `C(Any_Location, Year=2024, Any_Product) = 580`.

**Step 2 — Identify the Descend Operation**

Drill-Down from `Year` to `Quarter` requires that the system **decomposes** the aggregated value back into the four original quarter values. In a pure MOLAP system, the original cuboid is preserved; in ROLAP, the SQL query re-executes against the fact table.

**Step 3 — The Descend Operation**

$$
\text{DrillDown}\bigl(C_{2024}\bigr) = \bigl\{C_{Q1}, C_{Q2}, C_{Q3}, C_{Q4}\bigr\}
$$

**Step 4 — Reconstruct Quarterly Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Q1} &= 120 \\
C_{Q2} &= 150 \\
C_{Q3} &= 135 \\
C_{Q4} &= 175
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify Conservation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Verification: } \sum_{q=1}^{4} C_{Qq} &= 120 + 150 + 135 + 175 = 580 \\
&\equiv C_{2024} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic Note:** Drill-Down is information-preserving *only if* the original cuboid is stored. If the system pre-aggregated and discarded the quarterly values, drill-down becomes impossible — the information is **physically lost**. This is why KTU exam questions often ask about MOLAP vs. ROLAP trade-offs.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Slice Operation Derivation

**Problem Statement:**
Apply a **Slice** on the same 3D cube along the `Location` dimension with the value `Location = Mumbai`. How many cells remain, and what is the resulting dimensionality?

**Step 1 — State the Original Cube Size**

$$
N_{\text{cells}} = \vert \text{Location} \vert \times \vert \text{Time} \vert \times \vert \text{Product} \vert = 4 \times 4 \times 3 = 48
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Slice Operator**

The slice operator $\text{Slice}_{\text{Location} = \text{Mumbai}}(C)$ restricts the Location dimension:

$$
\text{Slice}_{\text{Location} = \text{Mumbai}}(C) = \{c(d_1, d_2, d_3) \;\vert\; d_1 = \text{Mumbai}\}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the New Cell Count**

$$
N_{\text{new}} = 1 \times 4 \times 3 = 12
$$

**Step 4 — State the New Dimensionality**

The resulting sub-cube is **2-dimensional** (Time × Product), holding 12 cells, all restricted to Mumbai.

**Step 5 — Reduction Ratio**

$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{48 - 12}{48} \times 100\% = 75\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Slice reduces the *effective* dimensionality for the user view, but the underlying cube still has 3 dimensions. This conceptual distinction is a **favourite KTU board question**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Dice Operation Derivation

**Problem Statement:**
Apply a **Dice** on the cube with the following multi-dimensional filter:
- `Location ∈ {Mumbai, Chennai}`
- `Time ∈ {Q1, Q2}`
- `Product = Laptop`

How many cells remain?

**Step 1 — Identify the Range for Each Dimension**

| Dimension | Selected Range | Cardinality |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|
| Location | {Mumbai, Chennai} | 2 |
| Time | {Q1, Q2} | 2 |
| Product | {Laptop} | 1 |

**Step 2 — Apply the Dice Operator**

$$
\text{Dice}_{\text{Loc} \in S_1, \text{Time} \in S_2, \text{Prod} = S_3}(C) = \{c \;\vert\; c.\text{loc} \in S_1, c.\text{time} \in S_2, c.\text{prod} = S_3\}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the New Cell Count**

$$
N_{\text{new}} = 2 \times 2 \times 1 = 4
$$

**Step 4 — Enumerate the Four Cells**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= c(\text{Mumbai}, Q1, \text{Laptop}) \\
C_2 &= c(\text{Mumbai}, Q2, \text{Laptop}) \\
C_3 &= c(\text{Chennai}, Q1, \text{Laptop}) \\
C_4 &= c(\text{Chennai}, Q2, \text{Laptop})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare to Slice**

Notice that a slice would fix *only* one dimension. Since we fixed **three** dimensions, this is a **dice**, not a slice. The cube's conceptual dimensionality remains 3, but the visible cells are restricted to 4.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing All Four Operations

| Property | Roll-Up | Drill-Down | Slice | Dice |
|----------|---------|------------|-------|------|
| **Direction** | Coarsening | Refining | Restrictive | Restrictive |
| **Dimensionality** | Same or reduced | Same or increased | Reduced by 1 (effective) | Same |
| **Aggregation?** | Yes (SUM, AVG, etc.) | No (pure navigation) | No | No |
| **Hierarchy** | Climbs up | Descends | Independent | Independent |
| **Number of dims filtered** | N/A (aggregates) | N/A (navigates) | Exactly 1 | 2 or more |
| **Resulting cells** | Fewer (coarser) | More (finer) | $\prod_{i \neq j} \vert D_i \vert$ | $\prod_{i \in F} \vert S_i \vert$ |
| **Inverse Operation** | Drill-Down | Roll-Up | No formal inverse | No formal inverse |
| **Common Use** | KPI dashboards | Root-cause analysis | Reporting | Targeted analysis |
