# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Differentiate between OLAP Roll-Up and Drill-Down operations with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):**

- **Roll-Up (1.5 marks):** Roll-Up is an OLAP operation that performs *aggregation* by climbing up a concept hierarchy or by reducing the number of dimensions. It summarizes data from finer granularity to coarser granularity. **Example:** Summing daily sales to monthly sales, then to yearly sales in the Time dimension. The aggregation function is typically `SUM`, `AVG`, or `COUNT`.

- **Drill-Down (1.5 marks):** Drill-Down is the *inverse* of Roll-Up. It navigates from coarser data to finer data by descending a concept hierarchy or by adding a new dimension. **Example:** Navigating from total yearly sales of 2024 (₹580 lakhs) down to the individual quarterly values (Q1=120, Q2=150, Q3=135, Q4=175). It is essentially a dis-aggregation or refinement operation that exposes more detail.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key:** Examiners award the 1.5 marks for each operation *only* if the student provides a clear example. A definition without an example caps the score at 1 mark per operation.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is the difference between OLAP Slice and Dice operations? Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):**

- **Slice (1.5 marks):** A slice operation performs a selection on **exactly one dimension** of a data cube, fixing it to a single value (or a sub-range). The result is a sub-cube with effectively one fewer dimension. **Example:** Slicing the 3D sales cube along the `Time` dimension at `Time = Q1 2024` produces a 2D sheet showing Location × Product sales for Q1 only.

- **Dice (1.5 marks):** A dice operation performs a selection on **two or more dimensions** simultaneously, producing a focused sub-cube that retains the original dimensionality but with restricted value ranges. **Example:** Dicing the cube with `Location ∈ {Mumbai, Chennai}`, `Time ∈ {Q1, Q2}`, and `Product = Laptop` yields 2 × 2 × 1 = 4 cells.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** Explain the four fundamental OLAP operations on a data cube with neat diagrams. State the roll-up and drill-down relationship mathematically. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 3D sales data cube with dimensions `Region ∈ {North, South, East, West}`, `Quarter ∈ {Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4}`, and `Product ∈ {Electronics, Clothing, Food}`. The measure is `Revenue` (in ₹ Crores). The base cuboid values for *Electronics* are tabulated below:

| Region \ Quarter | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
|------------------|----|----|----|----|
| North | 50 | 60 | 55 | 70 |
| South | 45 | 50 | 48 | 65 |
| East | 40 | 42 | 45 | 60 |
| West | 55 | 58 | 52 | 68 |

Perform the following operations and show the resulting cuboids:
- (i) Roll-Up on Quarter → Year
- (ii) Drill-Down on Region (if Region were further subdivided into States)
- (iii) Slice on Region = South
- (iv) Dice on Region ∈ {North, South} AND Quarter ∈ {Q3, Q4} AND Product = Electronics **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks Solution:**

The four OLAP operations are:

1. **Roll-Up (1.75 marks):** Aggregation by climbing a hierarchy or reducing dimensions. Mathematical formulation:

$$
\text{RollUp}_{SUM}\bigl(c(d_1, \dots, d_n)\bigr) = \sum_{\forall\, j \in \text{children}(d_i)} c(d_1, \dots, j, \dots, d_n)
$$

2. **Drill-Down (1.75 marks):** Dis-aggregation; the inverse of Roll-Up. Mathematical formulation:

$$
\text{DrillDown}\bigl(c(d_1, \dots, d_i, \dots, d_n)\bigr) = \bigl\{c(d_1, \dots, k, \dots, d_n) \;\vert\; k \in \text{descendants}(d_i)\bigr\}
$$

3. **Slice (1 mark):** Selects on **one** dimension: $\text{Slice}_{D_i = v}(C)$.

4. **Dice (1 mark):** Selects on **two or more** dimensions: $\text{Dice}_{D_{i_1} \in S_1, \dots}(C)$.

5. **Roll-Up ↔ Drill-Down Inverse Relation (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{DrillDown}\bigl(\text{RollUp}_{SUM}(C)\bigr) = C \quad \text{(information-preserving case)}
$$

A neat **Mermaid / labelled diagram** (showing 3D cube and arrows for each operation) carries the remaining conceptual credit. *(Refer to Section 4 diagrams of these notes.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution:**

**(i) Roll-Up on Quarter → Year [2 marks]:**

Apply SUM aggregation across the four quarters for each (Region, Product=Electronics) pair:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Year}_{N} &= 50 + 60 + 55 + 70 = 235 \\
\text{Year}_{S} &= 45 + 50 + 48 + 65 = 208 \\
\text{Year}_{E} &= 40 + 42 + 45 + 60 = 187 \\
\text{Year}_{W} &= 55 + 58 + 52 + 68 = 233
\end{aligned}
$$

| Region \ Year | 2024 (₹ Cr) |
|---------------|-------------|
| North | 235 |
| South | 208 |
| East | 187 |
| West | 233 |

**(ii) Drill-Down on Region [1 mark]:** Region would expand from 4 entries to (say) `4 × 3 = 12 states` (e.g., North → Delhi, Punjab, Haryana). The number of cells increases from $4 \times 4 \times 3 = 48$ to $12 \times 4 \times 3 = 144$.

**(iii) Slice on Region = South [2 marks]:** Extracts a 2D sheet of `Quarter × Product` (4 × 3 = 12 cells) restricted to South:

| Quarter \ Product | Electronics | Clothing | Food |
|-------------------|-------------|----------|------|
| Q1 | 45 | … | … |
| Q2 | 50 | … | … |
| Q3 | 48 | … | … |
| Q4 | 65 | … | … |

**(iv) Dice on Region ∈ {North, South} AND Quarter ∈ {Q3, Q4} AND Product = Electronics [2 marks]:**

The resulting sub-cube contains $2 \times 2 \times 1 = 4$ cells:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_1 &= c(\text{North}, Q3, \text{Electronics}) = 55 \\
c_2 &= c(\text{North}, Q4, \text{Electronics}) = 70 \\
c_3 &= c(\text{South}, Q3, \text{Electronics}) = 48 \\
c_4 &= c(\text{South}, Q4, \text{Electronics}) = 65
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the OLAP architecture in detail. Compare MOLAP, ROLAP, and HOLAP with respect to storage, query speed, and the support for OLAP operations (Roll-Up, Drill-Down, Slice, Dice). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how the four OLAP operations can be sequenced to perform a complete business analysis. Illustrate a real-world retail scenario where a manager uses all four operations in one analytical session. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks Solution:**

**OLAP Architecture (3 marks):** A typical OLAP system has three tiers: (i) the **Data Warehouse tier** (relational fact + dimension tables), (ii) the **OLAP Server tier** (MOLAP/ROLAP/HOLAP engine holding the cube), and (iii) the **Client/Front-End tier** (analyst tools that issue MDX/SQL queries).

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property | MOLAP | ROLAP | HOLAP |
|----------|-------|-------|-------|
| **Storage** | Proprietary multidimensional array (sparse / dense) | Relational tables only | Hybrid: relational + selected MD cubes |
| **Query Speed** | Fastest (pre-computed) | Slowest (SQL joins) | Balanced |
| **Roll-Up** | Instant (pre-aggregated) | Computed on-the-fly via GROUP BY | Fast for pre-aggregated dims |
| **Drill-Down** | Instant (if base cuboid stored) | Trivial (re-issue SQL) | Depends on storage mode |
| **Slice / Dice** | Very fast (indexed arrays) | Slower (WHERE clause filtering) | Balanced |
| **Scalability** | Limited (cube size) | High (relational scalability) | High |
| **Example Tools** | Microsoft Analysis Services (MD mode), Essbase | Oracle BI, MicroStrategy | IBM Cognos, SAP BW |

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution:**

**Real-World Retail Scenario — "BigMart India Quarterly Performance Review":**

The Regional Manager wants to understand why Q4 sales in Mumbai underperformed.

**Step 1 — ROLL-UP (1.5 marks):** The manager starts at the highest level by rolling up sales from store → city → state → region → country. He sees that India's total Q4 revenue is ₹500 Cr, but Mumbai's contribution looks low. *Operation used: Roll-Up along Location hierarchy.*

**Step 2 — DRILL-DOWN (1.5 marks):** He drills down from Region → State (Maharashtra) → City (Mumbai) → Store (Andheri, Bandra, Thane). He notices that the Andheri store is the main underperformer. *Operation used: Drill-Down along Location hierarchy.*

**Step 3 — SLICE (2 marks):** He then slices the cube at `Time = Q4 2024` to focus only on the problematic quarter, eliminating Q1–Q3 noise. *Operation used: Slice on Time dimension.*

**Step 4 — DICE (2 marks):** Finally, he dices the cube with `Location ∈ {Andheri, Bandra, Thane}` AND `Product ∈ {Electronics, Clothing}` AND `Time = Q4 2024` to focus on the exact subset of failing combinations. He discovers that *Clothing sales in Andheri* are the root cause. *Operation used: Dice on 3 dimensions.*

**Resulting Action:** Inventory reallocation + targeted promotion for Andheri clothing.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Slice and Dice** — A *single-dimension* filter is a SLICE; a *multi-dimension* filter is a DICE. Examiners will deduct up to 2 marks for this confusion. Memorize: **S**lice = **S**ingle.
> 2. **Forgetting the Aggregation Function in Roll-Up** — A roll-up answer that omits SUM/AVG/COUNT loses 1.5 marks. Always state: *"Roll-Up is performed using the SUM aggregation function..."*
> 3. **Saying Slice reduces dimensionality permanently** — It does not. The underlying cube still has all dimensions. The slice is a *view*, not a structural change.
> 4. **Not drawing diagrams** — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly awards marks for "neat diagrams." For every long answer, a labelled Mermaid/text diagram of the cube and the operation arrow earns at least 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up Roll-Up and Drill-Down direction** — Roll-Up goes *up* the hierarchy (less detail). Drill-Down goes *down* (more detail). Many students reverse these and lose 7 marks in one go.
> 6. **Ignoring the inverse relationship** — When asked to "show the relationship between Roll-Up and Drill-Down," explicitly write the formula $\text{DrillDown}(\text{RollUp}(C)) = C$ for at least 1.5 marks of credit.
> 7. **Dice without specifying multiple dimensions** — Always list at least 2 dimensions in a dice example. Writing "dice filters the cube" is too vague.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — OLAP Operations (Drill-Down, Roll-Up, Slicing, Dicing)**
>
> - **Four Operations:** Roll-Up, Drill-Down, Slice, Dice — the analytical primitives of OLAP.
> - **Roll-Up = Aggregating Up** (e.g., Day → Month → Year), uses `SUM`, `AVG`, `COUNT`, `MIN`, `MAX`.
> - **Drill-Down = Dis-aggregating Down** (e.g., Year → Quarter → Month), the *inverse* of Roll-Up.
> - **Slice = One-Dimension Filter** (e.g., `Time = Q1`), reduces effective dimensionality by 1.
> - **Dice = Multi-Dimension Filter** (e.g., `Region ∈ {N, S} AND Time ∈ {Q1, Q2}`), preserves dimensionality.
> - **Hierarchy:** Roll-Up climbs; Drill-Down descends. The same dimension (e.g., Time) usually has levels: Day → Month → Quarter → Year.
> - **Total Cells Formula:** $N_{\text{cells}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert D_i \vert$.
> - **Inverse Relation:** $\text{DrillDown}(\text{RollUp}(C)) = C$ (information-preserving).
> - **MOLAP stores pre-aggregated cubes** → fast Roll-Up; **ROLAP computes on-the-fly** → slower but scalable.
> - **Slice is a view, not a structural change** — the cube's schema remains intact.
> - **Dice ≠ Slice** — Dice uses **two or more** dimensions; Slice uses **exactly one**.
> - **KTU Board Tip:** Always pair every operation with a numerical example and a labelled diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Warehouse Maintenance: Data Loading Automation, Backup and Recovery, Data Warehouse Evolution and Scalability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT412 - DATA WAREHOUSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Warehouse Maintenance: Data Loading Automation, Backup and Recovery, Data Warehouse Evolution and Scalability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Data Warehouse Maintenance — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Warehouse Maintenance** refers to the continuous, structured set of operational, administrative, and technical processes required to keep a Data Warehouse (DW) **accurate, available, performant, and aligned with evolving business requirements** throughout its entire lifecycle. As per KTU 2024 Module 4, it encompasses three primary pillars: **(1) Data Loading Automation, (2) Backup and Recovery, and (3) Evolution & Scalability.**

Unlike the transactional OLTP systems that focus on short, atomic operations, a Data Warehouse deals with **historical, voluminous, decision-support data**. Maintenance in a DW context is therefore a *proactive, scheduled, and policy-driven* discipline rather than a reactive one.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of DW Maintenance

| Pillar | Core Concern | Frequency | Risk if Ignored |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Loading Automation** | Consistent, repeatable, error-free population of the warehouse | Daily / Hourly / Real-time | Stale data, broken ETL, reporting failures |
| **Backup & Recovery** | Protection against data loss, corruption, and disasters | Daily / Weekly | Permanent data loss, regulatory non-compliance |
| **Evolution & Scalability** | Adapting to new requirements and growing data volumes | Quarterly / Annually | Performance degradation, business irrelevance |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library Re-Archival System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a massive **central library** that stores every newspaper, book, and record ever published (the *Data Warehouse*). 
> 
> - **Data Loading Automation** is the *automated delivery truck* that arrives every night at 2 AM, picks up new publications from various city branches (source systems), sorts them, catalogs them, and places them on the correct shelves — all without the librarian manually lifting a finger.
> 
> - **Backup and Recovery** is the *off-site vault* where microfilm copies of every book are stored. If the main library catches fire (disaster), the vault allows full reconstruction.
> 
> - **Evolution & Scalability** is the *library expansion plan* — when 10 million new books arrive, the library doesn't panic; it builds a new wing, installs motorized shelves, and hires more cataloging robots. The system *grows* gracefully.

## 1.4 Why Maintenance is a *First-Class* Concern in DW

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **Kimball Lifecycle** (a foundational DW methodology), maintenance is **not** the final step — it is a **continuous cross-cutting concern** that runs in parallel with every other phase. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly elevates this to a full module because most real-world DW project failures (industry reports cite **60–70%**) occur in the maintenance phase, not the initial build.

## 1.5 Standard Maintenance Metrics (KTU-Recognized)

- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — average operational time between system crashes
- **Mean Time To Recover (MTTR)** — average time to restore service after a failure
- **Recovery Point Objective (RPO)** — maximum acceptable data loss measured in time
- **Recovery Time Objective (RTO)** — maximum acceptable downtime
- **Data Freshness / Latency** — delay between source commit and DW availability
- **ETL Throughput** — rows processed per second during load windows
- **Storage Growth Rate** — typically **20–40% per year** for active DWs

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Data Loading Automation

### 2.1.1 Conceptual Foundation

Data Loading Automation is the **orchestrated, scheduled, and unsupervised movement of data** from heterogeneous source systems into the Data Warehouse, encompassing extraction, transformation, and loading (ETL) along with validation, logging, and error-handling.

### 2.1.2 The ETL / ELT Distinction

| Aspect | ETL (Extract-Transform-Load) | ELT (Extract-Load-Transform) |
|---|---|---|
| Transformation Site | Staging server (before load) | Inside the DW engine (after load) |
| Best Suited For | Complex transformations, smaller DWs | Cloud MPP warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift) |
| Latency | Higher (batch) | Lower (can be near real-time) |
| Storage Cost | Lower | Higher (raw zone retained) |

### 2.1.3 Loading Strategies — Structured Logic Steps

1. **Full Load (Initial / Bulk)**
   - Truncates target table and reloads *every* record.
   - Used during initial DW setup or major schema changes.
   - Pros: Simple, consistent. Cons: Resource-heavy, long downtime.
   - Formula: $T_{full} \propto N_{rows} \times C_{transform}$

2. **Incremental Load (Delta Load)**
   - Loads only rows that are new or changed since the last successful load.
   - Identified using **change data capture (CDC)** mechanisms.
   - Pros: Fast, low resource use. Cons: Requires robust CDC, error-prone if CDC breaks.

3. **Near Real-Time / Streaming Load**
   - Uses message queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ) and stream processors.
   - Latency: **seconds to minutes**.

4. **Micro-Batch Load**
   - Hybrid approach — small batches every few minutes.
   - Balances throughput and latency.

### 2.1.4 Change Data Capture (CDC) — The Core of Automation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CDC** is the technical heart of incremental loading. Three recognized techniques:
> 
> 1. **Timestamps** — Source tables have a `last_modified` column; query `WHERE last_modified > last_run_timestamp`.
> 2. **Triggers** — Database triggers write changed rows into a CDC table.
> 3. **Log-Based CDC** — Reads the database transaction log (e.g., Oracle Redo Logs, PostgreSQL WAL, MySQL Binlog) — most reliable, zero source impact.

### 2.1.5 Automation Tools and Schedulers

- **Orchestrators:** Apache Airflow, Informatica PowerCenter, Talend, AWS Glue, Azure Data Factory
- **Schedulers:** Cron-based (Linux), Control-M, Tidal, Airflow DAGs
- **Monitoring:** Grafana, Prometheus, custom audit tables

> [!NOTE]
> A typical KTU-recommended automation flow: **Scheduler → Orchestrator → ETL Engine → Staging → Cleansing → Dimensional Load → Audit Log → Notification**.

---

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Backup and Recovery

### 2.2.1 Conceptual Foundation

Backup and Recovery is the **disciplined process of creating retrievable copies of DW data and metadata**, and the procedures to restore the system to a consistent state after data loss, corruption, or disaster.

### 2.2.2 Types of Backup — Structured Logic

1. **Full Backup** — Complete copy of entire DW.
   - Time: $T_{full} = D_{DW} / B_{throughput}$
   - Frequency: Weekly (typically)
   
2. **Incremental Backup** — Only data changed since the *last* backup of *any* type.
   - Smaller, faster.
   
3. **Differential Backup** — Only data changed since the *last full* backup.
   - Grows over time; restore needs full + latest differential.

4. **Snapshot Backup** — Point-in-time copy (storage-level or VM-level). Very fast.

5. **Log-Based Backup** — Back up transaction logs for point-in-time recovery (PITR).

### 2.2.3 Recovery Strategies

| Strategy | Description | RPO | RTO |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Restore from Tape/Disk** | Restore from last full + all subsequent logs | Minutes | Hours |
| **Snapshot Recovery** | Mount a previous snapshot | Seconds | Minutes |
| **Active-Passive Replication** | Standby DW receives replicated data | ~0 | Minutes |
| **Active-Active (Multi-Master)** | Both sites serve queries | 0 | ~0 |
| **Cloud Cross-Region DR** | Geo-redundant storage | Minutes | Hours |

### 2.2.4 The 3-2-1 Backup Rule (Industry Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3-2-1 Rule:** Maintain **3** copies of data, on **2** different media types, with **1** stored offsite. Modern extension: **3-2-1-1-0** — 3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite, 1 immutable/air-gapped, 0 errors on verification.

### 2.2.5 Backup Considerations Specific to Data Warehouses

- **Volume:** DWs are **10×–1000× larger** than OLTP systems; backup windows are tight.
- **Consistency:** Backups must be **globally consistent** (use `BEGIN BACKUP` / `END BACKUP` markers).
- **Metadata Backup:** Repository, catalog, ETL configurations must be backed up separately.
- **Test Restores:** **Untested backups do not exist** — schedule quarterly restore drills.

---

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Data Warehouse Evolution and Scalability

### 2.3.1 Conceptual Foundation

**Evolution** = changes in schema, data models, sources, or business rules over time. 
**Scalability** = the ability to handle increased load (data volume, query concurrency, user count) without proportional performance degradation.

### 2.3.2 Why DWs Must Evolve

- New source systems onboarded
- Business KPIs change (e.g., new revenue dimensions)
- Regulatory requirements (GDPR, India DPDP Act 2023)
- Mergers, acquisitions, organizational changes
- Technology refreshes

### 2.3.3 Types of Schema Evolution

1. **Additive Change** — Add a new column or dimension. Easiest; existing queries unaffected.
2. **Destructive Change** — Drop a column or dimension. Risky; breaks dependent reports.
3. **Semantic Change** — Same column, new meaning. Most dangerous; requires full audit.
4. **Restructuring** — Change grain, split/fact tables, alter hierarchies.

### 2.3.4 Scalability Dimensions

| Dimension | Metric | Common Solution |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Volume** | TB / PB growth | Partitioning, compression, archival |
| **Query Load** | Concurrent users | Materialized views, indexing, MPP |
| **Load Throughput** | Rows/hour | Parallel ETL, bulk loaders |
| **User Concurrency** | Active sessions | Workload management, query queues |
| **Geographical Scope** | Global users | Data federation, replication |

### 2.3.5 Scaling Strategies — Vertical vs. Horizontal

- **Vertical Scaling (Scale-Up):** Add CPU, RAM, disk to a single server. Simple but bounded.
- **Horizontal Scaling (Scale-Out):** Add more nodes. Foundation of modern MPP (Massively Parallel Processing) warehouses.
- **Elastic Scaling (Cloud):** Auto-scale compute and storage independently. Examples: Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift Serverless.

### 2.3.6 Data Warehouse Evolution Architecture (Layered View)

Modern DWs separate concerns to make evolution manageable:

1. **Raw / Landing Zone** — untouched source data, append-only
2. **Staging / Cleansed Zone** — validated, deduplicated
3. **Conformed / Integration Layer** — enterprise-wide consistent
4. **Presentation / Dimensional Layer** — star/snowflake schemas for BI
5. **Semantic Layer** — business-defined metrics, hierarchies

This separation allows **independent evolution** of each layer.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Full Backup Time | $T_{full} = \dfrac{D_{DW}}{B_{throughput}}$ | $D_{DW}$ = DW size (TB); $B_{throughput}$ = backup speed (TB/hr) |
| 2 | Total Backup Window | $T_{total} = T_{full} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{inc,i}$ | Sum of full + all incrementals |
| 3 | Storage Growth Projection | $S_{t} = S_{0} \times (1 + r)^{t}$ | $S_0$ = initial TB, $r$ = annual growth rate, $t$ = years |
| 4 | ETL Throughput | $E = \dfrac{N_{rows}}{T_{load}}$ | $N_{rows}$ = rows loaded; $T_{load}$ = time in seconds |
| 5 | Compression Ratio | $C_{r} = \dfrac{S_{uncompressed}}{S_{compressed}}$ | Higher is better; typical 3×–10× for columnar |
| 6 | Partitioning Gain (est.) | $G_{p} \approx 1 - \dfrac{1}{N_{partitions}}$ | For full-scan elimination |
| 7 | Data Freshness | $F = T_{now} - T_{last\_commit}$ | Lower is better |
| 8 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Expressed as percentage (e.g., 99.99%) |
| 9 | MTTR Reduction Goal | $MTTR_{new} = MTTR_{old} \times (1 - \alpha)$ | $\alpha$ = automation improvement factor |
| 10 | Scalability Index (Amdahl) | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}$ | $p$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = nodes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Table Syntax:** In all formulas above, the vertical bar symbol has been replaced with `\vert` / `frac` notation to maintain KTU 2024 markdown table integrity. Always do this in your own exam answers.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking & Finance:** DWs process **billions of transactions daily**; a 1-hour backup window is non-negotiable for regulatory reporting (RBI, SEBI).
- **E-Commerce:** Real-time loading of clickstream data via Kafka + Snowflake for personalization.
- **Healthcare:** HIPAA-compliant backups with 6-year retention; cross-region DR.
- **Telecom:** CDR (Call Detail Record) warehouses scale to **petabytes**; require automated archival to cold storage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Backup Window Calculation

### Problem Statement
A Data Warehouse has a current size of **$D_{DW} = 50 \text{ TB}$**. The backup infrastructure achieves a throughput of **$B_{throughput} = 5 \text{ TB/hr}$**. A full backup is taken weekly, and incremental backups are taken daily. The daily change rate (delta) is approximately **$\Delta = 2 \text{ TB}$**. Compute the **total weekly backup window** assuming 6 incremental backups between fulls.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify Variables**

We define:
- $D_{DW}$ = Size of Data Warehouse = $50 \text{ TB}$
- $B_{throughput}$ = Backup throughput = $5 \text{ TB/hr}$
- $\Delta$ = Daily incremental size = $2 \text{ TB}$
- $n$ = Number of incrementals = $6$

**Step 2 — Apply the Full Backup Time Formula**

$$T_{full} = \frac{D_{DW}}{B_{throughput}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values**

$$T_{full} = \frac{50 \text{ TB}}{5 \text{ TB/hr}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate**

$$T_{full} = 10 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — Incremental Backup Time (each)**

$$T_{inc} = \frac{\Delta}{B_{throughput}} = \frac{2 \text{ TB}}{5 \text{ TB/hr}} = 0.4 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 6 — Total Weekly Window Formula**

$$T_{total} = T_{full} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{inc,i}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute and Evaluate**

$$T_{total} = 10 + (6 \times 0.4)$$

$$T_{total} = 10 + 2.4$$

$$T_{total} = 12.4 \text{ hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 12.4-hour weekly window is **too long** for a mission-critical DW. Real-world solution: implement **parallel backup streams** (divide DW into 4 stripes, run 4 backup jobs in parallel), reducing wall-clock time to ~$12.4 / 4 = 3.1 \text{ hours}$. This is exactly the kind of optimization expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 5-Year Storage Growth Projection

### Problem Statement
A DW starts at **$S_0 = 20 \text{ TB}$** with an annual growth rate of **$r = 30\% = 0.30$**. Compute the projected size after **$t = 5 \text{ years}$** and recommend an archival strategy.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compound Growth Formula**

$$S_{t} = S_{0} \times (1 + r)^{t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Known Values**

$$S_{5} = 20 \times (1 + 0.30)^{5}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $(1.30)^{5}$**

$$(1.30)^{2} = 1.6900$$
$$(1.30)^{3} = 1.6900 \times 1.30 = 2.1970$$
$$(1.30)^{4} = 2.1970 \times 1.30 = 2.8561$$
$$(1.30)^{5} = 2.8561 \times 1.30 = 3.71293$$

**Step 4 — Final Multiplication**

$$S_{5} = 20 \times 3.71293 = 74.2586 \text{ TB}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

$$\text{Net growth} = 74.26 - 20 = 54.26 \text{ TB additional}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommendation:** Implement a **tiered storage strategy**:
> - **Hot Tier (0–12 months):** SSD-backed, frequently queried — ~30 TB
> - **Warm Tier (1–3 years):** HDD, compressed — ~25 TB
> - **Cold Tier (3+ years):** Object storage (S3 Glacier / Azure Archive) — ~19 TB
> This reduces active DW cost by approximately **40–60%**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Amdahl's Law for DW Scalability

### Problem Statement
A DW query workload has **$p = 0.80$** (80% parallelizable) and **$1-p = 0.20$** (sequential portion, e.g., coordinator, final aggregation). Compute the speedup with **$N = 8$** parallel nodes.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall Amdahl's Law**

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$S(8) = \frac{1}{(1-0.80) + \dfrac{0.80}{8}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the Denominator**

$$S(8) = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.10}$$

$$S(8) = \frac{1}{0.30}$$

$$S(8) = 3.33$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

With 8 nodes, the workload runs **3.33× faster** than a single node — not 8×, because the 20% sequential part is the bottleneck.

**Step 5 — Theoretical Maximum (Infinite Nodes)**

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1-p} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5\times$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even with **infinite** nodes, max speedup is **5×**. To exceed this, the sequential portion must be reduced — e.g., by pushing aggregations into the parallel engine or pre-computing via materialized views.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Maintenance Trade-offs

| Decision | Option A | Option B | Trade-off Axis |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Loading** | Full Load | Incremental Load | Simplicity vs. Speed |
| **Backup** | Disk-to-Disk | Disk-to-Tape | Speed vs. Cost |
| **Recovery** | Active-Passive | Active-Active | Cost vs. RPO/RTO |
| **Scaling** | Vertical | Horizontal | Simplicity vs. Limitlessness |
| **Evolution** | In-place change | Versioned schema | Speed vs. Safety |
| **CDC** | Timestamps | Log-Based | Source impact vs. Reliability |
| **Storage** | On-Premises | Cloud | Control vs. Elasticity |

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Maintenance Decision Framework

```
QUESTION: "Is your DW maintenance strategy sound?"
                          │
        ┌─────────────────┼─────────────────┐
        ▼                 ▼                 ▼
   Loading            Backup           Evolution
   Health?            Health?          Health?
        │                 │                 │
   ┌────┴────┐       ┌────┴────┐       ┌────┴────┐
   │         │       │         │       │         │
 CDC      Latency   RPO/RTO   3-2-1   Schema   Capacity
Working?  < SLA?    Met?      Rule?   Stable?  Sufficient?
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — End-to-End Data Loading Automation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Systems OLTP CRM ERP IoT] --> B[Scheduler Cron Airflow]
    B --> C[Orchestrator DAG Engine]
    C --> D[Extract Step CDC Log Based]
    D --> E[Staging Zone Raw Landing]
    E --> F[Transform Cleansing Dedup]
    F --> G[Validation Row Count Checks]
    G -->|Fail| H[Error Log Alert]
    G -->|Pass| I[Load into Dimensional Model]
    I --> J[Update Audit Metadata]
    J --> K[BI Tools Dashboards Reports]
    J --> L[Notification Email Dashboard]
```

**Interpretation:** This diagram maps the **complete automated data loading lifecycle** — from source extraction through dimensional load, with explicit error-handling and observability branches. KTU examiners often award marks for such end-to-end visualizations.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Backup and Recovery Architecture (3-2-1-1-0)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Production Data Warehouse] -->|Backup Job 1| B[Local Disk Copy]
    A -->|Backup Job 2| C[Offsite Disk Copy DR Site]
    A -->|Backup Job 3| D[Cloud Object Storage S3]
    A -->|Snapshot| E[Immutable WORM Storage]
    B --> F[Restore Drill Quarterly]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -->|Report| G[Compliance Audit]
```

**Interpretation:** Visualizes the **3-2-1-1-0 rule**: 3 copies (Local, Offsite, Cloud), 2+ media types (Disk, Object), 1 offsite (DR Site), 1 immutable (WORM), 0 errors (verified via drills).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — DW Evolution & Scalability Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1[Layer 1 Raw Landing]
        A1[Source A Data]
        A2[Source B Data]
        A3[Source C Data]
    end
    subgraph L2[Layer 2 Staging Cleansed]
        B1[Validated Records]
        B2[Deduplicated Records]
    end
    subgraph L3[Layer 3 Conformed]
        C1[Enterprise Dimensions]
        C2[Enterprise Facts]
    end
    subgraph L4[Layer 4 Presentation]
        D1[Star Schema Sales]
        D2[Star Schema Finance]
    end
    subgraph L5[Layer 5 Semantic]
        E1[Business Metrics KPIs]
    end
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> F[BI Self Service Users]
```

**Interpretation:** The **layered Medallion-style architecture** (Bronze → Silver → Gold → Platinum) — each layer evolves independently, providing **isolation of change** which is the core principle of DW evolution.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — DW Failure & Recovery Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Disaster Event Detected] --> B{Type of Failure?}
    B -->|Data Corruption| C[Restore from Latest Snapshot]
    B -->|Disk Failure| D[Failover to Mirror]
    B -->|Site Disaster| E[Activate DR Site]
    B -->|Application Error| F[Rollback to Last Good Version]
    C --> G[Validate Data Integrity]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -->|Pass| H[Resume Operations]
    G -->|Fail| I[Escalate to L2 Team]
```

**Interpretation:** Shows how **different failure types** trigger **different recovery strategies**, each with its own RTO/RPO profile — a high-yield topic for KTU answers.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Scalability Strategies Comparison Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Increased Demand Volume Users] --> B{Scaling Strategy}
    B -->|Vertical| C[Scale Up Add CPU RAM Disk]
    B -->|Horizontal| D[Scale Out Add Nodes]
    B -->|Elastic| E[Cloud Auto Scale On Demand]
    C --> F[Single Server Architecture]
    D --> G[MPP Cluster Architecture]
    E --> H[Serverless Cloud DW]
    F --> I[Limitation Hardware Bound]
    G --> J[Advantage Linear Scale]
    H --> K[Advantage Pay Per Use]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Data Warehouse Maintenance. List its three main components as per the KTU 2024 syllabus.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Data Warehouse Maintenance is the continuous set of operational, administrative, and technical processes required to keep a Data Warehouse accurate, available, performant, and aligned with evolving business requirements throughout its lifecycle.

The three main components are:
1. **Data Loading Automation** — Orchestrated, scheduled ETL/ELT processes with CDC.
2. **Backup and Recovery** — Strategies and procedures to protect against data loss and ensure restoration.
3. **Data Warehouse Evolution and Scalability** — Schema evolution, layered architecture, and capacity/scale management.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is Change Data Capture (CDC)? Name any two CDC techniques.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Change Data Capture (CDC) is a technique used to identify and capture only the data that has been added, modified, or deleted in source systems since the last successful load, enabling efficient **incremental loading** in a Data Warehouse.

Two CDC techniques:
1. **Timestamp-based CDC** — Uses a `last_modified` column in source tables to filter changed rows.
2. **Log-based CDC** — Reads database transaction logs (e.g., Oracle Redo Logs, MySQL Binlog) to capture changes without impacting source performance.

*(A third option: Trigger-based CDC — uses DB triggers to populate a CDC table.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style]**

#### **Q. (a)** Explain the three strategies of Data Loading Automation. Compare Full Load vs. Incremental Load in detail. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:**
Data Loading Automation refers to the orchestrated, scheduled, and unsupervised movement of data from source systems into the Data Warehouse. The three primary strategies are:

**1. Full Load (Bulk Load):**
- **Mechanism:** Truncates the target table and reloads *every* record from the source.
- **Use Case:** Initial DW population, major schema changes, periodic reconciliation.
- **Advantages:** Simple, ensures consistency, easy to validate.
- **Disadvantages:** Resource-intensive, long downtime, not scalable for large DWs.

**2. Incremental Load (Delta Load):**
- **Mechanism:** Loads only rows that are new or changed since the last successful load, identified via CDC.
- **Use Case:** Daily/hourly operational loads.
- **Advantages:** Fast, low resource use, minimal disruption.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires robust CDC; failure may cause data drift.

**3. Near Real-Time / Streaming Load:**
- **Mechanism:** Uses message queues (Kafka) and stream processors for continuous loading.
- **Use Case:** Real-time dashboards, fraud detection.
- **Advantages:** Lowest latency (seconds).
- **Disadvantages:** Complex infrastructure, higher cost.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Full Load | Incremental Load |
|---|---|---|
| Volume | Entire dataset | Only deltas |
| Time | Long | Short |
| Complexity | Low | Medium-High |
| Resource Use | High | Low |
| Error Impact | Self-correcting | Cumulative |
| Best For | Initial setup | Operational loads |

**Conclusion:** In modern DWs, a **hybrid approach** is common — full load periodically (weekly) + incremental loads daily.

---

#### **Q. (b)** Describe the various Backup strategies for a Data Warehouse. Explain the 3-2-1 backup rule with a suitable diagram. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:**
Backup strategies in a Data Warehouse ensure data protection, regulatory compliance, and business continuity. Given the **massive volume** of DW data, strategies must balance speed, cost, and recovery capability.

**Types of Backup:**

1. **Full Backup:** Complete copy of the entire DW. Slow but self-contained.
2. **Incremental Backup:** Only data changed since the *last* backup (any type). Fastest.
3. **Differential Backup:** Only data changed since the *last full* backup. Grows over time.
4. **Snapshot Backup:** Point-in-time copy using storage-level or VM-level mechanisms. Very fast.
5. **Log-Based Backup:** Captures transaction logs for Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR).

**The 3-2-1 Backup Rule:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3-2-1 Rule** is an industry-standard data protection strategy:
> - **3** — Maintain **three** copies of the data.
> - **2** — Store them on **two** different media types (e.g., disk + cloud).
> - **1** — Keep **one** copy **offsite** (different geographical location).
> 
> **Modern Extension (3-2-1-1-0):** +1 immutable/air-gapped copy, +0 verification errors.

**Diagram (refer to Mermaid Diagram 2 in Section 4):**
The architecture shows: Production DW → Local Disk + Offsite Disk + Cloud Object Storage + Immutable WORM, all subject to quarterly restore drills and compliance audit.

**DW-Specific Backup Considerations:**
- DWs are **10×–1000× larger** than OLTP; use **parallel backup streams**.
- Ensure **global consistency** (use `BEGIN BACKUP` / `END BACKUP` markers).
- Backup **metadata** (catalog, ETL configs) separately.
- **Test restores** quarterly — untested backups do not exist.

**Conclusion:** A well-designed backup strategy is the **insurance policy** of the Data Warehouse. Following 3-2-1-1-0 with verified drills is a hallmark of mature DW operations.

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q. (a)** What is Data Warehouse Evolution? Explain the different types of schema evolution with examples. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:**
Data Warehouse Evolution refers to the **planned, managed changes** in the DW's structure (schema, data models, business rules) to accommodate new business requirements, source systems, regulations, and technologies over time.

**Types of Schema Evolution:**

1. **Additive Change (Non-Breaking):**
   - Add a new column to a dimension or fact table.
   - Add a new dimension or fact table entirely.
   - **Example:** Adding `customer_loyalty_tier` column to the `Dim_Customer` table.
   - **Impact:** Existing queries continue to work unchanged.

2. **Destructive Change (Breaking):**
   - Drop a column, dimension, or fact table.
   - **Example:** Removing the `fax_number` column from `Dim_Customer`.
   - **Impact:** Breaks dependent reports and ETL jobs; requires full audit and migration.

3. **Semantic Change (Most Dangerous):**
   - The same column gets a new meaning or unit.
   - **Example:** `revenue` column changes from INR to USD; or from gross to net.
   - **Impact:** Silent data corruption in downstream reports; requires versioned semantics and a deprecation period.

4. **Restructuring Change:**
   - Change the grain of a fact table; split/merge dimensions; alter hierarchies.
   - **Example:** Splitting `Dim_Date` into `Dim_Date` and `Dim_Fiscal_Period`.
   - **Impact:** Requires full ETL re-design and historical data backfill.

**Best Practices for Evolution:**
- Use **versioned schemas** (`sales_v1`, `sales_v2`) during transitions.
- Maintain a **conformed layer** to insulate downstream consumers.
- Apply **dimensional modeling principles** (Kimball) to make evolution additive-friendly.
- Document all changes in a **metadata repository**.

**Conclusion:** A well-architected DW anticipates evolution. The **layered architecture** (Raw → Staging → Conformed → Presentation) is the primary defense against chaotic evolution.

#### **Q. (b)** Discuss the scalability challenges in a Data Warehouse. Compare Vertical Scaling, Horizontal Scaling, and Elastic Scaling with suitable examples. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:**
Scalability is the ability of a Data Warehouse to handle **increasing data volumes, query loads, and user concurrency** without proportional performance degradation. It is one of the most critical non-functional requirements.

**Scalability Challenges:**
- **Data Volume Explosion:** Modern DWs grow at 20–40% annually; can reach petabytes.
- **Query Complexity:** Analytical queries scan billions of rows; complex joins strain resources.
- **User Concurrency:** Hundreds/thousands of BI users running reports simultaneously.
- **Mixed Workloads:** Ad-hoc queries compete with scheduled reporting jobs.
- **Cost Pressure:** Scaling up linearly with traditional hardware is economically unsustainable.

**Comparison of Scaling Strategies:**

| Strategy | Mechanism | Example | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Vertical (Scale-Up)** | Add CPU, RAM, disk to a single server | Oracle Exadata, Teradata | Simple, no code change | Hardware ceiling, expensive at high end |
| **Horizontal (Scale-Out)** | Add more commodity nodes; MPP architecture | Greenplum, Teradata, Cloudera | Near-linear scale, commodity hardware | Complex query planning, data distribution |
| **Elastic (Cloud)** | Auto-provision compute/storage on demand | Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift Serverless | Pay-per-use, instant scale, zero ops | Vendor lock-in, data egress costs |

**Amdahl's Law Application:**
Speedup with $N$ nodes: $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}$

For a workload with 80% parallelizable portion, 8 nodes give **3.33×** speedup (not 8×), highlighting that the **sequential portion** is the real scalability ceiling.

**Modern Best Practice:**
Hybrid approach — use **cloud-native elastic scaling** for variable workloads, with **horizontal MPP** as the foundation. Implement **workload management** (WLM) to prioritize critical queries.

**Conclusion:** Scalability is not just a technical concern — it is a **business enabler**. The right scaling strategy depends on workload predictability, budget, and operational maturity.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls in DW Maintenance Answers:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing ETL with ELT** — Many students write them interchangeably. ETL transforms *before* loading; ELT transforms *after* loading (inside the DW engine). KTU examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Metadata Backup** — Backup answers that only mention *data* files lose 1–2 marks. Always mention that **ETL configurations, repository metadata, and audit logs** must also be backed up.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring the 3-2-1 Rule** — Backup questions worth 7 marks expect the 3-2-1 (or 3-2-1-1-0) rule *by name*. Writing only "keep multiple copies" is incomplete.
> 
> 4. **Mixing Evolution and Scalability** — These are *related but distinct*. Evolution = schema/structure changes. Scalability = capacity/performance growth. Examiners deduct marks when the two are conflated.
> 
> 5. **No Diagram in Long Answers** — KTU 2024 scheme awards 2–3 marks specifically for **neat, labeled diagrams** in 14-mark questions. A flow diagram or block diagram is **mandatory**.
> 
> 6. **Skipping Recovery Time Objective (RTO) and Recovery Point Objective (RPO)** — Any backup/recovery answer must define these two terms explicitly.
> 
> 7. **Using vague terms like "faster" or "better"** — Always quantify: "speedup of 3.33×", "RPO of 5 minutes", "growth of 30% per year".
